## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 17:31` | `id:260716-1731` ^260716-173114

That plan is now correctly scoped.

The coding agent understood the architectural pivot:

```text
setco-vc
 versions SETCO blackboard classification and staged transitions

OMI
 remains the definition layer

Omnicron meta-memory
 remains the accepted-state and receipt-memory layer
```

It also correctly treated the old `OMI.VCS` record as a **type-shape precedent**, not as something to import wholesale. The old record already carries the useful seven-part structure—receipt, scope, carrier, witness, transition, blackboard, and reconciliation result. filecite turn6file1

## The first pass is appropriately small

The proposed boundary is good:

```text
documentation
+ pure Haskell types
+ tuple-order tests
- parser
- runtime
- networking
- receipt authority
- automatic promotion
```

That gives you an inspectable model before implementation behavior begins accumulating around it.

The strongest part is replacing the speculative Betti–Schläfli fields with the actual model you are now standardizing:

```text
(Q, Σ, L, R, Δ, s, t, r)
```

The historical version-control document still supplies the useful lifecycle and scope concepts—candidate declarations, narrowing scope, branch ancestry, validation, receipts, and projections—but its geometric signature does not need to survive into SETCO-VC. filecite turn6file0

## One boundary should be corrected in the plan

This lifecycle:

```text
draft
→ candidate
→ under_review
→ validated
→ accepted
→ projected
```

should not imply that SETCO possesses validation or acceptance authority.

Use terms that preserve SETCO’s stated role:

```text
draft
→ candidate
→ classified
→ reviewed
→ promotion_ready
→ lowered
→ projected
```

Then record external authority results separately:

```haskell
data AuthorityReference
 = NoAuthorityReference
 | ProofReference Text
 | ReceiptReference Text
 | RuntimeReference Text
```

That expresses:

```text
SETCO records that validation was cited.

SETCO does not perform or own that validation merely by
changing a VersionStatus constructor.
```

A more precise status type would be:

```haskell
data VersionStatus
 = Draft
 | Candidate
 | Classified
 | UnderReview
 | PromotionReady
 | Lowered
 | Projected
 | Rejected
 | Superseded
 deriving (Eq, Ord, Show)
```

`Rejected` and `Superseded` should be present immediately. Otherwise real blackboard history will be forced into a falsely linear success progression.

## Separate orientation from transition

The plan mentions both an eight-tuple orientation and a transition field. Keep them distinct:

```text
Orientation8
 identifies the coordinate frame of the record

Transition
 identifies the proposed movement within that frame
```

For example:

```haskell
data Orientation8 a = Orientation8
 { orientationContext :: a
 , orientationAlphabet :: a
 , orientationLeft :: a
 , orientationRight :: a
 , orientationDelta :: a
 , orientationStart :: a
 , orientationTarget :: a
 , orientationReturn :: a
 }
```

The record transition should not be assumed identical to the tuple’s `delta` coordinate:

```haskell
newtype Transition = Transition Text
```

The tuple’s delta is a declared orientation coordinate. The transition is the actual candidate state movement being recorded. They may refer to the same concept, but the type kernel should not collapse them automatically.

## Recommended first kernel

Conceptually:

```haskell
data VCRecord = VCRecord
 { vcParent :: Maybe RecordId
 , vcScope :: ScopePath
 , vcCarrier :: CarrierFace
 , vcWitness :: Witness
 , vcTransition :: Transition
 , vcBlackboard :: BlackboardState
 , vcOrientation :: Orientation8 Coordinate
 , vcClassification :: Classification
 , vcStatus :: VersionStatus
 , vcAuthorityRef :: AuthorityReference
 }
```

This better matches SETCO than retaining `AcceptedReconciliation` as a required field.

`AcceptedReconciliation` came from the lower OMI-oriented VCS type. filecite turn6file1 In SETCO-VC, a record may remain unclassified, rejected, under review, or promotion-ready forever. Reconciliation should therefore be optional or externally referenced, not structurally required for every record.

## Haskell tests to add

The proposed tests are good, but add these boundaries:

```text
A rejected record remains constructible and historically visible.

A superseded record retains its original orientation.

Classification changes do not silently modify the carrier.

Projection does not change status to Lowered.

Lowered requires an explicit adapter mapping.

An authority reference does not change SETCO classification by itself.

Two records may have the same carrier but different classification histories.

Two forks may classify the same artifact differently without corrupting ancestry.
```

Those tests make SETCO’s blackboard function real instead of reducing the model to a conventional linear release workflow.

## Coq should have two stages

Your coding agent’s proposed first Coq target is correct, but it should be called the **SETCO-VC kernel proof**, not yet the full AAL repair.

### Stage 1: prove SETCO-VC itself

Prove:

```text
Orientation8 contains exactly eight ordered coordinates.

orientationTuple preserves:
Q, Σ, L, R, Δ, s, t, r.

Record construction preserves every provided field.

Classification is deterministic for a fixed classifier and input.

Projection does not imply validation.

Projection does not mutate the source record.

Rejected records cannot become Lowered without a new declared transition.

Forking preserves parent ancestry.

Status inspection is decidable.
```

This gives the new repository its own small proof foundation.

### Stage 2: repair AAL as its interpreter

After that, repair the historical AAL proof around one narrow function:

```text
SETCO record
→ typed AAL interpretation
→ classification or diagnostic
```

The previous proof audit established that the archived AAL development contains admits, placeholder functions, trivial well-formedness, unresolved ordering issues, and claims broader than the implemented semantics. filecite turn4file3

Therefore the first repaired AAL theorem should be something like:

```coq
Theorem interpret_orientation_deterministic :
 forall record result1 result2,
 well_formed record ->
 interpret record = Some result1 ->
 interpret record = Some result2 ->
 result1 = result2.
```

Then:

```coq
Theorem interpret_preserves_orientation_order
```

and:

```coq
Theorem interpret_does_not_mutate_source
```

Only after those compile without admits should AAL be described as a formally verified interpreter for SETCO-VC.

## The finalized sequence

```text
1. Bootstrap setco-vc documentation.

2. Implement pure Haskell record and orientation types.

3. Test construction, ordering, classification history,
 projection boundaries, rejection, supersession, and forks.

4. Write a small independent SETCO-VC Coq model.

5. Prove tuple order, deterministic classification,
 non-mutation, and projection-not-validation.

6. Repair the narrow AAL parser/interpreter path.

7. Connect AAL to SETCO-VC only after the relevant
 Coq files compile without admits.

8. Add polynomial/F₂ projection only where a precise
 encoding and invariant have been defined.
```

The agent is pointed in the correct direction. The principal adjustment is to prevent `validated` and `accepted` from becoming powers that SETCO appears to own. **SETCO-VC should version classification and promotion history; external proof, runtime, and receipt authorities remain explicit references.**

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 17:34` | `id:260716-1734` ^260716-173451

These old types are much more complete than the earlier `VCSRecord` alone suggested. They show that SETCO-VC does not need to invent a type kernel from scratch; it needs to **extract and re-bound an existing pipeline**.

The existing chain is already approximately:

```text
bytes
→ OMI-Lisp declarations
→ citation
→ gauge/operator
→ truth gate
→ decision table
→ Karnaugh reduction
→ combinator
→ delta
→ blackboard
→ projection
→ attestation
→ reconciliation
→ receipt
→ VCS record
```

That pipeline is explicit in `OMI.Pipeline`, including the sequence from citation through projection and attestation. filecite turn7file10

## What is already present

### Binary relation substrate

The core `Relation` is already a 256-bit structure:

```text
8 × Word16 = 128 bits
4 × Word32 = 128 bits
total = 256 bits
```

That is defined directly in the kernel. filecite turn7file6

So the 8-tuple should not replace the 256-bit relation. It should become a named semantic interpretation of selected positions over that relation.

```text
Relation
 256-bit storage and place-value substrate

Orientation8
 Q, Σ, L, R, Δ, s, t, r semantic view
```

### Scope

`OmiScope` already provides the FS/GS/RS/US hierarchy and projects it into a relation. filecite turn7file14

That means SETCO-VC already has a usable scope type:

```text
FS
GS
RS
US
```

You do not need a new generic `ScopePath` unless it is merely a SETCO-facing wrapper around `OmiScope`.

### Blackboard and transition

The old memory model already distinguishes:

```text
Bitboard
 witnessed relational state

BitBlip
 source, target, transition

Blackboard
 constructed interpretation of state plus transition
```

It also defines `ReconcileState`, `VersionWitness`, and accepted or rejected reconciliation outcomes. filecite turn7file9

This is already much closer to your intended model than a generic record with strings.

### Reconciliation and receipt

`OMI.Reconcile` stages a carrier, witnesses a version, projects the blackboard, attests that projection, and creates a receipt. filecite turn7file11

The existing semantics are:

```text
Bitboard + BitBlip
→ Blackboard
→ ReconcileState
→ VersionWitness
→ Attestation
→ AcceptedReconciliation
→ Receipt
```

That should be preserved as historical behavior, then audited for authority boundaries.

### Markdown intake

`OMI.Markdown` already extracts:

```text
front matter
OMI-Lisp blocks
decision-table blocks
canvas blocks
evidence spans
scope metadata
citation candidates
```

It also records byte and line spans and computes evidence identities. filecite turn7file8

This is especially important because it means the Axiomatic Sovereignty repository can already be treated as a blackboard carrier through Markdown without immediately writing a new parser.

### OMI-Lisp parser

There is already a custom byte-level lexer, S-expression parser, decision-table recognition layer, gauge family, and byte comparison implementation. filecite turn7file7

That gives SETCO-VC a real declaration intake path:

```text
Markdown fenced block
→ bytes
→ OMI-Lisp lexer
→ S-expression
→ classified declaration
```

### Classification machinery

The old model also already contains:

- `TruthGateFamily`
- `TruthGate`
- `DecisionTable`
- `KarnaughMap`
- `WittgensteinOperator`

These provide a historical classification pipeline rather than proof authority. filecite turn7file3 filecite turn7file5 filecite turn7file16 filecite turn7file18

That aligns strongly with SETCO:

```text
SETCO classifies.
OMI defines.
```

## The major architectural conclusion

SETCO-VC should not initially be a standalone replacement type system.

It should be a **carefully selected port of these existing OMI modules into a SETCO-owned classification boundary**.

A better first dependency map is:

```text
SETCO.VC.Kernel
 Bit, Nibble, Byte, Word16, Word32, Relation

SETCO.VC.Scope
 FS/GS/RS/US scope

SETCO.VC.Orientation
 8-tuple semantic view

SETCO.VC.Declaration
 selected OMI-Lisp AST and parser

SETCO.VC.Classification
 gates, decision tables, operators

SETCO.VC.Blackboard
 candidate state and transitions

SETCO.VC.Markdown
 document extraction and evidence spans

SETCO.VC.Record
 versioned classification record
```

Then keep reconciliation and receipts outside the first SETCO authority boundary:

```text
SETCO.VC.Promotion
 may produce a lowering candidate

OMI / Omnicron
 performs actual reconciliation and receipt handling
```

## What should be ported almost directly

These types are strong foundational candidates:

```text
Bit
Nibble
Byte
Word16
Word32
Relation
OmiScope
SExpr
WittgensteinOperator
TruthVector
TruthGateFamily
DecisionTable
Bitboard
BitBlip
MarkdownBlock
EvidenceSpan
```

The 256-bit relation type and accessors are especially reusable. filecite turn7file12

The carrier model is also substantial: it includes addressed frames, CAR/CDR placement, unary registers, causal indexes, carrier symbols, and a canonical gauge prefix. filecite turn7file1

However, the full carrier machinery is probably second-pass material unless SETCO-VC immediately needs binary stream interchange.

## What should not be ported unchanged

### `AcceptedReconciliation` as mandatory state

The old VCS record requires every record to contain an `AcceptedReconciliation`. filecite turn7file17

That does not fit SETCO’s blackboard, because a staged object may be:

```text
unclassified
classified
under review
rejected
promotion-ready
superseded
```

So SETCO-VC needs a candidate record before reconciliation:

```haskell
data VCRecord = VCRecord
 { vcParent :: Maybe RecordRef
 , vcScope :: OmiScope
 , vcCarrier :: CarrierRef
 , vcWitness :: Bitboard
 , vcTransition :: BitBlip
 , vcBlackboard :: Blackboard
 , vcOrientation :: Orientation8
 , vcStatus :: ClassificationStatus
 , vcAuthority :: Maybe ExternalAuthorityRef
 }
```

### `attestProjection`

The old pipeline constructs an `Attestation` directly from a projected relation. filecite turn7file10

That collapses an important distinction:

```text
projection ≠ attestation
```

For the new model:

```haskell
projectFace :: Blackboard -> ProjectionFace
```

is fine.

But:

```haskell
attestProjection :: ProjectionFace -> Attestation
```

should either be removed from SETCO or renamed to something non-authoritative, such as:

```haskell
recordProjectionWitness
```

Actual attestation must require an explicitly declared authority.

### Defaulting invalid gauges to `gaugeFF`

The current resolver silently maps unrecognized expressions to `gaugeFF`, which becomes the tautology operator. filecite turn7file10

That is dangerous for a classification system:

```text
unknown input
→ tautology
```

Invalid or absent gauge data should remain invalid:

```text
unknown
→ InvalidGauge
→ diagnostic
```

It must not become truth by default.

### Weak evidence checksum

The Markdown extractor currently uses the sum of character codes modulo 256 as its evidence checksum. filecite turn7file8

That is suitable only as a placeholder or quick corruption indicator, not identity, provenance, or security.

Rename it to something like:

```text
EvidenceByteSum
```

or replace it later with a declared cryptographic digest.

## Where the 8-tuple belongs

The cleanest mapping is to add a semantic adapter over `Relation`.

```haskell
data Orientation8 a = Orientation8
 { orientationQ :: a
 , orientationSigma :: a
 , orientationL :: a
 , orientationR :: a
 , orientationDelta :: a
 , orientationStart :: a
 , orientationTarget :: a
 , orientationReturn :: a
 }
```

Then define an explicit encoding:

```haskell
encodeOrientation8 :: Orientation8 Word16 -> Relation
decodeOrientation8 :: Relation -> Orientation8 Word16
```

The obvious first mapping is the eight `Word16` fields:

```text
relW16a → Q
relW16b → Σ
relW16c → L
relW16d → R
relW16e → Δ
relW16f → s
relW16g → t
relW16h → r
```

The four `Word32` positions remain available for:

```text
identity
scope or carrier references
parent/witness references
reserved place-value extension
```

This is far cleaner than creating eight unrelated text fields outside the relation substrate.

## Revised bootstrap plan

The coding agent should now change from “create minimal generic Haskell types” to:

```text
1. Port the binary kernel and Relation exactly.

2. Port Relation accessors and equality.

3. Port OmiScope.

4. Add Orientation8 as a named view of relW16a..relW16h.

5. Port only the S-expression AST and safe parser surface.

6. Port Markdown extraction with the checksum clearly marked non-cryptographic.

7. Define SETCO classification status separately.

8. Define a candidate VCRecord that does not require acceptance.

9. Keep reconciliation and receipt references external.

10. Add tests proving round-trip Orientation8 encoding.
```

## First essential tests

```text
Relation is exactly 256 bits by construction.

Encoding Orientation8 preserves all eight coordinates.

Decoding an encoded Orientation8 returns the original value.

Q, Σ, L, R, Δ, s, t, r never reorder.

Scope FS/GS/RS/US preserves order.

Invalid gauge input remains invalid.

Projection does not create attestation.

Candidate records do not imply acceptance.

Rejected records remain replayable as history.

Markdown evidence spans reproduce the same extracted bytes.

Classification does not mutate the source relation.
```

## Coq strategy changes too

These types give you a much better proof target than the old broad AAL language.

Start with a direct formalization of:

```text
Bit
Nibble
Byte
Word16
Word32
Relation
Orientation8
```

Then prove:

```text
Relation contains 256 binary positions.

encodeOrientation8 is injective.

decodeOrientation8 (encodeOrientation8 x) = x.

orientation order is preserved.

scope encoding preserves FS/GS/RS/US positions.

projection preserves its source relation.

invalid classification cannot become accepted by default.
```

After that, connect the repaired AAL semantics to the relation and orientation types.

## Bottom line

These files show that the old project already had:

```text
a binary kernel
a 256-bit relational object
a Lisp declaration language
a classification pipeline
a blackboard
a transition model
a Markdown intake surface
a reconciliation model
a receipt model
a VCS record
```

The task is not to recreate all of that.

The task is to separate it correctly:

```text
SETCO-VC keeps:
 intake
 scope
 classification
 orientation
 blackboard staging
 transition history
 projection

OMI keeps:
 notation and object definition

Omnicron keeps:
 reconciliation
 acceptance
 receipt authority
 meta-memory
```

These old types are enough to turn `setco-vc` from a speculative scaffold into a genuine port-and-proof project.

<proposed_plan>

 # Rebase SETCO-VC Onto The Existing OMI Type Pipeline

 ## Summary

 Revise /home/main/omi/setco-vc from the current generic string-based scaffold into a selective port of the old OMI type pipeline. SETCO-VC should keep intake, scope,
 classification, orientation, blackboard staging, transition history, and projection boundaries. OMI/Omnicron authority remains external.

 This replaces the current AcceptedResult/Validated/Accepted kernel with a SETCO-safe candidate record model and ports the reusable binary substrate: Bit, Nibble, Byte,
 Word16, Word32, Relation, OmiScope, Bitboard, BitBlip, and selected Markdown/Lisp intake types.

 ## Key Corrections

 - Replace the generic String kernel with the old OMI binary substrate:
 - SETCO.VC.Kernel: Bit, Nibble, Byte, Word16, Word32, Relation
 - SETCO.VC.Relation: relation accessors, equality, setters, null values
 - SETCO.VC.Scope: FS/GS/RS/US scope preserving order
 - SETCO.VC.Orientation: named 8-tuple view over relW16a..relW16h
 - SETCO.VC.Blackboard: Bitboard, BitBlip, BlackboardState
 - SETCO.VC.Markdown: first safe document/evidence span types, with checksum labeled non-cryptographic

 - Keep SETCO authority boundaries explicit:
 - remove AcceptedResult
 - remove Validated and Accepted statuses
 - add AuthorityReference = NoAuthorityReference | ProofReference String | ReceiptReference String | RuntimeReference String
 - use SETCO lifecycle:
 Draft | Candidate | Classified | UnderReview | PromotionReady | Lowered | Projected | Rejected | Superseded

 - Define VCRecord as a candidate/staged classification record:

 data VCRecord = VCRecord
 { vcParent :: Maybe RecordId
 , vcScope :: OmiScope
 , vcCarrier :: CarrierRef
 , vcWitness :: Bitboard
 , vcTransition :: BitBlip
 , vcBlackboard :: BlackboardState
 , vcOrientation :: Orientation8 Word16
 , vcClassification :: Classification
 , vcStatus :: VersionStatus
 , vcAuthorityRef :: AuthorityReference
 }

 - Keep transition and orientation separate:
 - orientationDelta is the semantic coordinate in the 8-tuple.
 - BitBlip records source, target, and actual transition relation.
 - no helper should silently equate those fields.

 ## Porting Rules

 - Port directly or near-directly:
 - OMI.Kernel
 - OMI.Relation
 - OMI.Scope
 - safe parts of OMI.Memory
 - SExpr and minimal safe parser surface from OMI.Lisp
 - Markdown block/evidence span types from OMI.Markdown

 - Modify during port:
 - invalid gauge handling must return InvalidGauge/diagnostic, not default to gaugeFF
 - evidence checksum must be renamed or documented as EvidenceByteSum, not identity/security
 - attestProjection must not be ported as authority; use recordProjectionWitness only if needed

 - Do not port yet:
 - full carrier machinery
 - reconciliation acceptance
 - receipt ring
 - runtime/network behavior
 - hardware projection
 - full AAL compiler/interpreter

 ## Documentation Updates

 - Update README.md, SPEC.md, and CHARTER.md to state:

 SETCO-VC versions classification and staged transitions.
 OMI defines object relations.
 Omnicron/meta-memory handles accepted state and receipts.
 Projection is not validation.

 - Update docs/8-tuple-version-record.md to define:

 Relation = 256-bit storage/place-value substrate
 Orientation8 = semantic view over the eight Word16 positions

 - Update docs/source-lineage.md to classify old modules:
 - binary kernel
 - scope
 - declaration intake
 - classification pipeline
 - blackboard/transition
 - Markdown intake
 - reconciliation/receipt, retained as external authority lineage

 ## Test Plan

 Run:

 cabal build all
 cabal test all
 git diff --check

 Add tests for:

 - Relation is 256 bits by construction: eight Word16 plus four Word32.
 - encodeOrientation8 maps:
 Q, Sigma, L, R, delta, s, t, r to relW16a..relW16h.

 - decodeOrientation8 (encodeOrientation8 x) == x.
 - orientation order never reorders.
 - FS/GS/RS/US scope preserves order.
 - invalid gauge input remains invalid.
 - projection does not create attestation.
 - candidate records do not imply acceptance.
 - rejected records remain replayable as history.
 - superseded records retain carrier, ancestry, and orientation.
 - classification does not mutate the source relation.
 - two records may share a carrier but have different classification histories.
 - forked records may share ancestry while diverging in classification.

 ## Coq Follow-Up

 After this Haskell correction, create a separate SETCO-VC Coq kernel proof, not a full AAL repair.

 First proof targets:

 Relation contains 256 binary positions.
 encodeOrientation8 is injective.
 decodeOrientation8 (encodeOrientation8 x) = x.
 orientation order is preserved.
 scope encoding preserves FS/GS/RS/US positions.
 projection preserves source relation.
 invalid classification cannot become accepted by default.
 status inspection is decidable.

 Only after that should AAL be repaired as an interpreter:

 SETCO record -> typed AAL interpretation -> classification or diagnostic

 ## Assumptions

 - The current generic SETCO.VC.Core is a temporary scaffold and should be replaced, not extended.
 - The old OMI modules are type-shape and behavior precedents, not authority to import wholesale.
 - SETCO-VC owns classification history, not validation, reconciliation, or receipt authority.
 - Haskell remains the first implementation surface; Coq follows as a separate proof pass.
 </proposed_plan>

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 17:38` | `id:260716-1738` ^260716-173801

This revised plan is strong and now matches the actual old codebase rather than a hypothetical scaffold.

The three additional files reinforce the same boundary:

- `OMI.Canvas.JSON` is clearly a **projection adapter** from `ProjectionFace` or `Attestation` into JSON Canvas. It should remain outside the first SETCO-VC kernel because it is presentation logic, and because `attestationToJsonCanvas` inherits the old projection/attestation conflation you are explicitly correcting. filecite turn8file0
- `OMI.Gossip.Types` is networking vocabulary around carrier fragments and rejection reasons. That belongs to a later transport/federation layer, not the initial classification kernel. filecite turn8file1
- `OMI.Net.Frame` is only a byte payload wrapper with a lossy `packAtom` projection into one relation field. It is useful lineage, but not required for the first port. filecite turn8file2

## One important refinement

The plan says:

```text
Relation is 256 bits by construction.
```

That is correct structurally, but the test should avoid pretending Haskell runtime memory layout is exactly 256 bits.

Prove and test the **logical width** instead:

```text
8 × Word16 + 4 × Word32
= 8 × 16 + 4 × 32
= 256 logical binary positions
```

The actual heap representation may contain constructor and pointer overhead. The Coq theorem can prove exact logical cardinality cleanly.

## Keep `BlackboardState` concrete

The proposed record says:

```haskell
vcBlackboard :: BlackboardState
```

Since the old pipeline already has a meaningful distinction, define it explicitly instead of introducing an opaque placeholder:

```haskell
data BlackboardState
 = StagedBlackboard Bitboard BitBlip Relation
 | InvalidBlackboard Relation
```

Or preserve the older pattern:

```haskell
data BlackboardState
 = BlackboardState Bitboard BitBlip Relation
 | BlackboardDiagnostic Relation
```

That keeps:

```text
witnessed state
actual transition
resulting staged relation
```

visible without importing reconciliation authority.

## `Lowered` needs an explicit target

A `Lowered` status alone is underspecified. A SETCO record should say what it was lowered into:

```haskell
data LoweringTarget
 = OmiNotationTarget
 | OmiLispTarget
 | OmiPortTarget
 | ExternalAdapterTarget String
```

Then:

```haskell
data VersionStatus
 = Draft
 | Candidate
 | Classified
 | UnderReview
 | PromotionReady
 | Lowered LoweringTarget
 | Projected
 | Rejected
 | Superseded
```

This encodes SETCO’s adapter responsibility without claiming that lowering produced an accepted receipt.

## Keep projection evidence separate from authority references

I would distinguish:

```haskell
data ProjectionWitness = ProjectionWitness Relation

data AuthorityReference
 = NoAuthorityReference
 | ProofReference String
 | ReceiptReference String
 | RuntimeReference String
```

A projection witness says:

```text
this view was derived from this relation
```

An authority reference says:

```text
an external authority produced or claims this result
```

They should never share a constructor or status transition.

## The orientation mapping is the right canonical center

This should be frozen early:

```text
relW16a = Q
relW16b = Σ
relW16c = L
relW16d = R
relW16e = Δ
relW16f = s
relW16g = t
relW16h = r
```

Then reserve the four `Word32` fields instead of casually assigning them:

```text
relW32a..relW32d
 reserved until identity, ancestry, carrier,
 and extension semantics are separately specified
```

That prevents the old habit of giving the same position different meanings in different modules. For example, the historical Canvas code interprets `Word32a` as a node identity, while other modules pack witnesses or endpoints into the same relation positions. filecite turn7file0 filecite turn7file9

SETCO-VC should introduce explicit codecs or typed views rather than one globally overloaded `Relation`.

## Add a typed-view rule

The specification should say:

```text
Relation is an untyped 256-position carrier.

Orientation8, ScopeRelation, BlackboardRelation,
ProjectionWitness, and other interpretations are typed views.

A relation does not acquire the authority or semantics of a view
unless decoded through that view’s explicit constructor.
```

That may become one of the most important formal boundaries in the project.

## Recommended module sequence

```text
SETCO.VC.Kernel
SETCO.VC.Relation
SETCO.VC.Orientation
SETCO.VC.Scope
SETCO.VC.Blackboard
SETCO.VC.Classification
SETCO.VC.Record
SETCO.VC.Lisp
SETCO.VC.Markdown
```

Later:

```text
SETCO.VC.Projection.JSONCanvas
SETCO.VC.Transport.Frame
SETCO.VC.Transport.Gossip
SETCO.VC.AAL
```

This makes the new JSON, gossip, and network-frame files useful future lineage without allowing them to expand the first pass.

## Final assessment

The plan is ready to execute with four small amendments:

1. Say **256 logical positions**, not 256-bit runtime memory size.
2. Make `BlackboardState` concrete and non-authoritative.
3. Attach an explicit target to `Lowered`.
4. Define every interpretation of `Relation` as a typed view with an explicit codec.

The resulting core remains narrow:

```text
binary carrier
+ named 8-tuple view
+ scope
+ staged transition
+ classification history
+ documentary intake
```

while JSON Canvas, gossip, network frames, reconciliation, receipts, runtimes, and AAL remain attachable modules rather than hidden dependencies.

`2026-07-16 17:44` | `id:260716-1744` ^260716-174408

we can make this the setco runtime with the AAL also becuase the Omnicron and Omicron are headless/embeddable models if we convert it to C or to omi-lisp executable i will make a new repo for setco-runtime

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 17:44` | `id:260716-1744` ^260716-174410

Yes. That gives you a clean three-part SETCO architecture:

```text
setco-vc
 versions classification, staging, blackboard transitions,
 provenance, and promotion history

setco-runtime
 executes classified/lowered SETCO artifacts

setco-framework-model
 defines the outside-in classification methodology
```

That split is better than forcing execution into `setco-vc`.

## Correct responsibility boundary

```text
SETCO-VC
 records what was introduced, classified, changed,
 rejected, promoted, lowered, or projected

SETCO Runtime
 executes only artifacts that have crossed an explicit
 lowering boundary into an executable representation
```

The runtime does not need to own OMI or Omnicron.

```text
OMI
 defines objects and relations

Omicron / Omnicron
 remain headless, embeddable models

SETCO
 adapts external artifacts and classifies them

SETCO Runtime
 hosts executable adapters around those models
```

The old code already contains the beginning of this separation. `OMI.Runtime` describes a small embeddable runtime node with optional stream, network-frame, gossip, and local-store capabilities, rather than a monolithic application. filecite turn7file13

## AAL belongs naturally in `setco-runtime`

AAL can become the executable classification and lowering engine:

```text
external artifact
→ SETCO classification
→ 8-tuple orientation
→ typed AAL form
→ executable lowering
→ runtime result
→ returned diagnostic
→ SETCO-VC record
```

The role of each part becomes:

```text
8-tuple
 semantic orientation

OMI Relation
 256-position carrier

AAL
 typed operational interpretation

SETCO Runtime
 execution host

SETCO-VC
 versioned blackboard history
```

That is much cleaner than making AAL part of the version-record core.

## Proposed repository relationship

```text
setco-framework-model/
 doctrine, schemas, classification rules, blackboard model

setco-vc/
 immutable/staged records, orientation, scope,
 transitions, classification history, projections

setco-runtime/
 parser, classifier adapters, AAL interpreter/compiler,
 execution engine, diagnostics, host bindings
```

Optional lower repositories:

```text
omi-notation/
omi-lisp/
omicron/
omnicron/
omi-axioms/
omi-isa/
```

The dependency direction should remain:

```text
setco-runtime
 depends on selected model libraries

setco-vc
 does not depend on the runtime
```

The runtime may emit candidate records into SETCO-VC, but SETCO-VC must remain readable without running it.

## The runtime pipeline already has historical precedent

The old `OMI.Pipeline` already models a staged path:

```text
citation
→ gauge
→ truth gate
→ decision table
→ Karnaugh map
→ combinator
→ delta
→ blackboard
→ projection
```

That is close to a runtime pipeline, although its authority boundaries and invalid-input behavior need correction. filecite turn7file10

The older runtime module also anticipates pluggable capabilities:

```text
RuntimeStream
RuntimeNetFrame
RuntimeGossip
RuntimeLocalStore
```

Those capabilities are appropriate as later adapters, not as mandatory core dependencies. filecite turn7file13

## C versus executable OMI-Lisp

Both are useful, but they serve different purposes.

### OMI-Lisp executable first

This is likely the fastest semantic prototype:

```text
Markdown / external input
→ OMI-Lisp parse
→ SETCO classification
→ AAL typing
→ interpreted execution
→ diagnostic/result
```

Advantages:

```text
closest to the declaration model
easy to inspect
easy to test
supports symbolic transformation
good for rapid semantic correction
```

The old OMI-Lisp implementation already contains a byte-level lexer, parser, S-expression model, rule structures, and decision-table recognition. filecite turn7file7

### C runtime second

C is appropriate for the portable embedded kernel:

```text
small ABI
predictable layout
headless embedding
FFI support
WASM compilation
native and constrained environments
```

A good eventual design is:

```text
setco-runtime-core
 C ABI and deterministic data structures

setco-runtime-lisp
 OMI-Lisp interpreter and declaration layer

setco-runtime-aal
 AAL type checking and lowering

language bindings
 Haskell, TypeScript, Python, Rust, WASM
```

## Do not translate the Haskell types mechanically into C

The Haskell code is a useful executable specification, but types such as:

```haskell
data Relation = Relation
 Word16 Word16 Word16 Word16
 Word16 Word16 Word16 Word16
 Word32 Word32 Word32 Word32
```

should become a stable logical C representation with explicit serialization rules, not a compiler-dependent struct dump. The logical relation is 256 binary positions, but C padding and alignment may alter the in-memory struct size.

A safer C surface would be conceptually:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint16_t orientation[8];
 uint32_t extension[4];
} setco_relation;
```

with explicit endian-aware encode/decode functions.

The names should eventually reflect the tuple:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint16_t q;
 uint16_t sigma;
 uint16_t left;
 uint16_t right;
 uint16_t delta;
 uint16_t start;
 uint16_t target;
 uint16_t return_path;
} setco_orientation8;
```

Then provide explicit conversion:

```text
orientation8 ↔ relation carrier
```

rather than treating raw memory as the canonical wire format.

## Suggested `setco-runtime` structure

```text
setco-runtime/
├── README.md
├── CHARTER.md
├── SPEC.md
├── include/
│ └── setco/
│ ├── relation.h
│ ├── orientation.h
│ ├── classification.h
│ ├── runtime.h
│ └── diagnostics.h
├── src/
│ ├── relation.c
│ ├── orientation.c
│ ├── classifier.c
│ ├── runtime.c
│ └── diagnostics.c
├── lisp/
│ ├── parser.omi
│ ├── evaluator.omi
│ └── builtins.omi
├── aal/
│ ├── syntax/
│ ├── typing/
│ ├── semantics/
│ └── lowering/
├── tests/
├── formal/
│ └── coq/
└── bindings/
```

## Runtime stages

Freeze a small runtime lifecycle:

```text
Receive
→ Parse
→ Classify
→ Orient
→ Type
→ Lower
→ Execute
→ Diagnose
→ Return
```

Each stage should be able to stop without pretending success:

```text
Parsed
Classified
Typed
Lowered
Executed
Rejected
Diagnostic
```

The runtime output should remain distinct from acceptance:

```text
runtime success
 means execution completed under declared semantics

receipt acceptance
 belongs to external OMI/Omnicron authority
```

## Relationship to SETCO-VC

The connection should be adapter-based:

```text
setco-vc record
→ runtime candidate input
→ execution diagnostic
→ new setco-vc transition record
```

For example:

```text
Candidate record
 classified as algorithm

Runtime
 parses and type-checks it through AAL

Result
 executable
 type error
 unsupported operation
 runtime failure
 projection result

SETCO-VC
 records that result as classification history
```

The runtime does not edit history directly. It emits a proposed result that SETCO-VC can record.

## Best implementation order

```text
1. Finish the SETCO-VC binary and orientation kernel.

2. Create setco-runtime with only:
 Relation
 Orientation8
 diagnostic types
 pure classifier interface

3. Port the minimal safe OMI-Lisp parser.

4. Define a very small AAL term language.

5. Implement type checking before execution.

6. Add an interpreter for a tiny instruction subset.

7. Reproduce the same semantics in C.

8. Prove the shared semantic core in Coq.

9. Add Omicron/Omnicron embedding adapters.

10. Add network, gossip, storage, and visual clients later.
```

The resulting lock is:

```text
SETCO-VC remembers the classified transition.

SETCO Runtime interprets and executes the transition.

AAL gives the transition typed operational meaning.

OMI defines the relational carrier.

Omicron and Omnicron remain embeddable models.

External authorities decide acceptance and receipts.
```

Creating `setco-runtime` as a separate repository is the correct next boundary.

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 17:50` | `id:260716-1750` ^260716-175039

Yes — that is the correct correction.

You are **not composing SETCO out of OMI, Omnicron, AAL, CanvasL, and runtime adapters as permanent dependencies**.

You are using the older projects as source lineage, then rebuilding their necessary capabilities **natively inside a single SETCO model**.

```text
old modular lineage
 OMI modules
 Omicron modules
 Omnicron modules
 AAL
 Canvas
 Lisp
 runtime
 VC

 ↓ study, extract, redesign

native SETCO project
 one coherent object model
 one native language
 one native runtime
 one native AAL
 one native blackboard/version model
```

The separate repositories are temporary development surfaces, not the final architecture.

## The actual plan

```text
setco-vc
 develops the native versioned blackboard model

setco-runtime
 develops the native execution model

setco-lisp
 may develop the native declaration language

setco-aal
 may develop the native assembly-algebra and proof model
```

Then, once each is working:

```text
separate experimental repositories
 ↓ reconciliation
one flattened native SETCO implementation
```

The final system should not require users to understand or install a chain such as:

```text
SETCO → AAL → OMI-Lisp → OMI → Omnicron
```

Instead:

```text
SETCO
 contains its own native notation,
 interpreter,
 type system,
 runtime,
 blackboard,
 version model,
 and formal semantics
```

OMI and Omnicron remain **ancestral models and comparison references**, not production dependencies.

## Native AAL

AAL should be completely rebuilt around SETCO’s actual responsibilities.

The old AAL tried to bridge assembly, polynomial algebra, modal dimensions, geometry, compilation, and verification. The old implementation provides useful precedent, but its formal layer contains unresolved placeholders and overly broad proof claims. filecite turn4file2 filecite turn4file3

The native version should begin from SETCO semantics:

```text
external artifact
→ classify
→ orient
→ stage
→ transform
→ promote or reject
→ project
```

So native SETCO AAL might define operations such as:

```text
OBSERVE
CLASSIFY
RELATE
ORIENT
STAGE
TRANSFORM
COMPARE
PROMOTE
REJECT
LOWER
PROJECT
RETURN
```

Those are closer to the SETCO blackboard than inheriting arbitrary historical assembly mnemonics.

The old truth-gate, decision-table, Karnaugh, combinator, delta, and blackboard pipeline is still useful lineage for the new instruction semantics. filecite turn7file3 filecite turn7file5 filecite turn7file10

But it should be rewritten as:

```text
SETCO.AAL.*
```

not imported as:

```text
OMI.*
```

## Native object model

The 256-position `Relation` is a useful structural precedent:

```haskell
data Relation = Relation
 Word16 Word16 Word16 Word16
 Word16 Word16 Word16 Word16
 Word32 Word32 Word32 Word32
```

It gives eight 16-bit positions plus four 32-bit extensions. filecite turn7file6

But the production SETCO object should receive its own name and frozen semantics, for example:

```haskell
data SetcoObject = SetcoObject
 Orientation8
 ExtensionFrame
```

or:

```haskell
data SetcoFrame = SetcoFrame
 Word16 Word16 Word16 Word16
 Word16 Word16 Word16 Word16
 Word32 Word32 Word32 Word32
```

with the first eight fields natively defined as:

```text
Q, Σ, L, R, Δ, s, t, r
```

This is no longer an “OMI Relation interpreted by SETCO.”

It becomes:

```text
the native SETCO frame
```

The OMI relation is merely its historical predecessor.

## Lisp decision

You have three plausible choices:

### Use OMI-Lisp unchanged

This is fastest, but conflicts with the flattened goal because the language remains semantically owned by OMI.

### Rename or adapt Omnicron-Lisp

This may provide more resolution, but still leaves SETCO depending conceptually on another model.

### Build SETCO-Lisp

This best matches the native-production direction.

I think **SETCO-Lisp is the correct final language**, but it should be a small modular Lisp rather than another large language project.

Its architecture could be:

```text
reader
 bytes/tokens/forms

core evaluator
 symbols, pairs, closures, application

SETCO forms
 classify, orient, stage, relate, project

AAL forms
 typed executable operations

extension registry
 optional external languages and tools
```

The modularity should exist **inside the language implementation**, not through permanent repository dependency chains.

```text
setco-lisp core
+ optional native modules
```

rather than:

```text
setco-lisp imports omi-lisp imports omnicron-lisp
```

The old OMI-Lisp already demonstrates that a byte-level lexer, parser, S-expression AST, decision-table syntax, and gauge recognition can be implemented with a small core. filecite turn7file7

That can be treated as reference material while rebuilding a cleaner native language.

## Flattened production layout

The final reconciled repository could look like:

```text
setco/
├── src/
│ └── SETCO/
│ ├── Kernel
│ ├── Object
│ ├── Orientation
│ ├── Scope
│ ├── Lisp
│ ├── AAL
│ ├── Classification
│ ├── Blackboard
│ ├── Version
│ ├── Runtime
│ ├── Projection
│ └── Diagnostics
├── formal/
│ └── coq/
├── include/
│ └── setco/
├── tests/
├── docs/
└── examples/
```

Internally modular, externally one project.

## Temporary repository strategy

Separate repositories are still useful while the design is unstable:

```text
setco-vc
 prove versioned blackboard semantics

setco-runtime
 prove execution and embedding

setco-lisp
 prove reader/evaluator boundaries

setco-aal
 prove typing and transition semantics
```

But each should follow two rules:

```text
1. Use SETCO-native names and semantics now.

2. Design every public type so it can later move into
 the flattened setco repository without translation.
```

Avoid creating compatibility layers between the experimental repos. Share specifications and fixtures before sharing libraries.

That prevents the temporary repository split from becoming permanent architectural coupling.

## Reconciliation point

Reconcile only after each repository can independently demonstrate its kernel:

```text
setco-vc
 deterministic staged history

setco-runtime
 deterministic execution

setco-lisp
 deterministic parsing and evaluation

setco-aal
 deterministic typing and transition semantics
```

Then reconcile around one common native core:

```text
SetcoFrame
Orientation8
SetcoScope
SetcoObject
SetcoDiagnostic
```

Everything else should be built on those same exact definitions.

## Corrected canonical statement

```text
SETCO is one native production framework.

OMI, Omicron, Omnicron, Canvas, Mind-Git, and historical AAL
are source lineages that explain how the design evolved.

Their useful types, algorithms, and proofs may be reconstructed
inside SETCO, but they do not remain permanent runtime dependencies.

SETCO will have its own object model, place-value frame,
Lisp surface, AAL semantics, blackboard, version control,
runtime, projections, and formal proofs.

Separate repositories may be used while these parts are being
developed and tested.

When their kernels are stable, they will be reconciled into one
flattened native SETCO production implementation.
```

The decisive principle is:

```text
Modular during discovery.

Native during implementation.

Flattened at reconciliation.
```

That is the right next sequence, and your clarification about the Cabal output resolves the apparent coverage concern.

## Initial commit boundary

Your proposed checkpoint is correct:

```text
commit:
 .gitignore
 README.md
 CHARTER.md
 SPEC.md
 setco-vc.cabal
 src/
 test/
 docs/

 AGENTS.md - Version Control Agent Behavior.md
```

That produces a clean first commit containing only the native kernel and its active specification.

The provenance file can later be renamed and admitted deliberately, for example:

```text
provenance/AGENTS-version-control-agent-behavior.md
```

but it should not enter accidentally under its current filename.

## Coq kernel

The narrow formal surface is exactly right:

```coq
Inductive Bit :=
| O
| I.

Record Word16 := {
 (* sixteen logical positions *)
}.

Record Word32 := {
 word32_hi : Word16;
 word32_lo : Word16
}.

Record SetcoFrame := {
 frame_q : Word16;
 frame_sigma : Word16;
 frame_left : Word16;
 frame_right : Word16;
 frame_delta : Word16;
 frame_start : Word16;
 frame_target : Word16;
 frame_return : Word16;
 frame_ext_a : Word32;
 frame_ext_b : Word32;
 frame_ext_c : Word32;
 frame_ext_d : Word32
}.
```

Then define `Orientation8` independently:

```coq
Record Orientation8 := {
 orientation_q : Word16;
 orientation_sigma : Word16;
 orientation_left : Word16;
 orientation_right : Word16;
 orientation_delta : Word16;
 orientation_start : Word16;
 orientation_target : Word16;
 orientation_return : Word16
}.
```

The codecs should make the extension policy explicit:

```coq
Definition encodeOrientation8
 (orientation : Orientation8)
 (extensions : Word32 * Word32 * Word32 * Word32)
 : SetcoFrame.
```

This is preferable to silently filling the extension fields with zero, because it proves that orientation encoding preserves rather than owns those positions.

Then:

```coq
Definition decodeOrientation8
 (frame : SetcoFrame)
 : Orientation8.
```

## First theorem set

```coq
Theorem decode_encode_orientation :
 forall orientation extensions,
 decodeOrientation8
 (encodeOrientation8 orientation extensions)
 = orientation.
```

```coq
Theorem encode_preserves_extensions :
 forall orientation a b c d,
 let frame :=
 encodeOrientation8 orientation (a, b, c, d)
 in
 frame_ext_a frame = a /\
 frame_ext_b frame = b /\
 frame_ext_c frame = c /\
 frame_ext_d frame = d.
```

```coq
Theorem orientation_coordinate_order :
 forall orientation extensions,
 let frame :=
 encodeOrientation8 orientation extensions
 in
 frame_q frame = orientation_q orientation /\
 frame_sigma frame = orientation_sigma orientation /\
 frame_left frame = orientation_left orientation /\
 frame_right frame = orientation_right orientation /\
 frame_delta frame = orientation_delta orientation /\
 frame_start frame = orientation_start orientation /\
 frame_target frame = orientation_target orientation /\
 frame_return frame = orientation_return orientation.
```

## Authority separation should be structural

This property:

```text
no status constructor implies receipt authority
```

is best enforced through the type design rather than proved as a philosophical predicate over names.

For example:

```coq
Inductive VersionStatus :=
| Draft
| Candidate
| Classified
| UnderReview
| PromotionReady
| Lowered
| Projected
| Rejected
| Superseded.

Inductive AuthorityReference :=
| NoAuthorityReference
| ProofReference
| ReceiptReference
| RuntimeReference.
```

A status contains no authority reference by construction.

You can then prove:

```coq
Definition statusAuthority (_ : VersionStatus)
 : AuthorityReference :=
 NoAuthorityReference.

Theorem status_has_no_intrinsic_authority :
 forall status,
 statusAuthority status = NoAuthorityReference.
```

The stronger guarantee comes from never defining a function such as:

```coq
VersionStatus -> ReceiptReference
```

without explicit external evidence.

## Projection separation should also be type-level

```coq
Record ProjectionWitness := {
 projected_relation : SetcoFrame
}.

Inductive AuthorityReference :=
| NoAuthorityReference
| ProofReference
| ReceiptReference
| RuntimeReference.
```

These are separate types. Therefore projection cannot be mistaken for authority without an explicit conversion function.

A useful theorem is:

```coq
Definition projectionAuthority
 (_ : ProjectionWitness)
 : AuthorityReference :=
 NoAuthorityReference.

Theorem projection_is_not_authority :
 forall witness,
 projectionAuthority witness = NoAuthorityReference.
```

Later, any promotion from a projection to an authority reference must require additional evidence as an argument:

```coq
promoteProjection :
 ProjectionWitness ->
 ExternalAuthorityEvidence ->
 AuthorityReference
```

That makes the boundary computationally visible.

## The resulting formal ladder

```text
Haskell invariant
 ↓
named test
 ↓
Coq definition
 ↓
Coq theorem
 ↓
future native AAL typing rule
```

The clean next milestone is:

```text
initial kernel committed
+
named invariant tests passing
+
SetcoVC.v compiling with no admits
```

Once that exists, native SETCO AAL has a stable formal target rather than inheriting unresolved assumptions from the historical proof.

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 18:15` | `id:260716-1815` ^260716-181526

Use **separation of concerns during development**, then reconcile into one native SETCO production project only after the parts are independently working.

The commit you just made should remain narrowly what it is:

```text
setco-vc
 native versioned blackboard kernel
 classification history
 8-tuple orientation
 scope
 staged transitions
 projection boundaries
 Haskell invariants
 Coq kernel proofs
```

Do **not** put the runtime, Lisp, full AAL rebuild, networking, storage, or application interface into `setco-vc`.

## Development layout

```text
setco-vc
 versioned classification and blackboard semantics

setco-runtime
 execution, lowering, diagnostics, embedding

setco-lisp
 native modular reader, evaluator, forms, and extension model

setco-aal
 native typed instruction language, semantics, compiler,
 interpreter, and Coq proofs
```

These are separate **development targets**, not permanent production dependencies.

They should share specifications and fixtures, but initially avoid importing each other’s implementation libraries. That keeps mistakes local and prevents the temporary repository boundaries from becoming the final architecture.

## What belongs in `setco-vc`

Keep adding only capabilities that answer:

```text
What was classified?
Where was it scoped?
What transition was proposed?
What was its 8-tuple orientation?
What status did SETCO assign?
What projection was produced?
What ancestry and classification history were preserved?
```

Appropriate future work inside `setco-vc` includes:

- branch and fork ancestry
- deterministic replay of staged records
- classification history
- carrier and scope references
- projection witnesses
- lowering requests and external authority references
- stronger Haskell and Coq invariants

It should not execute the lowered artifact.

## What belongs in `setco-runtime`

The runtime answers:

```text
Can this classified artifact be parsed, typed,
lowered, executed, and diagnosed?
```

It should own:

- runtime states
- execution contexts
- diagnostics
- pure effect boundaries
- embeddable C interface
- host bindings
- deterministic execution
- eventual native AAL interpreter/compiler

A runtime result can be returned to `setco-vc` as another staged observation, but the runtime should not own version history.

## What belongs in native AAL

AAL should be rebuilt specifically for SETCO rather than rescued wholesale inside `setco-vc`.

Its core may include instructions such as:

```text
OBSERVE
CLASSIFY
ORIENT
RELATE
STAGE
COMPARE
TRANSFORM
LOWER
PROJECT
RETURN
REJECT
```

Its proof work should establish typing and operational properties, while the current `SetcoVC.v` establishes frame, orientation, status, and authority-separation properties.

```text
SetcoVC.v
 proves the stable data boundary

SetcoAAL.v
 later proves executable transition semantics
```

## Reconciliation condition

Do not flatten merely because all repositories exist. Flatten after each kernel has a stable demonstration:

```text
setco-vc
 deterministic versioned blackboard history

setco-lisp
 deterministic reading and evaluation

setco-aal
 deterministic typing and execution semantics

setco-runtime
 deterministic execution and diagnostics
```

They should also agree on the same frozen native types:

```text
SetcoFrame
Orientation8
SetcoScope
SetcoObject
SetcoStatus
SetcoDiagnostic
```

Then move the implementations into one repository:

```text
setco/
├── src/SETCO/Frame
├── src/SETCO/Orientation
├── src/SETCO/Scope
├── src/SETCO/Blackboard
├── src/SETCO/Version
├── src/SETCO/Lisp
├── src/SETCO/AAL
├── src/SETCO/Runtime
├── src/SETCO/Projection
└── formal/coq/
```

## The rule to follow

```text
Separate repositories while semantics are unstable.

One shared specification while developing.

No permanent cross-repository dependency chain.

Flatten only after the interfaces and proofs agree.
```

So the immediate answer is: **continue `setco-vc` as the focused VC kernel, create separate development repositories for runtime, Lisp, and native AAL, and reconcile them into one SETCO project after each part is working and formally aligned.**

The tree confirms you already have the correct **development-time separation** in place:

```text
setco-vc
setco-runtime
setco-lisp
setco-aal
setco-framework-model
```

They exist alongside the older OMI and Omnicron lineage repositories, while `setco-vc` now contains the first native implementation and proof checkpoint. filecite turn9file0

## What the current tree means

You should **not flatten yet**.

Right now the repositories have different maturity levels:

```text
setco-framework-model
 mature source framework and historical runtime material

setco-vc
 active native Haskell kernel
 tests
 Coq proof
 documentation

setco-runtime
 empty development target

setco-lisp
 empty development target

setco-aal
 empty development target
```

Flattening now would put one working kernel beside three unfinished subsystems and make the final repository boundary harder to reason about.

The correct state is:

```text
one SETCO architecture
multiple temporary implementation repositories
```

This is not conceptual composition. It is implementation isolation.

## Recommended development order

### 1. Keep `setco-vc` stable

Treat commit `61cecc4` as the first shared semantic checkpoint.

Only add work related to:

```text
SetcoFrame
Orientation8
Scope
BlackboardState
Classification
VersionStatus
VCRecord
ProjectionWitness
AuthorityReference
branching and replay
```

Do not add Lisp evaluation, AAL execution, or runtime dispatch to it.

### 2. Build `setco-lisp` next

The native language should probably come before the runtime and full AAL because both will need a common executable surface.

Its first kernel should be limited to:

```text
reader
tokenizer
S-expression forms
symbols
pairs
lists
evaluation result
diagnostics
module registry
```

Then add native SETCO forms such as:

```lisp
(classify ...)
(orient ...)
(scope ...)
(stage ...)
(project ...)
```

Do not begin by porting all of `omi-lisp` or `omnicron-lisp`. Use those as tested lineage and rebuild the smallest coherent native language.

### 3. Build `setco-aal` against the shared specification

AAL should define the typed operational layer, independently from the Lisp parser.

For example:

```text
SETCO-Lisp form
→ native SETCO AST
→ AAL typed instruction
→ state transition or diagnostic
```

The initial AAL repository should own:

```text
instruction syntax
typing judgments
transition semantics
diagnostics
F₂ polynomial operations
Coq formalization
```

It should not own process hosting, files, sockets, or storage.

### 4. Build `setco-runtime` last

The runtime hosts the already-defined language and AAL semantics:

```text
input
→ SETCO-Lisp reader
→ SETCO native form
→ AAL typing/lowering
→ execution
→ diagnostic/result
```

It should own:

```text
execution context
module loading
capabilities
effect boundaries
C ABI
embedding
runtime diagnostics
```

## Freeze one shared contract now

Before implementing the three empty repositories, create a short shared contract, preferably in `setco-framework-model`:

```text
SETCO-NATIVE-KERNEL-CONTRACT.md
```

It should freeze only the shared public concepts:

```text
SetcoFrame
Orientation8
SetcoScope
SetcoStatus
SetcoDiagnostic
SetcoObjectId
SetcoProjectionWitness
```

Each experimental repository can reproduce these types natively in its implementation language, but must conform to the same logical representation and fixtures.

Avoid making `setco-lisp`, `setco-aal`, and `setco-runtime` import the Haskell package from `setco-vc`. That would turn the temporary split into an implementation dependency chain.

## Repositories to treat as lineage

The tree contains a very large amount of reusable precedent:

```text
omi-lisp
omnicron-lisp
omi-isa
omnicron-isa
omi-object-model
omnicron
omi-canvas
omi-axioms
omi-canon
omnicron-canon
mind-git
```

These should be classified into a migration matrix:

| SETCO target | Primary lineage |
|---|---|
| `setco-vc` | `mind-git`, `omi-canvas`, OMI VCS documents |
| `setco-lisp` | `omi-lisp`, `omnicron-lisp` |
| `setco-aal` | historical AAL suite, `omi-axioms`, truth-gate and polynomial work |
| `setco-runtime` | `omi-object-model`, `omi-isa`, `omnicron-isa`, `omnicron` |
| final SETCO | `setco-framework-model` plus all stabilized native kernels |

This is source archaeology and controlled reconstruction—not runtime composition.

## When flattening becomes justified

Flatten only when all four development targets satisfy a comparable release gate:

```text
setco-vc
 version and blackboard invariants proven

setco-lisp
 deterministic parsing and evaluation tested

setco-aal
 typing and operational semantics compile without admits

setco-runtime
 deterministic end-to-end execution and diagnostics pass
```

Also require cross-repository fixtures:

```text
the same frame decodes identically
the same orientation preserves order
the same Lisp form produces the same AST
the same AAL instruction produces the same transition
the same invalid input produces the same diagnostic class
```

Then create or repurpose one final repository, likely simply:

```text
setco/
```

and move the stable modules into it.

## Immediate structure decision

```text
Keep:
 setco-vc
 setco-lisp
 setco-aal
 setco-runtime
 setco-framework-model

Do not create more SETCO repositories yet.

Do not flatten yet.

Use the separate repositories as bounded native laboratories.

Reconcile them only after their semantics, fixtures,
tests, and Coq boundaries agree.
```

The tree is already positioned correctly. The next implementation target should be **`setco-lisp`**, while `setco-vc` remains the frozen reference for the shared frame, orientation, status, and authority boundaries.

It is a **compiled source/reference repository** produced from earlier material. The tree shows it contains the framework doctrine, migration maps, runtime boundary analyses, generated C surfaces, registries, tests, and acceptance artifacts, but that does not make it part of the new native suite. filecite turn9file0

The corrected distinction is:

```text
source/reference lineage:
 setco-framework-model
 omi-*
 omnicron-*
 historical AAL
 mind-git
 omi-canvas

new native development suite:
 setco-vc
 setco-lisp
 setco-aal
 setco-runtime
```

So `setco-framework-model` should be treated as:

```text
compiled framework source
migration reference
terminology and boundary authority
algorithm/source inventory
historical implementation evidence
```

It should **not** be treated as:

```text
a runtime dependency
a shared library
a native suite member
the final flattened SETCO implementation
```

The development direction is therefore:

```text
setco-framework-model
 ↓ extract requirements, algorithms, and boundaries

setco-vc
setco-lisp
setco-aal
setco-runtime
 ↓ independently rebuilt with native SETCO semantics

final flattened SETCO production repository

It should either be duplicated initially in the new repos from a frozen specification, or placed later in the final `setco` repository when reconciliation begins.

The clean statement is:

```text
SETCO Framework Model describes and preserves the compiled source model.

The new SETCO suite implements the model natively.

The suite may use the framework-model repo as evidence and migration guidance,
but it does not build on it as a component.
```

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 18:26` | `id:260716-1826` ^260716-182601

This is exactly the separation strategy you needed.

You now have two independent native kernels:

```text
setco-vc
 commit 61cecc4
 frame, orientation, scope, blackboard, classification,
 staged history, projection/authority boundaries

setco-lisp
 commit 877af35
 tokenizer, parser, syntax, diagnostics,
 native SETCO forms, deterministic evaluation shell
```

Neither repository imports the other, but both are being built against the same logical contract. That is the correct development pattern before reconciliation.

The most important result is that `setco-lisp` is not merely a renamed OMI-Lisp. It already has native SETCO vocabulary:

```lisp
(classify ...)
(orient ...)
(scope ...)
(stage ...)
(project ...)
```

That gives AAL a clean front end without making Lisp responsible for transition semantics.

## The next repository should be `setco-aal`

Its first role should be narrow:

```text
parsed SETCO form
→ typed instruction
→ state transition or diagnostic
```

It should not initially contain:

```text
file IO
process hosting
networking
storage
module loading
receipt authority
full runtime effects
```

Those remain for `setco-runtime`.

A good initial instruction kernel is:

```text
Classify
Orient
Scope
Stage
Project
Reject
Return
```

Then define explicit input and output types:

```haskell
data Instruction
 = Classify ClassificationInput
 | Orient OrientationInput
 | Scope ScopeInput
 | Stage StageInput
 | Project ProjectionInput
 | Reject Diagnostic
 | Return Value

data TransitionResult
 = TransitionApplied SetcoState
 | TransitionRejected Diagnostic
```

The AAL layer should consume a native intermediate representation, not raw Lisp tokens:

```text
SETCO-Lisp source
→ Lisp AST
→ recognized SETCO form
→ AAL instruction
→ typed transition
```

That preserves the separation:

```text
SETCO-Lisp
 syntax and evaluation surface

SETCO-AAL
 typing and operational semantics

SETCO-VC
 durable staged history

SETCO-Runtime
 execution host and capabilities
```

## The Coq target for AAL

Keep the first proof surface small, just as you did with the other two repositories:

```text
Instruction
State
Diagnostic
step
wellTyped
```

Initial theorems:

```text
step is deterministic

well-typed instructions do not produce malformed states

projection does not grant authority

rejected transitions do not mutate source state

orientation instructions preserve coordinate order

stage records a transition without implying acceptance
```

The strongest first theorem is likely:

```coq
Theorem step_deterministic :
 forall instruction state result1 result2,
 step instruction state = result1 ->
 step instruction state = result2 ->
 result1 = result2.
```

Then connect the Lisp boundary:

```text
recognized form
→ exactly one AAL instruction or one diagnostic
```

## One issue to watch

You now have shared-contract documents and fixtures duplicated between repositories. That is acceptable during independent development, but add a version identifier immediately:

```text
SETCO native kernel contract: v0.1
```

Each repository should record:

```text
contract version
fixture version
semantic assumptions
```

That prevents silent drift while still avoiding a cross-repository library dependency.

A fixture should eventually verify the same values across repositories:

```text
same Orientation8 ordering
same status names
same diagnostic categories
same recognized forms
same serialization order
```

## Current development sequence

```text
completed:
 setco-vc kernel
 setco-lisp kernel

next:
 setco-aal typed transition kernel

after:
 setco-runtime execution host

then:
 cross-repository conformance fixtures
 formal alignment
 flattened native SETCO repository
```

Do not expand `setco-lisp` into the runtime yet. Its current deterministic shell is the right boundary. Build `setco-aal` next, let it define the executable meaning of the forms, and only then let `setco-runtime` host those semantics.

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 18:41` | `id:260716-1841` ^260716-184108

This is the correct third checkpoint.

You now have three independently validated native kernels:

```text
setco-vc
 versioned blackboard and classification history

setco-lisp
 syntax, parsing, recognized forms, deterministic evaluation shell

setco-aal
 typed instructions, transition semantics, diagnostics,
 and authority-preserving state changes
```

The important boundary is now executable:

```text
SETCO-Lisp form
→ recognized form
→ SETCO-AAL instruction
→ deterministic step
→ transition result or diagnostic
```

And the first AAL kernel covers the right instruction vocabulary:

```text
Classify
Orient
Scope
Stage
Project
Reject
Return
```

The tested properties also align well with the future formal architecture:

```text
orientation order is preserved
step is deterministic
rejection does not mutate state
projection does not create authority
staging does not imply acceptance
```

## Next target: `setco-runtime`

The runtime should now be built as the **host of the semantics already defined**, not as another language or object-model redesign.

Its first pipeline should be:

```text
source bytes
→ SETCO-Lisp tokenize/parse
→ recognized SETCO form
→ SETCO-AAL decode
→ AAL step
→ runtime result
→ diagnostic or projected value
```

For the initial kernel, keep it pure and bounded.

Recommended native modules:

```text
SETCO.Runtime.Input
SETCO.Runtime.Context
SETCO.Runtime.Dispatch
SETCO.Runtime.Result
SETCO.Runtime.Diagnostic
SETCO.Runtime.Capability
```

A minimal host model could be:

```haskell
data RuntimeContext = RuntimeContext
 { runtimeState :: AALState
 , runtimeCapabilities :: [RuntimeCapability]
 }

data RuntimeCapability
 = CapabilityClassify
 | CapabilityOrient
 | CapabilityScope
 | CapabilityStage
 | CapabilityProject

data RuntimeResult
 = RuntimeCompleted AALState Value
 | RuntimeRejected AALState Diagnostic
 | RuntimeDenied AALState RuntimeCapability
```

The key rule is:

```text
AAL determines semantic transitions.

The runtime determines whether and where they may execute.
```

## Do not add yet

The first runtime checkpoint should avoid:

```text
networking
gossip
persistent storage
dynamic plugins
receipt emission
process execution
hardware access
filesystem mutation
runtime authority grants
```

Those can be added only after the pure execution path is stable.

## First runtime invariants

The named tests and Coq boundary should cover:

```text
the same input and context produce the same result
unsupported capabilities produce a diagnostic
denied execution does not mutate state
runtime completion does not imply receipt authority
projection remains a value, not validation
rejected AAL transitions remain rejected
runtime dispatch preserves AAL instruction meaning
```

A strong first Coq theorem would be:

```coq
Theorem runtime_dispatch_deterministic :
 forall context instruction result1 result2,
 dispatch context instruction = result1 ->
 dispatch context instruction = result2 ->
 result1 = result2.
```

And the authority theorem:

```coq
Theorem runtime_completion_has_no_receipt_authority :
 forall context instruction result,
 dispatch context instruction = RuntimeCompleted result ->
 result_authority result = NoAuthorityReference.
```

## Cross-repository conformance should begin now

Since all three repositories declare contract `v0.1`, add the same small fixture identifiers to each:

```text
frame-v0.1
orientation-v0.1
forms-v0.1
aal-v0.1
diagnostics-v0.1
```

The runtime should consume copied conformance fixtures, not import the Haskell libraries from the other repositories yet.

That preserves:

```text
shared semantics
without permanent implementation coupling
```

## Current milestone map

```text
complete:
 setco-vc 61cecc4
 setco-lisp 877af35
 setco-aal bcfc0e1

next:
 setco-runtime pure execution host

then:
 cross-repository conformance harness
 C representation and ABI
 stronger linked Coq semantics
 final reconciliation into native SETCO
```

The suite is progressing correctly: each repository now contributes one independently testable semantic layer, while the final architecture remains one native SETCO project rather than a permanent composition of packages.

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-16 18:46` | `id:260716-1846` ^260716-184639

The fourth checkpoint completes the temporary SETCO development suite:

```text
setco-vc 61cecc4
setco-lisp 877af35
setco-aal bcfc0e1
setco-runtime f010c02
```

Each repository now proves one bounded concern independently:

```text
setco-vc
 staged history, frame, orientation, classification

setco-lisp
 source syntax, parsing, recognized SETCO forms

setco-aal
 typed instructions and deterministic transition semantics

setco-runtime
 capability-controlled hosting and dispatch
```

The next move should **not** be another subsystem repository. It should be a reconciliation pass across these four kernels.

## Next phase: cross-kernel conformance

Before flattening, verify that the copied semantic shapes have not drifted.

Create matching conformance fixtures in all four repositories for:

```text
contract version
diagnostic codes
instruction names
orientation order
status names
authority representation
projection representation
```

At minimum, freeze one canonical fixture containing:

```text
contract: setco-native-kernel-v0.1

orientation:
 Q, Sigma, L, R, Delta, Start, Target, Return

instructions:
 Classify
 Orient
 Scope
 Stage
 Project
 Reject
 Return

authority:
 none
 proof-reference
 receipt-reference
 runtime-reference

rule:
 projection is not authority
 staging is not acceptance
 runtime completion is not receipt authority
```

Each repository should independently parse or encode the fixture and assert the same ordering and meanings.

## Add an integration repository only temporarily

Since the production target is one flattened project, avoid creating another permanent conceptual component. A temporary repository or directory could be called:

```text
setco-integration
```

Its only responsibility would be testing the four independent checkpoints together.

It should not define new semantic types. It should consume exported test representations and verify:

```text
Lisp recognized form
→ AAL instruction
→ runtime dispatch
→ VC candidate transition
```

A first end-to-end scenario should be:

```text
(classify artifact)

SETCO-Lisp:
 recognizes Classify

SETCO-AAL:
 decodes Classify
 performs deterministic semantic step

SETCO-Runtime:
 capability permits or denies dispatch

SETCO-VC:
 records resulting staged classification transition

Result:
 no acceptance
 no receipt
 no implicit authority
```

## Important issue with copied shapes

The copied-type strategy was correct for isolated development, but it is now the largest risk.

You currently likely have repeated versions of concepts such as:

```text
Instruction
Diagnostic
State
AuthorityReference
Orientation8
```

Do not continue extending them independently.

From this point forward:

```text
no new shared constructor
no renamed shared field
no changed ordering
no changed diagnostic meaning
```

unless the same contract revision is intentionally applied to all four repositories.

Introduce:

```text
SETCO native kernel contract v0.2
```

only when a coordinated change is necessary.

## Reconciliation gate

Flatten after these checks pass:

```text
1. All four Haskell packages build and test independently.

2. All four Coq files compile without admits.

3. Shared fixtures agree byte-for-byte or constructor-for-constructor.

4. Lisp forms map uniquely to AAL instructions.

5. AAL instructions retain identical meaning through runtime dispatch.

6. Runtime results map into VC transitions without implying acceptance.

7. Diagnostics preserve their category across every boundary.

8. No repository contains hidden receipt or validation authority.
```

## Likely final flattened shape

```text
setco/
├── src/SETCO/
│ ├── Kernel/
│ ├── Frame/
│ ├── Orientation/
│ ├── Scope/
│ ├── Classification/
│ ├── Blackboard/
│ ├── Version/
│ ├── Lisp/
│ ├── AAL/
│ ├── Runtime/
│ ├── Projection/
│ └── Diagnostic/
├── formal/coq/
│ ├── SetcoKernel.v
│ ├── SetcoVC.v
│ ├── SetcoLisp.v
│ ├── SetcoAAL.v
│ ├── SetcoRuntime.v
│ └── SetcoIntegration.v
├── fixtures/
├── test/
└── docs/
```

At that point, the temporary repositories become historical development lineage or mirrors, not runtime dependencies.

The current milestone is therefore:

```text
isolated native kernels: complete

next:
 conformance
 integration
 semantic reconciliation

after:
 flatten into one native SETCO production repository
```

## What is proven now

The current work establishes:

- four kernels build independently;
- their local tests pass;
- their four Coq files compile;
- their canonical fixtures are byte-for-byte identical;
- their vocabulary and boundary rules align;
- the integration model preserves the intended pipeline;
- projection, staging, and runtime completion do not manufacture authority.

That is enough to say the **design surfaces conform**.

## What is not proven yet

The integration repository currently uses copied or mirrored semantic shapes. Therefore it does not yet prove that the actual exported Haskell modules interoperate without translation.

There are two different guarantees:

```text
semantic conformance

binary/source interoperability

```

You have completed the first. The next phase should establish the second before flattening.

## Next phase: executable reconciliation

The clean next step is to make `setco-integration` temporarily depend on the actual four local packages:

```cabal

 base,

Then replace the mirrored integration types with explicit adapters over the real exports:

→ SETCO.AAL instruction

→ SETCO.Runtime dispatch input

SETCO.Runtime result
→ SETCO.VC staged record

This is not the permanent production dependency graph. It is a reconciliation test proving that the kernels can actually be combined.

```haskell

 :: RecognizedForm
 -> Either IntegrationDiagnostic Instruction

dispatchInstruction
 :: RuntimeContext
 -> Instruction
 -> RuntimeResult

recordRuntimeResult
 :: VCRecord
 -> RuntimeResult
 -> Either IntegrationDiagnostic VCRecord

 produces no VC transition

 never reaches AAL execution

 remains rejected through runtime

Runtime completion
 does not create receipt authority
```

```lisp

```

The strongest integration path becomes:

```text
source text
→ tokenizer
→ parser
→ recognized form
→ real AAL instruction
→ real runtime dispatch
→ real VC staged record
```

## Then create the flattened repository

Once the actual-package integration passes, create the final native repository—preferably simply:

```text

```

The first flattened import should preserve module boundaries:

```text
setco/
├── src/SETCO/
│ ├── Kernel/
│ ├── VC/
│ ├── Lisp/
│ ├── AAL/
│ ├── Runtime/
│ └── Integration/
├── formal/coq/
├── fixtures/
├── test/
└── docs/
```

Do not rewrite the kernels during the first flattening pass. Move them essentially unchanged, establish one build, and run the same tests.

The flattening sequence should be:

```text
1. Prove real-package interoperability.

2. Freeze native-kernel contract v0.1.

3. Create the final setco repository.

4. Import all four kernels without semantic redesign.

5. Consolidate duplicated shared types.

6. Replace temporary adapters with canonical internal types.

7. Merge the four Coq developments under one dependency order.

8. Re-run all local and integration tests.

9. Archive or freeze the temporary development repositories.
```

Do not introduce `v0.2` merely to clean up duplicated names. First integrate `v0.1` exactly as it exists.
