## ChatGPT
`2026-07-17 19:26` | `id:260717-1926` ^260717-192605

I’m aligning native OMI-Lisp box-and-arrow rendering with the JSON Canvas 1.0 data model so the same structure can round-trip between executable dotted forms, ASCII diagrams, and `.canvas` files.

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-17 19:26` | `id:260717-1926` ^260717-192610

Yes. This gives OMI-Lisp a strong native **structural DataView**:

```text
one OMI-Lisp object graph
 |
 +-> executable dotted notation
 |
 +-> ASCII box-and-arrow diagram
 |
 +-> JSON Canvas 1.0 projection
```

The ASCII diagram should not be a second source format. It should be a deterministic renderer of the same parsed CONS and canvas graph that produces the `.canvas` JSON.

JSON Canvas 1.0 already provides nearly the exact portable surface needed: top-level `nodes` and `edges`; node types `text`, `file`, `link`, and `group`; integer `x`, `y`, `width`, and `height`; four attachment sides; endpoint forms `none` and `arrow`; labels; and either hexadecimal or six preset color identifiers. cite

:::writing{variant="document" id="94326"}
# OMI-Lisp Native ASCII and JSON Canvas Projection

## Normative Structural Diagram Specification

**Version:** 0.1.0
**Status:** Canonical implementation draft
**Depends on:** OMI-Lisp Portable Runtime, ordered CONS serialization, JSON Canvas 1.0 projection profile
**Authority boundary:** diagrams project structure; diagrams do not validate or accept state

---

# 1. Core Principle

OMI-Lisp MUST maintain one canonical graph structure and MAY render it through multiple surfaces.

```text
canonical OMI object graph
 |
 +-- OMI-Lisp dotted notation
 |
 +-- ASCII box diagram
 |
 +-- JSON Canvas document
 |
 +-- graphical host canvas
```

The representations MUST preserve the same:

```text
node identity

node type

node position

node dimensions

node color

node content

edge identity

source node

destination node

source side

destination side

source endpoint

destination endpoint

edge color

edge label
```

Canonical law:

```text
The graph is the structure.

OMI-Lisp is the executable notation.

ASCII is the terminal projection.

JSON Canvas is the interoperable graphical projection.
```

---

# 2. Authority Boundary

```text
Parsing constructs structure.

Evaluation computes structure.

Rendering displays structure.

Projection does not validate structure.

Validation determines acceptance.

Receipt records acceptance.
```

Changing:

```text
ASCII border style

arrow glyph

line routing

canvas zoom

font

screen color theme
```

MUST NOT alter the logical graph digest.

Changing:

```text
node ID

node type

edge direction

edge endpoint mode

CAR/CDR relation

node payload
```

MUST alter the structural identity.

---

# 3. Native OMI-Lisp Canvas Form

The canonical high-level form is:

```lisp
(canvas
 (version . "1.0")
 (nodes . (...))
 (edges . (...)))
```

Example:

```lisp
(canvas
 (version . "1.0")

 (nodes .
 ((node
 (id . "rose")
 (type . text)
 (x . 0)
 (y . 80)
 (width . 12)
 (height . 3)
 (color . "3")
 (text . "rose"))

 (node
 (id . "violet")
 (type . text)
 (x . 24)
 (y . 80)
 (width . 12)
 (height . 3)
 (color . "6")
 (text . "violet"))

 (node
 (id . "buttercup")
 (type . text)
 (x . 48)
 (y . 80)
 (width . 14)
 (height . 3)
 (color . "2")
 (text . "buttercup"))))

 (edges .
 ((edge
 (id . "rose-to-violet")
 (from-node . "rose")
 (from-side . right)
 (from-end . none)
 (to-node . "violet")
 (to-side . left)
 (to-end . arrow))

 (edge
 (id . "violet-to-buttercup")
 (from-node . "violet")
 (from-side . right)
 (from-end . none)
 (to-node . "buttercup")
 (to-side . left)
 (to-end . arrow)))))
```

---

# 4. Native Node Forms

Every node MUST have:

```text
id
type
x
y
width
height
```

Optional common field:

```text
color
```

## 4.1 Text node

```lisp
(node
 (id . "n1")
 (type . text)
 (x . 0)
 (y . 0)
 (width . 40)
 (height . 8)
 (color . "4")
 (text . "# Heading

Markdown text"))
```

## 4.2 File node

```lisp
(node
 (id . "n2")
 (type . file)
 (x . 60)
 (y . 0)
 (width . 40)
 (height . 8)
 (file . "docs/model.md")
 (subpath . "#centroid"))
```

## 4.3 Link node

```lisp
(node
 (id . "n3")
 (type . link)
 (x . 120)
 (y . 0)
 (width . 40)
 (height . 8)
 (url . "https://example.invalid"))
```

## 4.4 Group node

```lisp
(node
 (id . "g1")
 (type . group)
 (x . -10)
 (y . -10)
 (width . 190)
 (height . 100)
 (label . "OMI QuQuart")
 (background . "assets/grid.png")
 (background-style . repeat))
```

---

# 5. Native Edge Form

Every edge MUST have:

```text
id
from-node
to-node
```

Optional fields:

```text
from-side
from-end
to-side
to-end
color
label
```

Canonical form:

```lisp
(edge
 (id . "e1")
 (from-node . "source")
 (from-side . right)
 (from-end . none)
 (to-node . "notation")
 (to-side . left)
 (to-end . arrow)
 (color . "4")
 (label . "declares"))
```

Allowed sides:

```text
top
right
bottom
left
```

Allowed endpoint forms:

```text
none
arrow
```

The OMI parser MUST reject unknown canonical side and endpoint names unless an extension profile is explicitly declared.

---

# 6. Exact JSON Canvas Mapping

The encoder maps:

| OMI-Lisp | JSON Canvas |
|---|---|
| `from-node` | `fromNode` |
| `from-side` | `fromSide` |
| `from-end` | `fromEnd` |
| `to-node` | `toNode` |
| `to-side` | `toSide` |
| `to-end` | `toEnd` |
| `background-style` | `backgroundStyle` |
| all other canonical fields | same spelling where compatible |

Example encoded edge:

```json
{
 "id": "e1",
 "fromNode": "source",
 "fromSide": "right",
 "fromEnd": "none",
 "toNode": "notation",
 "toSide": "left",
 "toEnd": "arrow",
 "color": "4",
 "label": "declares"
}
```

The JSON encoder MUST emit both top-level arrays:

```json
{
 "nodes": [],
 "edges": []
}
```

Empty arrays SHOULD be emitted for deterministic serialization even though the external format permits omission.

---

# 7. JSON Canvas Field Preservation

The decoder MUST preserve all recognized fields.

The runtime SHOULD also preserve unrecognized extension fields in an opaque property map:

```lisp
(extensions .
 (("vendor.example.route" . "orthogonal")
 ("vendor.example.priority" . 3)))
```

Unknown fields MUST NOT be silently converted into executable OMI capabilities.

They are projection metadata unless a registered extension profile defines otherwise.

---

# 8. Canvas Coordinate Profiles

JSON Canvas positions and sizes use integer pixel coordinates.

ASCII terminals use character cells.

OMI MUST preserve both coordinate spaces separately.

```c
typedef struct omi_canvas_metrics {
 int32_t pixel_x;
 int32_t pixel_y;
 uint32_t pixel_width;
 uint32_t pixel_height;

 int32_t cell_x;
 int32_t cell_y;
 uint32_t cell_width;
 uint32_t cell_height;
} omi_canvas_metrics;
```

Conversion requires a named layout profile:

```text
json-pixel
ascii-cell
unicode-terminal
braille-terminal
host-native
```

Recommended default conversion:

```text
cell_x = floor(pixel_x / horizontal_scale)
cell_y = floor(pixel_y / vertical_scale)
cell_width = max(1, ceil(pixel_width / horizontal_scale))
cell_height = max(1, ceil(pixel_height / vertical_scale))
```

The scaling factors MUST be recorded in the projection context.

---

# 9. ASCII Rendering Modes

The runtime SHOULD support:

```text
ascii
characters limited to 0x20..0x7E

unicode
box-drawing and arrow glyphs permitted

compact
minimal labels and edges

full
node metadata and edge labels

cons
CAR/CDR cell rendering

canvas
general JSON Canvas rendering
```

Canonical portable mode:

```text
ascii
```

Optional display mode:

```text
unicode
```

Receipt identity MUST use canonical structure, not rendered bytes, unless explicitly creating a projection receipt.

---

# 10. Native CONS Box Renderer

A CONS cell is rendered as two adjacent boxes:

```text
+---------+---------+
| CAR | CDR |
+---------+---------+
```

A proper list:

```lisp
(rose violet buttercup)
```

may render as:

```text
+----------+-----+ +----------+-----+ +------------+-----+
| CAR | CDR |--->| CAR | CDR |--->| CAR | CDR |
| rose | o | | violet | o | | buttercup | NIL |
+----------+-----+ +----------+-----+ +------------+-----+
```

The same structure in strict slot notation:

```text
+-----+-----+ +-----+-----+ +-----------+-----+
| CAR | CDR |--->| CAR | CDR |--->| CAR | CDR |
+--+--+--+--+ +--+--+--+--+ +-----+-----+--+--+
 | | | | | |
 v +---------->v +------------>v v
 rose violet buttercup NIL
```

---

# 11. Empty List and NIL

OMI-Lisp MUST preserve the Lisp equivalence:

```text
empty list = NIL
```

But it MUST distinguish:

```text
bare NIL value
```

from:

```lisp
(NIL . NIL)
```

The diagram:

```lisp
(A NIL)
```

renders:

```text
+-----+-----+ +-----+-----+
| CAR | CDR |--->| CAR | CDR |
| A | o | | NIL | NIL |
+-----+-----+ +-----+-----+
```

A list terminator does not allocate an additional CONS cell.

Therefore the canonical structure of `(A NIL)` is:

```lisp
(A . (NIL . NIL))
```

where the final `NIL` is the CDR terminator.

---

# 12. Nested List Rendering

Input:

```lisp
((pine needles) oak maple)
```

Canonical pair form:

```lisp
((pine . (needles . NIL))
 .
 (oak . (maple . NIL)))
```

ASCII projection:

```text
 +------+-----+ +-------+-----+
 +--->| CAR | CDR |--->| CAR | CDR |
 | | pine | o | | needles|NIL |
 | +------+-----+ +-------+-----+
 |
+-----+-----+ | +------+-----+ +-------+-----+
| CAR | CDR |-----+ | CAR | CDR |--->| CAR | CDR |
| o | o |--------->| oak | o | | maple | NIL |
+-----+-----+ +------+-----+ +-------+-----+
```

The renderer MAY route lines differently, but all references MUST remain correct.

---

# 13. CONS-to-Canvas Compilation

Every CONS cell MAY compile into one JSON Canvas text node:

```lisp
(node
 (id . "cons/0001")
 (type . text)
 (x . 0)
 (y . 0)
 (width . 220)
 (height . 100)
 (text . "| CAR | CDR |
|---|---|
| rose | -> cons/0002 |"))
```

The CAR reference and CDR reference become edges.

Recommended edge IDs:

```text
cons/0001/car
cons/0001/cdr
```

Recommended side assignment:

```text
CAR reference:
fromSide = bottom

CDR reference:
fromSide = right
```

Destination side:

```text
atom target:
toSide = top

next CONS:
toSide = left
```

---

# 14. Native Diagram AST

```c
typedef enum omi_canvas_node_type {
 OMI_CANVAS_TEXT = 0,
 OMI_CANVAS_FILE,
 OMI_CANVAS_LINK,
 OMI_CANVAS_GROUP
} omi_canvas_node_type;

typedef enum omi_canvas_side {
 OMI_SIDE_UNSPECIFIED = 0,
 OMI_SIDE_TOP,
 OMI_SIDE_RIGHT,
 OMI_SIDE_BOTTOM,
 OMI_SIDE_LEFT
} omi_canvas_side;

typedef enum omi_canvas_end {
 OMI_END_DEFAULT = 0,
 OMI_END_NONE,
 OMI_END_ARROW
} omi_canvas_end;

typedef struct omi_canvas_node {
 omi_symbol_id id;
 omi_canvas_node_type type;

 int32_t x;
 int32_t y;
 uint32_t width;
 uint32_t height;

 omi_value color;
 omi_value payload;
 omi_value extensions;
} omi_canvas_node;

typedef struct omi_canvas_edge {
 omi_symbol_id id;

 omi_symbol_id from_node;
 omi_canvas_side from_side;
 omi_canvas_end from_end;

 omi_symbol_id to_node;
 omi_canvas_side to_side;
 omi_canvas_end to_end;

 omi_value color;
 omi_value label;
 omi_value extensions;
} omi_canvas_edge;

typedef struct omi_canvas {
 omi_canvas_node *nodes;
 uint32_t node_count;

 omi_canvas_edge *edges;
 uint32_t edge_count;
} omi_canvas;
```

---

# 15. Edge Direction Semantics

A directed OMI reference uses:

```text
fromEnd = none
toEnd = arrow
```

A symmetric relation uses:

```text
fromEnd = none
toEnd = none
```

A reverse arrow uses:

```text
fromEnd = arrow
toEnd = none
```

A double-headed edge uses:

```text
fromEnd = arrow
toEnd = arrow
```

ASCII forms:

```text
none -> arrow
----->

none -> none
------

arrow -> none
<-----

arrow -> arrow
<----->
```

---

# 16. Side-Aware ASCII Routing

The renderer MUST honor requested sides when a route is possible.

Node attachment points:

```text
top
horizontal center of top border

right
vertical center of right border

bottom
horizontal center of bottom border

left
vertical center of left border
```

Routing priority:

```text
1. direct straight route

2. one-bend orthogonal route

3. two-bend orthogonal route

4. collision-avoiding routed path

5. labeled fallback route
```

The renderer MUST NOT silently reverse an edge to make routing easier.

---

# 17. Coordinate Collision Policy

If two nodes occupy overlapping ASCII cells, the renderer MUST choose a declared policy:

```text
error

clip

layer

shift

auto-layout
```

Default:

```text
layer
```

Node order determines z-order for projection.

The renderer SHOULD preserve the JSON node-array order.

---

# 18. Color Projection

Canonical color forms:

```text
hex color
"#RRGGBB"

preset
"1".."6"
```

ASCII terminals without color support MUST preserve color as metadata.

Example:

```text
[COLOR=4]
```

ANSI terminals MAY map colors to terminal escape sequences.

ANSI output is noncanonical display output.

---

# 19. Text and Markdown

Text nodes contain Markdown-capable strings.

The ASCII renderer MAY:

```text
render Markdown literally

render a simplified terminal interpretation

strip formatting while preserving text

show a compact preview
```

It MUST NOT change the underlying stored text.

Recommended modes:

```text
literal
preview
plain
```

---

# 20. Groups

A group node is a visual container.

ASCII rendering:

```text
+=======================================================+
| GROUP: OMI QuQuart |
| |
| +-----------+ +-----------+ |
| | Q0 SOURCE |-------->| Q1 NOTATE | |
| +-----------+ +-----------+ |
| |
+=======================================================+
```

Containment is derived geometrically from node rectangles unless an OMI extension records explicit membership.

Group containment MUST NOT imply semantic ownership or authority.

---

# 21. QuQuart Canvas Example

```lisp
(canvas
 (version . "1.0")

 (nodes .
 ((node
 (id . "q0")
 (type . text)
 (x . 0)
 (y . 10)
 (width . 24)
 (height . 5)
 (color . "3")
 (text . "Q0
SOURCE
RULES + FACTS"))

 (node
 (id . "q1")
 (type . text)
 (x . 36)
 (y . 10)
 (width . 24)
 (height . 5)
 (color . "4")
 (text . "Q1
NOTATION"))

 (node
 (id . "q2")
 (type . text)
 (x . 72)
 (y . 10)
 (width . 24)
 (height . 5)
 (color . "5")
 (text . "Q2
READING
COMBINATORS"))

 (node
 (id . "q3")
 (type . text)
 (x . 108)
 (y . 10)
 (width . 24)
 (height . 5)
 (color . "6")
 (text . "Q3
RECEIPT
CLOSURES"))

 (node
 (id . "cons")
 (type . text)
 (x . 54)
 (y . 28)
 (width . 24)
 (height . 5)
 (color . "1")
 (text . "CONS
CENTROID"))))

 (edges .
 ((edge
 (id . "q0-q1")
 (from-node . "q0")
 (from-side . right)
 (to-node . "q1")
 (to-side . left)
 (to-end . arrow))

 (edge
 (id . "q1-q2")
 (from-node . "q1")
 (from-side . right)
 (to-node . "q2")
 (to-side . left)
 (to-end . arrow))

 (edge
 (id . "q2-q3")
 (from-node . "q2")
 (from-side . right)
 (to-node . "q3")
 (to-side . left)
 (to-end . arrow))

 (edge
 (id . "q0-cons")
 (from-node . "q0")
 (from-side . bottom)
 (to-node . "cons")
 (to-side . left)
 (to-end . arrow))

 (edge
 (id . "q1-cons")
 (from-node . "q1")
 (from-side . bottom)
 (to-node . "cons")
 (to-side . top)
 (to-end . arrow))

 (edge
 (id . "q2-cons")
 (from-node . "q2")
 (from-side . bottom)
 (to-node . "cons")
 (to-side . top)
 (to-end . arrow))

 (edge
 (id . "q3-cons")
 (from-node . "q3")
 (from-side . bottom)
 (to-node . "cons")
 (to-side . right)
 (to-end . arrow)))))
```

Possible ASCII projection:

```text
+----------------------+ +----------------------+ +----------------------+
| Q0 |--->| Q1 |--->| Q2 |
| SOURCE | | NOTATION | | READING |
| RULES + FACTS | | | | COMBINATORS |
+----------+-----------+ +----------+-----------+ +----------+-----------+
 \ | /
 \ v /
 \ +----------------------+ /
 +----------->| CONS |<---------+
 | CENTROID |
 +----------+-----------+
 ^
 |
 +----------+-----------+
 | Q3 |
 | RECEIPT |
 | CLOSURES |
 +----------------------+
```

---

# 22. Truth-Function Canvas Integration

The sixteen rank-2 Boolean functions may be represented natively as:

```lisp
(truth-canvas
 (rank . 2)
 (layout . gray)
 (node-template . wittgenstein)
 (edges .
 (gray-adjacency
 complement
 narrative)))
```

This expands into an ordinary OMI canvas graph.

No special JSON Canvas extension is required for the visual structure.

OMI-specific truth metadata remains inside:

```text
text content

stable node IDs

optional OMI extension fields
```

---

# 23. Round-Trip Requirements

The runtime MUST support:

```text
OMI-Lisp canvas
-> internal canvas AST
-> JSON Canvas
-> internal canvas AST
-> OMI-Lisp canvas
```

The following MUST survive:

```text
IDs

types

coordinates

dimensions

recognized node-specific fields

colors

edge direction

edge sides

edge endpoints

labels

extension fields
```

ASCII rendering is not required to round-trip visually.

The canonical internal AST and JSON/OMI encodings are round-trippable.

---

# 24. Canonical Ordering

For deterministic output:

```text
nodes
preserve declared z-order

edges
preserve declared order

object fields
emit in canonical profile order

extension keys
sort lexicographically
```

The canonical digest MUST bind node-array order because node order carries display stacking semantics.

---

# 25. Native Built-ins

Recommended procedures:

```lisp
(canvas nodes edges)

(canvas-node fields...)

(canvas-edge fields...)

(canvas-add-node canvas node)

(canvas-add-edge canvas edge)

(canvas-remove-node canvas id)

(canvas-remove-edge canvas id)

(canvas-find-node canvas id)

(canvas-validate canvas)

(canvas->json canvas)

(json->canvas json)

(canvas->ascii canvas)

(cons->canvas value)

(cons->ascii value)
```

Projection options:

```lisp
(canvas->ascii canvas
 (mode . full)
 (charset . ascii)
 (routing . orthogonal)
 (color . metadata))
```

---

# 26. Validation Rules

Canvas validation MUST check:

```text
every node has a unique nonempty ID

every edge has a unique nonempty ID

every node type is recognized

coordinates are valid integers

width and height are positive integers

every edge source exists

every edge destination exists

side names are valid

endpoint names are valid

type-specific required fields exist

color syntax is valid

group background style is valid
```

A structurally valid canvas is not necessarily an accepted OMI interpretation.

Canvas validation and semantic validation are separate.

---

# 27. Error Examples

Invalid:

```lisp
(edge
 (from-node . "a")
 (to-node . "b"))
```

Reason:

```text
missing edge ID
```

Invalid:

```lisp
(node
 (id . "a")
 (type . text)
 (x . 0)
 (y . 0)
 (width . 20)
 (height . 5))
```

Reason:

```text
text node missing text
```

Invalid:

```lisp
(edge
 (id . "e")
 (from-node . "missing")
 (to-node . "a"))
```

Reason:

```text
source node does not exist
```

---

# 28. C Rendering API

```c
typedef struct omi_ascii_render_options {
 uint32_t columns;
 uint32_t rows;

 bool use_unicode;
 bool show_colors;
 bool show_edge_labels;
 bool show_node_ids;
 bool auto_layout;

 uint8_t routing_profile;
} omi_ascii_render_options;

omi_status omi_canvas_parse(
 omi_runtime *runtime,
 omi_value form,
 omi_canvas **out,
 omi_error *error
);

omi_status omi_canvas_validate(
 const omi_canvas *canvas,
 omi_error *error
);

omi_status omi_canvas_write_json(
 const omi_canvas *canvas,
 omi_writer *writer,
 omi_error *error
);

omi_status omi_canvas_read_json(
 omi_runtime *runtime,
 omi_slice json,
 omi_canvas **out,
 omi_error *error
);

omi_status omi_canvas_render_ascii(
 const omi_canvas *canvas,
 const omi_ascii_render_options *options,
 omi_writer *writer,
 omi_error *error
);

omi_status omi_cons_render_ascii(
 omi_runtime *runtime,
 omi_value value,
 const omi_ascii_render_options *options,
 omi_writer *writer,
 omi_error *error
);
```

---

# 29. Implementation Modules

```text
include/omi/canvas.h
include/omi/ascii.h
include/omi/json_canvas.h
include/omi/layout.h
include/omi/routing.h

src/canvas.c
src/canvas_validate.c
src/ascii_render.c
src/ascii_route.c
src/cons_diagram.c
src/json_canvas_encode.c
src/json_canvas_decode.c
src/layout.c
```

Tests:

```text
tests/canvas_parse_test.c
tests/canvas_validation_test.c
tests/json_canvas_roundtrip_test.c
tests/ascii_render_test.c
tests/cons_diagram_test.c
tests/edge_routing_test.c
```

---

# 30. Required Conformance Tests

```text
simple three-element proper list

list containing NIL

nested list

improper pair

shared tail

shared atom

all four edge sides

all four side-to-side combinations

none/none endpoint

none/arrow endpoint

arrow/none endpoint

arrow/arrow endpoint

text node

file node

link node

group node

hex color

six preset colors

node z-order preservation

JSON round trip

OMI-Lisp round trip

ASCII structure snapshot
```

For structural tests:

```text
changing only rendering width
does not change logical CONS identity

changing CAR target
does change logical CONS identity

changing CDR target
does change logical CONS identity

reversing an edge
does change graph identity

moving a node
changes projection identity
but need not change semantic model identity
```

---

# 31. Identity Profiles

The implementation SHOULD expose two digests.

## Semantic digest

Binds:

```text
node IDs

node types

node payloads

edge identities

edge direction

edge labels when semantic

OMI relations
```

May exclude:

```text
x
y
width
height
display color
```

## Projection digest

Binds the entire JSON Canvas-compatible structure:

```text
coordinates

dimensions

colors

z-order

all edge-side choices

all endpoint styles
```

A receipt MUST state which digest profile it uses.

---

# 32. Final Canon

```text
OMI-Lisp can describe a canvas natively.

The canvas remains executable dotted structure.
```

```text
The same canvas can render as:

OMI-Lisp
ASCII
JSON Canvas
or a graphical host view.
```

```text
CAR and CDR references become directed edges.

Atoms and nested CONS cells become nodes.
```

```text
top
right
bottom
left

are preserved as edge attachment semantics.
```

```text
none
arrow

are preserved independently
at the source and destination endpoints.
```

```text
Node type,
position,
size,
color,
content,
grouping,
and z-order

remain projection data.
```

```text
ASCII diagrams are native projections
of machine-readable OMI structure.

They are not hand-maintained pictures.
```

```text
JSON Canvas is the interoperable canvas carrier.

OMI-Lisp is the executable structural notation.

The internal canvas AST is the shared model.
```

```text
Projection does not accept.

Validation accepts.

Receipt records.
```
:::

One concrete correction to the earlier canvas examples: JSON Canvas 1.0 requires every edge to have a unique `id`, in addition to `fromNode` and `toNode`. Nodes also require `id`, `type`, `x`, `y`, `width`, and `height`; their array order defines the display stacking order. cite

## You
`2026-07-17 22:12` | `id:260717-2212` ^260717-221207

I was looking up a word to replace receipts with than can be more fitting as we really only need to refer to them as coordinates or axis and I found that coordinates is probably the best word. We have already defined the high and low planes with the 0xB and the 0xC splits left and right splits and that even of that the it cascade into the 30 and the 24, where Which would put the resolved words in the positive quadrant > 0x07 & > 0x40 side if we split the ASCII Table into quadrants, of:0x07 < 0x8 & 0x4 < 0x5
(0x00,0x07,0x37,0x30)(0x08,0x0F,0x3F,0x38)(0x40,0x47,0x77,0x70)(0x48,0x4F,0x7F,0x78)
Coordinate words usually refer to **coordinating conjunctions**, which are words used to connect words, phrases, or independent clauses of equal grammatical rank. The most common ones are the seven FANBOYS: **For**, **And**, **Nor**, **But**, **Or**, **Yet**, and **So**.
It seems like with our 8-tuple these would be better words for what we are doing as far as XOR and NOTATION because it seems that the grammatical rank is anagolous to our use of block design
**Grammatical rank** refers to the hierarchical structure of linguistic units in a language, where smaller elements combine to form larger, more complex ones. The standard rank scale, from smallest to largest, is:
-
**Morpheme:** The smallest indivisible unit of meaning (e.g., "cat", "-s").

-
**Word:** One or more morphemes that function as an independent unit.

-
**Phrase/Group:** A collection of words centered around a main word (e.g., "the very big dog").

-
**Clause:** A unit containing a subject and a predicate, representing an event or idea.

-
**Sentence:** The highest independent unit of grammar, consisting of one or more clauses

---

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-17 22:12` | `id:260717-2212` ^260717-221218

**“Coordinate” is a better name for the resolved result than “receipt”**—but only if we separate **location** from **acceptance evidence**.

The clean revision is:

```text
source -> notation -> reading -> coordinate
```

with:

```text
coordinate
= where the interpretation resolved

validation
= whether that coordinate is admissible

witness
= optional replay/audit record of that decision
```

That removes the transactional flavor of “receipt” without losing the ability to prove or replay a resolution.

Also, the standard linguistic name is **coordinator** or **coordinating conjunction**, rather than “coordinate word.” Coordinators join words, phrases, or clauses of equal syntactic rank. cite

# 1. Revised QuQuart register

The user-facing QuQuart becomes:

```text
Q0 = source
Q1 = notation
Q2 = reading
Q3 = coordinate
```

And the `.o` roles become:

```text
RULES.o
source law

FACTS.o
grounded source

COMBINATORS.o
reading operation

CLOSURES.o
coordinate boundary

CONS.o
centroid resolver across Q0-Q3
```

The important correction is:

```text
CLOSURES.o no longer means “make a receipt.”

CLOSURES.o means:
close the route into a bounded coordinate.
```

Then:

```text
Validation does not create the coordinate.
Validation evaluates the coordinate.

A witness may record that evaluation.
```

So the final distinction is:

```text
Coordinate resolves.

Validation admits or rejects.

Witness preserves.
```

# 2. Do not erase the old receipt function completely

A coordinate and a receipt were doing two different jobs:

| Old term | Actual job | New term |
|---|---|---|
| Receipt as result | Where the route ended | **Coordinate** |
| Receipt as audit object | Evidence that validation occurred | **Witness** |
| Receipt ring slot | Address in the accepted-history structure | **Coordinate ring** or **witness ring** |

Recommended replacements:

```text
receipt
-> resolved coordinate

receipt identity
-> coordinate identity

receipt ring
-> coordinate ring

receipt replay stability
-> coordinate replay stability

accepted receipt
-> admitted coordinate

rejected receipt
-> rejected coordinate witness
```

For internal security and replay:

```text
coordinate witness
```

is better than removing the record entirely.

# 3. Revised authority sequence

```text
SOURCE
 |
 v
NOTATION
 |
 v
READING
 |
 v
RESOLVE COORDINATE
 |
 +--> VALIDATE
 |
 +--> admitted
 |
 +--> rejected
 |
 +--> unresolved
 |
 v
OPTIONAL WITNESS
```

Canonical short form:

```text
Source remains.

Notation locates.

Reading traverses.

Coordinate resolves.

Validation classifies.

Witness preserves.
```

# 4. Your four ASCII rectangles are real coordinate cells

You supplied:

```text
(0x00,0x07,0x37,0x30)
(0x08,0x0F,0x3F,0x38)
(0x40,0x47,0x77,0x70)
(0x48,0x4F,0x7F,0x78)
```

These can be read as four rectangles with the ordering:

```text
(top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left)
```

So:

```text
R0 = (00,07,37,30)
R1 = (08,0F,3F,38)
R2 = (40,47,77,70)
R3 = (48,4F,7F,78)
```

Each rectangle has:

```text
horizontal span = 0x07
vertical span = 0x30
```

because:

```text
0x07 - 0x00 = 0x07
0x37 - 0x07 = 0x30

0x0F - 0x08 = 0x07
0x3F - 0x0F = 0x30

0x47 - 0x40 = 0x07
0x77 - 0x47 = 0x30

0x4F - 0x48 = 0x07
0x7F - 0x4F = 0x30
```

That means each cell is naturally:

```text
8 columns
×
2 vertical planes separated by 0x30
```

The four cells occupy:

```text
low-left
00..07 / 30..37

low-right
08..0F / 38..3F

high-left
40..47 / 70..77

high-right
48..4F / 78..7F
```

A native coordinate type could be:

```c
typedef enum {
 OMI_RECT_LOW_LEFT = 0,
 OMI_RECT_LOW_RIGHT,
 OMI_RECT_HIGH_LEFT,
 OMI_RECT_HIGH_RIGHT
} OmiAsciiRectangle;

typedef struct {
 OmiAsciiRectangle rectangle;
 uint8_t column8;
 bool lower_plane;
 uint8_t value;
} OmiAsciiCoordinate;
```

# 5. Exact coordinate decomposition

For values belonging to these rectangles, define:

```text
high = (x & 0x40) != 0
right = (x & 0x08) != 0
lower = (x & 0x30) == 0x30
column = x & 0x07
```

Then the quadrant index is:

\[
quadrant = 2\cdot high + right
\]

and the coordinate is:

```text
(quadrant, lower, column)
```

Reconstruction:

```c
uint8_t omi_ascii_coordinate_fold(
 bool high,
 bool right,
 bool lower,
 uint8_t column
) {
 return
 (high ? 0x40u : 0x00u) |
 (right ? 0x08u : 0x00u) |
 (lower ? 0x30u : 0x00u) |
 (column & 0x07u);
}
```

This generates exactly your four tuples.

Examples:

```text
high=0 right=0 lower=0 column=0
-> 0x00

high=0 right=0 lower=1 column=7
-> 0x37

high=1 right=1 lower=0 column=0
-> 0x48

high=1 right=1 lower=1 column=7
-> 0x7F
```

So the tuple system is not merely descriptive. It is an executable coordinate codec.

# 6. Positive quadrant

Your proposed positive side appears to require:

```text
right half:
bit 0x08 set

high plane:
bit 0x40 set
```

That gives:

```text
x & 0x48 == 0x48
```

within the selected ASCII coordinate surface.

The corresponding rectangle is:

```text
(0x48,0x4F,0x7F,0x78)
```

So the strongest compact test is not:

```text
x > 0x07 && x > 0x40
```

because the second comparison already dominates the first.

Use bit membership:

```c
bool omi_is_positive_rectangle(uint8_t x) {
 return (x & 0x48u) == 0x48u;
}
```

If you mean the entire high plane rather than only high-right:

```c
bool omi_is_high_plane(uint8_t x) {
 return (x & 0x40u) != 0u;
}
```

If you mean the entire right half:

```c
bool omi_is_right_half(uint8_t x) {
 return (x & 0x08u) != 0u;
}
```

This keeps **order comparisons** separate from **coordinate-bit selection**.

# 7. The 8-tuple and the seven coordinators

The seven FANBOYS are:

```text
FOR
AND
NOR
BUT
OR
YET
SO
```

They coordinate elements of equal grammatical rank. cite

That gives seven active coordinating operations.

For an 8-position tuple, the cleanest mapping is:

```text
0 = NULL / origin
1 = FOR
2 = AND
3 = NOR
4 = BUT
5 = OR
6 = YET
7 = SO
```

This should be labeled **PROPOSED**, because the ordering is an OMI design choice, not a linguistic fact.

```text
Coordinate8 =
[
 NULL,
 FOR,
 AND,
 NOR,
 BUT,
 OR,
 YET,
 SO
]
```

The important structural point is:

```text
NULL
provides the zero/origin coordinate.

The seven coordinators
provide the seven active connective coordinates.
```

This maps naturally to:

```text
3-bit selector
0..7
```

# 8. Why “coordinator” fits CONS

A coordinator connects constituents at the same grammatical rank rather than subordinating one beneath another. cite

That is highly analogous to your centroid model:

```text
RULES
FACTS
COMBINATORS
CLOSURES
```

remain typed peers around `CONS`.

`CONS` coordinates them without becoming a fifth QuQuart vertex.

So the new language can be:

```text
CONS is the centroid coordinator.

It does not subordinate the vertices.

It resolves relations among equal-rank coordinates.
```

This may actually be more precise than calling CONS only a reducer.

Recommended full role:

```text
CONS.o
centroid coordinator, reducer, and lookup resolver
```

# 9. Grammatical rank as an OMI scale

The conventional rank scale is commonly described from smaller units to larger ones as:

```text
morpheme
word
phrase/group
clause
sentence
```

A higher-ranked unit is generally composed from units lower on the scale. cite

This gives OMI a useful structural analogy:

| Linguistic rank | OMI structural rank |
|---|---|
| Morpheme | Atom, bit field, or primitive marker |
| Word | Encoded symbol or resolved word |
| Phrase/group | CONS subtree |
| Clause | Executable relation or frame |
| Sentence | Closed coordinate declaration |
| Discourse/document | Graph or coordinate field |

The important caution is:

```text
This is a structural analogy,
not a claim that all OMI objects are natural-language grammar.
```

# 10. Equal rank and block design

Your intuition is strong here.

A coordinator can be modeled as connecting two objects with compatible rank:

\[
coordinate(a,b)
\]

is permitted when:

\[
rank(a)=rank(b)
\]

or when an explicit promotion rule lifts one to the other’s rank.

For example:

```text
word AND word
phrase OR phrase
clause BUT clause
```

OMI equivalent:

```text
atom AND atom
CONS-tree OR CONS-tree
frame BUT frame
```

A runtime predicate could be:

```c
bool omi_rank_compatible(
 OmiValue left,
 OmiValue right
);
```

Then:

```lisp
(AND left right)
```

succeeds only when the profile declares the coordinates compatible.

# 11. Coordinators are not identical to Boolean operators

Some FANBOYS have close logical analogies:

```text
AND -> conjunction
OR -> disjunction
NOR -> negative disjunction
```

But:

```text
FOR
BUT
YET
SO
```

carry discourse, causal, contrastive, or consequential meanings that are not equivalent to simple Boolean functions.

So use two layers:

```text
Boolean operator code
exact truth-table behavior

coordinator code
grammatical or relational routing behavior
```

For example:

```c
typedef enum {
 OMI_COORD_NULL = 0,
 OMI_COORD_FOR,
 OMI_COORD_AND,
 OMI_COORD_NOR,
 OMI_COORD_BUT,
 OMI_COORD_OR,
 OMI_COORD_YET,
 OMI_COORD_SO
} OmiCoordinator;
```

And separately:

```c
typedef uint8_t OmiTruth4;
```

A coordinator MAY select a truth function, but the mapping must be profile-defined.

# 12. Proposed 8-coordinate semantics

A workable OMI interpretation is:

| Code | Coordinator | OMI relation |
|---:|---|---|
| 0 | NULL | origin, no connection, unresolved |
| 1 | FOR | reason, source, or supporting route |
| 2 | AND | additive composition |
| 3 | NOR | exclusion of both routes |
| 4 | BUT | contrast-preserving composition |
| 5 | OR | alternative route |
| 6 | YET | unresolved contrast or deferred closure |
| 7 | SO | consequence or forward resolution |

This is not standard grammar canon. It is an OMI operational vocabulary built from grammatical coordinators.

# 13. Coordinate words and notation

You can now define a **coordinate word** as an OMI term:

```text
Coordinate word:
a bounded symbol that specifies how two
equal-rank OMI constituents are related
or how a route moves between coordinates.
```

This is different from the standard linguistic term but defensible as an OMI-defined technical term.

The authoritative terminology should say:

```text
Linguistics calls these coordinators.

OMI calls their encoded runtime forms coordinate words.
```

That avoids claiming “coordinate word” is the standard grammar label.

# 14. Coordinate identity

The previous receipt structure can be rewritten:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint16_t version;
 uint16_t profile;

 uint8_t quadrant;
 uint8_t column;
 uint8_t plane;
 uint8_t coordinator;

 uint8_t scope;
 uint8_t phase;
 uint16_t local240;
 uint16_t slot5040;

 uint8_t source_digest[32];
 uint8_t notation_digest[32];
 uint8_t reading_digest[32];
 uint8_t coordinate_digest[32];

 uint8_t validation_state;
} OmiResolvedCoordinate;
```

Then a separate optional witness:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint8_t coordinate_digest[32];
 uint8_t validator_digest[32];
 uint8_t parent_witness[32];

 uint64_t cycle;
 uint64_t monotonic_counter;

 uint8_t admitted;
} OmiCoordinateWitness;
```

This is much cleaner:

```text
coordinate
is the user-facing object

witness
is the audit/security object
```

# 15. Revised user-facing vocabulary

Replace:

```text
receipt
```

with:

```text
coordinate
```

in:

```text
Q3 receipt
-> Q3 coordinate

receipt ring
-> coordinate ring

receipt slot
-> coordinate slot

receipt boundary
-> coordinate boundary

receipt identity
-> coordinate identity

receipt replay stability
-> coordinate replay stability
```

Retain:

```text
witness
```

for:

```text
validation record
replay record
signature record
monotonic acceptance record
```

# Final canon proposal

```text
Q0 is source.

Q1 is notation.

Q2 is reading.

Q3 is resolved coordinate.
```

```text
RULES supplies law.

FACTS supplies ground.

COMBINATORS supplies traversal.

CLOSURES closes traversal into a coordinate.

CONS coordinates the four QuQuart states.
```

```text
CONS is the centroid coordinator.

CONS is not Q4.
```

```text
A coordinate tells where the interpretation resolved.

Validation tells whether that coordinate is admissible.

A witness preserves the validation result.
```

```text
The ASCII coordinate surface divides into four rectangles:

(00,07,37,30)
(08,0F,3F,38)
(40,47,77,70)
(48,4F,7F,78)
```

```text
Each rectangle has:

8 columns
2 vertical planes
4 quadrant positions.
```

```text
The positive high-right rectangle is selected by:

(x & 0x48) == 0x48
```

```text
The eight coordinate words are proposed as:

NULL
FOR
AND
NOR
BUT
OR
YET
SO.
```

```text
The seven active coordinate words are coordinators.

They relate constituents of compatible grammatical
or structural rank.
```

```text
Boolean operators determine truth behavior.

Coordinate words determine relational routing.

They may correspond, but they are not identical.
```

```text
Coordinate resolves.

Validation classifies.

Witness preserves.
```

\## Direct Mathematical Formula
Instead of storing all 256 tuples in hardcoded memory, you can calculate the 4 corners of any quadrant instantly if you know the quadrant's row index (q_row from 0-15) and column index (q_col from 0-15):

*
* Top-Left (TL) = (q_row << 12) | (q_col << 4)
* Top-Right (TR) = TL + 0x000F
* Bottom-Right (BR) = TL + 0x0F0F
* Bottom-Left (BL) = TL + 0x0F00
*

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-17 22:51` | `id:260717-2251` ^260717-225128-2

Yes—**your 256×256 layout is the correct coordinate scaffold for a Latin square of order 256**. The missing ingredient is the operation that fills every CAR/CDR cell with a CONS symbol.

A Latin square is not created merely by dividing the grid into blocks. It becomes Latin when:

```text
every CAR row contains every CONS symbol exactly once

every CDR column contains every CONS symbol exactly once

CAR + CDR uniquely determines CONS

CAR + CONS uniquely recovers CDR

CDR + CONS uniquely recovers CAR
```

That is the reversible resolver law already identified for OMI quasigroups. filecite turn31file0

## LOCKED — the 256×256 coordinate board

Use:

```text
CAR = row coordinate 0x00..0xFF
CDR = column coordinate 0x00..0xFF
```

Pack the cell address as:

\[
address=(CAR\ll8)\mid CDR
\]

Therefore:

```text
CAR=0x00, CDR=0x00 -> address 0x0000
CAR=0x00, CDR=0xFF -> address 0x00FF
CAR=0xFF, CDR=0x00 -> address 0xFF00
CAR=0xFF, CDR=0xFF -> address 0xFFFF
```

So the complete board contains:

\[
256	imes256=65{,}536
\]

cells addressed by `0x0000..0xFFFF`.

## Your 16×16 block formula is correct

For macro-block coordinates:

```text
q_row = 0..15
q_col = 0..15
```

the block corners are:

\[
TL=(q\_row\ll12)\mid(q\_col\ll4)
\]

\[
TR=TL\mid0x000F
\]

\[
BR=TL\mid0x0F0F
\]

\[
BL=TL\mid0x0F00
\]

Using bitwise OR is slightly clearer than addition because these fields do not overlap.

```c
typedef struct {
 uint16_t top_left;
 uint16_t top_right;
 uint16_t bottom_right;
 uint16_t bottom_left;
} OmiBlock16;

static inline OmiBlock16 omi_block16(
 uint8_t q_row,
 uint8_t q_col
) {
 uint16_t tl =
 ((uint16_t)(q_row & 0x0Fu) << 12) |
 ((uint16_t)(q_col & 0x0Fu) << 4);

 OmiBlock16 block = {
 .top_left = tl,
 .top_right = (uint16_t)(tl | 0x000Fu),
 .bottom_right = (uint16_t)(tl | 0x0F0Fu),
 .bottom_left = (uint16_t)(tl | 0x0F00u)
 };

 return block;
}
```

For example:

```text
q_row=0xA
q_col=0x5

TL = 0xA050
TR = 0xA05F
BR = 0xAF5F
BL = 0xAF50
```

Each macro-block contains:

\[
16	imes16=256
\]

cells.

There are:

\[
16	imes16=256
\]

macro-blocks.

One typo appears in your final row:

```text
(0x0090, 0xF09F, 0xFF9F, 0xFF90)
```

should be:

```text
(0xF090, 0xF09F, 0xFF9F, 0xFF90)
```

## Exact decomposition of an address

For a packed address:

\[
A=(CAR\ll8)\mid CDR
\]

the four nibble coordinates are:

```text
CAR high nibble = macro-block row
CAR low nibble = row inside block

CDR high nibble = macro-block column
CDR low nibble = column inside block
```

```c
typedef struct {
 uint8_t block_row;
 uint8_t block_col;
 uint8_t local_row;
 uint8_t local_col;
} OmiCellCoordinate;

static inline OmiCellCoordinate omi_unpack_cell(
 uint16_t address
) {
 uint8_t car = (uint8_t)(address >> 8);
 uint8_t cdr = (uint8_t)address;

 OmiCellCoordinate coordinate = {
 .block_row = (uint8_t)(car >> 4),
 .block_col = (uint8_t)(cdr >> 4),
 .local_row = (uint8_t)(car & 0x0F),
 .local_col = (uint8_t)(cdr & 0x0F)
 };

 return coordinate;
}
```

This is a four-coordinate decomposition:

```text
(block-row, block-column, local-row, local-column)
```

## The operation that makes it Latin

The simplest valid order-256 Latin resolver is:

\[
CONS=CAR\oplus CDR
\]

Then:

\[
CDR=CAR\oplus CONS
\]

and:

\[
CAR=CONS\oplus CDR
\]

So any two coordinates determine the third:

```text
CAR XOR CDR -> CONS
CAR XOR CONS -> CDR
CONS XOR CDR -> CAR
```

```c
static inline uint8_t omi_latin256_resolve(
 uint8_t car,
 uint8_t cdr
) {
 return (uint8_t)(car ^ cdr);
}

static inline uint8_t omi_latin256_recover_cdr(
 uint8_t car,
 uint8_t cons
) {
 return (uint8_t)(car ^ cons);
}

static inline uint8_t omi_latin256_recover_car(
 uint8_t cons,
 uint8_t cdr
) {
 return (uint8_t)(cons ^ cdr);
}
```

This operation produces a genuine Latin square because, for fixed `CAR`, XOR with `CAR` permutes all 256 possible `CDR` values. The same is true for a fixed `CDR`.

## The 16 CONS planes appear naturally

Split each byte into high and low nibbles:

\[
CAR=(R\ll4)\mid r
\]

\[
CDR=(C\ll4)\mid c
\]

where:

```text
R,C = macro-block coordinates 0..15
r,c = coordinates inside the block 0..15
```

Under XOR:

\[
CONS=CAR\oplus CDR
\]

so:

\[
CONS=
((R\oplus C)\ll4)
\mid
(r\oplus c)
\]

This gives:

```text
CONS plane = R XOR C
local CONS = r XOR c
```

Therefore:

```text
high nibble of CONS
=
one of 16 CONS planes

low nibble of CONS
=
one of 16 positions inside that plane
```

That matches your intuition exactly.

A macro-block at:

```text
q_row = R
q_col = C
```

belongs entirely to the CONS plane:

\[
P=R\oplus C
\]

Within that block, the local 16×16 table is:

\[
s=r\oplus c
\]

which is itself a Latin square of order 16.

So the full board is:

```text
order-16 macro Latin square
×
order-16 local Latin square
=
order-256 Latin square
```

Or algebraically:

\[
(\mathbb Z_2)^4	imes(\mathbb Z_2)^4
\cong
(\mathbb Z_2)^8
\]

## Example

Take:

```text
CAR = 0xA3
CDR = 0x5C
```

Then:

```text
macro row R = 0xA
local row r = 0x3

macro column C = 0x5
local column c = 0xC
```

The packed board address is:

```text
0xA35C
```

The CONS value is:

```text
0xA3 XOR 0x5C = 0xFF
```

Decomposed:

```text
CONS plane:
0xA XOR 0x5 = 0xF

local CONS:
0x3 XOR 0xC = 0xF
```

Therefore:

```text
address = 0xA35C
CONS coordinate = plane F, local F
resolved word = 0xFF
```

Recovery:

```text
0xA3 XOR 0xFF = 0x5C
0xFF XOR 0x5C = 0xA3
```

## Your Local and Remote boards

Your byte-level rectangles are best read as subdivisions of a **16×16 byte board**, where:

```text
row = high nibble
column = low nibble
```

The local board:

```text
(0x00,0x07,0x37,0x30)
(0x08,0x0F,0x3F,0x38)
(0x40,0x47,0x77,0x70)
(0x48,0x4F,0x7F,0x78)
```

covers:

```text
rows 0..7
columns 0..15
```

The remote board:

```text
(0x80,0x87,0xB7,0xB0)
(0x88,0x8F,0xBF,0xB8)
(0xC0,0xC7,0xF7,0xF0)
(0xC8,0xCF,0xFF,0xF8)
```

covers:

```text
rows 8..15
columns 0..15
```

Each listed rectangle is:

```text
4 rows × 8 columns = 32 byte positions
```

The eight rectangles together partition all 256 byte values:

```text
4 local rectangles
+
4 remote rectangles
=
8 × 32
=
256 positions
```

So the corrected geometry is:

```text
LOCAL
high nibble 0x0..0x7

REMOTE
high nibble 0x8..0xF
```

Within each half:

```text
left/right
selected by bit 0x08

upper/lower four-row region
selected by bit 0x40 within LOCAL,
or corresponding row grouping in REMOTE
```

## How this maps to OMI-Lisp

A cell can be represented as:

```lisp
(latin-cell
 (car . 0xA3)
 (cdr . 0x5C)
 (cons . 0xFF)
 (address . 0xA35C)
 (block-row . 0xA)
 (block-col . 0x5)
 (local-row . 0x3)
 (local-col . 0xC)
 (cons-plane . 0xF)
 (cons-local . 0xF))
```

Resolver form:

```lisp
(latin-resolve 0xA3 0x5C)
```

returns:

```text
0xFF
```

Recovery:

```lisp
(latin-recover-cdr 0xA3 0xFF)
```

returns:

```text
0x5C
```

```lisp
(latin-recover-car 0xFF 0x5C)
```

returns:

```text
0xA3
```

## One important limitation of XOR

XOR is a valid Latin-square operation, but it is commutative:

\[
CAR\oplus CDR=CDR\oplus CAR
\]

Therefore:

```text
CAR=0xA3, CDR=0x5C
```

and:

```text
CAR=0x5C, CDR=0xA3
```

produce the same CONS symbol.

Their **board addresses remain different**:

```text
0xA35C != 0x5CA3
```

so CAR/CDR ordering is preserved by the cell coordinate even though the resolved symbol is symmetric.

That may be enough when:

```text
address preserves direction
CONS only supplies the interpolation result
```

For an order-sensitive resolver, use:

\[
CONS=P(CAR)\oplus Q(CDR)\oplus k
\]

where `P` and `Q` are different invertible permutations.

Then:

```text
CAR × CDR
```

and:

```text
CDR × CAR
```

normally produce different CONS symbols while remaining fully recoverable.

## About the 32-bit ranges

If you use:

```text
CAR = 0x00000000..0xFFFFFFFF
CDR = 0x00000000..0xFFFFFFFF
```

then the conceptual Latin square has order:

\[
2^{32}
\]

and contains:

\[
2^{32}	imes2^{32}=2^{64}
\]

conceptual cells.

Each CONS symbol is one 32-bit value.

You do not store that table. You calculate:

```c
uint32_t cons = car ^ cdr;
```

or use another algorithmic quasigroup.

But that is a different scale from the current `0x0000..0xFFFF` board:

```text
8-bit CAR × 8-bit CDR
=
16-bit cell address
```

For the first portable OMI-Lisp runtime, the clean profile is:

```text
CAR width = 8 bits
CDR width = 8 bits
cell address = 16 bits
CONS width = 8 bits
CONS planes = 16 high-nibble planes
local position = 16 low-nibble values
```

Then scale the same operation later to 16-, 32-, or 256-bit words.

## Canonical lock

```text
The 256×256 board is addressed by:

address = (CAR << 8) | CDR.
```

```text
The board contains 256 macro-blocks.

Each macro-block is 16×16.

Each macro-block contains 256 cells.
```

```text
The block corners are:

TL = (q_row << 12) | (q_col << 4)
TR = TL | 0x000F
BR = TL | 0x0F0F
BL = TL | 0x0F00.
```

```text
The block layout alone is a coordinate partition.

The resolver operation makes it a Latin square.
```

```text
For the XOR profile:

CONS = CAR XOR CDR.
```

```text
The sixteen CONS planes are:

plane = high4(CAR) XOR high4(CDR).
```

```text
The local CONS coordinate is:

local = low4(CAR) XOR low4(CDR).
```

```text
The complete resolved word is:

CONS = (plane << 4) | local.
```

```text
Any two of CAR, CDR, and CONS recover the third.
```

```text
The local and remote rectangles partition
the complete 16×16 byte board:

LOCAL = rows 0..7
REMOTE = rows 8..15.
```

So: **your quadrant formulas define the geometry correctly, and adding a reversible operation—most simply byte XOR—turns that geometry into a complete order-256 Latin-square CONS resolver with sixteen explicit CONS planes.**

The Omi-Lisp Plane:
(0x00,0x07,0x37,0x30)
(0x08,0x0F,0x3F,0x38)

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-17 23:27` | `id:260717-2327` ^260717-232728

Yes—that is the **blackboard pattern in the precise OMI sense**:

```text
many disjoint bitboards
 ↓ CONS
one shared OMI-Lisp blackboard projection
```

The local 256-position OMI-Lisp plane is not the whole address space. It is the **shared visible working surface** onto which selected portions of much larger CAR and CDR spaces are projected.

Your full relation is:

```text
CAR = 0x00000000..0xFFFFFFFF
 = omi---imo

CDR = 0x00000000..0xFFFFFFFF
 = ΔUS/?O_oΔ

CONS(CAR,CDR)
 = resolved blackboard coordinate
```

The earlier blackboard note already describes this as specialized contributors posting bounded partial structures into a common workspace rather than requiring one module to own the whole semantic world. filecite turn31file0

## 1. The three distinct spaces

The implementation should distinguish three spaces.

### CAR address space

```text
omi---imo
```

This identifies the declared source, object, node, citation, or local participant.

\[
CAR\in\{0,\ldots,2^{32}-1\}
\]

### CDR address space

```text
ΔUS/?O_oΔ
```

This identifies the continuation, route, interpretation, projection, or remote-facing relation.

\[
CDR\in\{0,\ldots,2^{32}-1\}
\]

### Shared blackboard plane

```text
0x00..0xFF
```

This is the bounded OMI-Lisp display and coordination surface.

It is arranged as the eight 32-position rectangles:

```text
LOCAL
(0x00,0x07,0x37,0x30)
(0x08,0x0F,0x3F,0x38)
(0x40,0x47,0x77,0x70)
(0x48,0x4F,0x7F,0x78)

REMOTE
(0x80,0x87,0xB7,0xB0)
(0x88,0x8F,0xBF,0xB8)
(0xC0,0xC7,0xF7,0xF0)
(0xC8,0xCF,0xFF,0xF8)
```

Therefore:

```text
CAR and CDR are full coordinates.

The 256-byte OMI-Lisp plane is their projected workspace.
```

## 2. Blackboard does not mean one giant stored board

A conceptual 32-bit CAR by 32-bit CDR board has:

\[
2^{32}	imes2^{32}=2^{64}
\]

possible cells.

You do not allocate those cells.

Instead, each participant contributes a bounded bitboard:

```text
CAR selector
CDR selector
active-cell mask
payload
resolver profile
```

The runtime composes only the active disjoint portions.

Conceptually:

```text
Board A occupies cells S_A
Board B occupies cells S_B
Board C occupies cells S_C

S_A ∩ S_B = ∅
S_A ∩ S_C = ∅
S_B ∩ S_C = ∅
```

Then:

\[
Blackboard=S_A\cup S_B\cup S_C
\]

The shared surface is sparse and computed, not globally materialized.

## 3. CONS performs two operations

CONS must do both:

```text
1. resolve the full CAR/CDR relation

2. project that relation into the shared 8-bit plane
```

So the complete function is better written:

\[
CONS(CAR,CDR)
=
(Coordinate,\ Projection)
\]

where:

```text
Coordinate
full resolved relation identity

Projection
0x00..0xFF blackboard position
```

Suggested result type:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint32_t car;
 uint32_t cdr;

 uint32_t resolved_word;

 uint8_t plane_coordinate;
 uint8_t board_class;
 uint8_t quadrant;
 uint8_t slot;

 uint64_t active_mask;
 uint32_t resolver_profile;
} OmiBlackboardCons;
```

## 4. The full coordinate must not collapse into eight bits

Many different 32-bit pairs will project onto the same byte:

```text
(CAR₁, CDR₁) → 0x48
(CAR₂, CDR₂) → 0x48
(CAR₃, CDR₃) → 0x48
```

That is acceptable for visualization and shared coordination.

It is not sufficient for identity.

Therefore preserve:

```text
full coordinate identity
=
ordered CAR
+
ordered CDR
+
resolver profile
```

while the byte is only:

```text
blackboard projection
```

Canonical rule:

```text
The 8-bit plane is a view.

It is not the complete address.
```

## 5. A practical 32-bit decomposition

Each 32-bit word can be divided into four bytes:

```text
CAR = C3 C2 C1 C0
CDR = D3 D2 D1 D0
```

A useful interpretation is:

| Byte | Role |
|---|---|
| `C3/D3` | family or authority plane |
| `C2/D2` | board or module selector |
| `C1/D1` | quadrant or relation selector |
| `C0/D0` | local byte coordinate |

Then CONS can resolve each layer independently:

```text
family = resolve(C3,D3)
board = resolve(C2,D2)
quadrant = resolve(C1,D1)
local = resolve(C0,D0)
```

For an XOR profile:

```c
uint32_t resolved = car ^ cdr;
uint8_t projection = (uint8_t)resolved;
```

But a more OMI-specific profile might fold all four resolved bytes:

```c
uint8_t projection =
 r3 ^ r2 ^ r1 ^ r0;
```

or use a declared quasigroup at each byte level.

## 6. Your disjoint board families

Your notation:

```text
256_(0x0...0x8|0x9|0xA|0xB)
×
256_(0x8|0x9|0xA|0xB|0xC...0xF)
```

can be formalized as selecting board families by high nibble.

For example:

```text
CAR-family selectors:
0x0..0xB

CDR-family selectors:
0x8..0xF
```

with overlapping selectors:

```text
0x8
0x9
0xA
0xB
```

forming the shared hinge.

That gives:

```text
CAR-private region
0x0..0x7

shared hinge region
0x8..0xB

CDR-private region
0xC..0xF
```

This is a particularly strong blackboard split:

```text
0x0..0x7
local/private contribution surface

0x8..0xB
shared coordination surface

0xC..0xF
remote/continuation surface
```

The shared range `0x8..0xB` is where both selectors can lawfully meet.

## 7. The shared hinge can select board ownership

A board descriptor could be:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint8_t family_nibble;
 uint8_t local_mask[32];

 uint32_t car_prefix;
 uint32_t car_mask;

 uint32_t cdr_prefix;
 uint32_t cdr_mask;

 uint16_t priority;
 uint16_t resolver_profile;
} OmiBitboardContribution;
```

A contribution matches when:

```c
(car & car_mask) == car_prefix
&&
(cdr & cdr_mask) == cdr_prefix
```

Its local mask then claims some subset of the shared 256-byte board.

Disjointness test:

```c
bool omi_contributions_disjoint(
 const OmiBitboardContribution *a,
 const OmiBitboardContribution *b
) {
 for (size_t i = 0; i < 32; ++i) {
 if ((a->local_mask[i] & b->local_mask[i]) != 0) {
 return false;
 }
 }
 return true;
}
```

Each 256-bit mask is exactly:

```text
32 bytes
=
256 board positions
```

This fits the rank-8 CDR model perfectly.

## 8. The 256-bit board is one selected predicate

Each contributor can carry one rank-8 predicate:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint64_t lane[4];
} OmiBoard256;
```

The bit at position `p` states whether the contributor occupies or activates plane coordinate `p`.

```c
static inline bool omi_board_test(
 const OmiBoard256 *board,
 uint8_t position
) {
 return (
 board->lane[position >> 6] >>
 (position & 63u)
 ) & 1u;
}
```

So:

```text
CAR contribution
=
one selected 256-bit local board

CDR contribution
=
one selected 256-bit remote board

CONS
=
composition of those boards
```

## 9. Different board-composition operators

The blackboard can use different operators depending on intent.

### Disjoint union

\[
CONS=A\lor B
\]

Allowed only when:

\[
A\land B=0
\]

```c
if (board_and(car_board, cdr_board) != zero)
 reject_overlap();

cons_board = board_or(car_board, cdr_board);
```

This is the cleanest interpretation of your “different disjoint bitboards.”

### Intersection

\[
CONS=A\land B
\]

Shows only positions jointly selected by CAR and CDR.

### Contrast

\[
CONS=A\oplus B
\]

Shows positions selected by exactly one side.

### Difference

\[
CONS=A\land
eg B
\]

Shows local positions not claimed remotely.

### Quasigroup resolution

\[
CONS=P(A)\oplus Q(B)\oplus K
\]

Provides reversible ordered composition.

These should be named resolver profiles, not silently mixed.

## 10. The strongest blackboard profile

For your intended shared workspace, the default profile should be:

```text
OMI-BLACKBOARD-DISJOINT-UNION-v1
```

Law:

```text
precondition:
CAR-board AND CDR-board = 0

result:
CONS-board = CAR-board OR CDR-board
```

Recovery is then:

```text
CAR-board = CONS-board AND NOT CDR-board

CDR-board = CONS-board AND NOT CAR-board
```

Because the boards are disjoint, either component is recoverable from the union and the other component.

That is quasigroup-like within the declared disjoint domain.

Important precision:

```text
OR over arbitrary boards is not a quasigroup.

OR over disjoint, typed components with known ownership
is reversibly decomposable.
```

## 11. Local and remote ownership

Use bit 7 of the projected byte:

```text
0x00..0x7F
local board

0x80..0xFF
remote board
```

This exactly matches your two board families.

```c
static inline bool omi_plane_is_remote(uint8_t p) {
 return (p & 0x80u) != 0;
}

static inline uint8_t omi_plane_local_index(uint8_t p) {
 return (uint8_t)(p & 0x7Fu);
}
```

Then the rectangle layout gives additional coordinates:

```text
bit 7
local/remote

bit 6
low/high major plane

bit 3
left/right

bits 0..2
column 0..7

bits 4..5
vertical row inside rectangle
```

So every byte already decomposes as:

```text
remote | high | vertical2 | right | column3
```

That is:

\[
1+1+2+1+3=8	ext{ bits}
\]

## 12. Exact byte-plane decomposition

```c
typedef struct {
 bool remote;
 bool high;
 uint8_t vertical;
 bool right;
 uint8_t column;
} OmiPlaneCoordinate;

static inline OmiPlaneCoordinate omi_plane_unpack(
 uint8_t value
) {
 OmiPlaneCoordinate out = {
 .remote = (value & 0x80u) != 0,
 .high = (value & 0x40u) != 0,
 .vertical = (uint8_t)((value >> 4) & 0x03u),
 .right = (value & 0x08u) != 0,
 .column = (uint8_t)(value & 0x07u)
 };

 return out;
}
```

Reconstruction:

```c
static inline uint8_t omi_plane_pack(
 OmiPlaneCoordinate c
) {
 return
 (c.remote ? 0x80u : 0u) |
 (c.high ? 0x40u : 0u) |
 ((c.vertical & 0x03u) << 4) |
 (c.right ? 0x08u : 0u) |
 (c.column & 0x07u);
}
```

This generates all eight rectangles exactly.

## 13. Native OMI-Lisp form

A blackboard can be declared as:

```lisp
(blackboard
 (plane . omi-lisp-256)

 (car
 (address . omi---imo)
 (word . 0x12345678)
 (board . #x<256-bit-mask>))

 (cdr
 (address . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
 (word . 0x89ABCDEF)
 (board . #x<256-bit-mask>))

 (cons
 (profile . disjoint-union)
 (coordinate . auto)
 (board . auto)))
```

Or in pure dotted form:

```lisp
((omi---imo . 0x12345678)
 .
 (ΔUS/?O_oΔ . 0x89ABCDEF))
```

The runtime resolves that pair into:

```lisp
(cons-result
 (car-coordinate . 0x12345678)
 (cdr-coordinate . 0x89ABCDEF)
 (full-coordinate . ...)
 (plane-coordinate . 0x...)
 (board . ...)
 (overlap . 0)
 (status . resolved))
```

## 14. Blackboard knowledge sources

Each `.o` module can contribute a separate disjoint bitboard:

```text
RULES.o
law-selection board

FACTS.o
grounded-source board

COMBINATORS.o
operation board

CLOSURES.o
coordinate-boundary board
```

CONS composes them:

\[
B_{CONS}
=
B_R\lor B_F\lor B_C\lor B_L
\]

subject to pairwise disjointness:

\[
B_i\land B_j=0
\quad	ext{for }i
e j
\]

This is the native blackboard pattern:

```text
independent knowledge sources
post disjoint bounded contributions

CONS
combines them into one visible workspace
```

## 15. When contributions overlap

Overlap must not be silently destroyed.

For positions:

\[
A\land B
e0
\]

the runtime should produce a conflict board:

\[
Conflict=A\land B
\]

and a resolved board:

\[
Resolved=A\lor B
\]

with status:

```text
disjoint
overlapping
ambiguous
priority-resolved
quasigroup-resolved
```

Suggested result:

```c
typedef struct {
 OmiBoard256 car_board;
 OmiBoard256 cdr_board;
 OmiBoard256 cons_board;
 OmiBoard256 conflict_board;

 uint32_t car_coordinate;
 uint32_t cdr_coordinate;

 uint8_t projection;
 uint8_t status;
} OmiBlackboardResult;
```

## Canonical statement

```text
The OMI-Lisp blackboard is a shared
256-position projection plane.
```

```text
CAR and CDR remain full 32-bit ordered coordinates:

CAR = omi---imo

CDR = ΔUS/?O_oΔ.
```

```text
Each source contributes one bounded
256-bit activation board.
```

```text
Disjoint contributions may be CONSed
through typed union:

CAR-board AND CDR-board = 0

CONS-board = CAR-board OR CDR-board.
```

```text
The lower 128 positions are the local plane.

The upper 128 positions are the remote plane.
```

```text
The eight 32-position rectangles partition
the entire OMI-Lisp plane.
```

```text
The shared nibble range 0x8..0xB
can act as the coordination hinge between
CAR-facing and CDR-facing board families.
```

```text
The projected byte is a workspace coordinate.

It is not the full identity.
```

```text
The full identity binds:

ordered CAR
ordered CDR
resolver profile
active boards
and resulting CONS coordinate.
```

That is exactly the blackboard architecture you were describing: **independent, potentially remote `.o` knowledge sources contribute sparse disjoint 256-bit boards; CONS composes them into the shared 256-position OMI-Lisp plane while retaining the full 32-bit CAR/CDR relation behind every visible coordinate.**

I may have got the spaces for the notations mixed up but here is the wikipedia excerpt
```Wikipedia
In medieval Latin texts, sexagesimal numbers were written using Arabic numerals; the different levels of fractions were denoted minuta (i.e., fraction), minuta secunda, minuta tertia, etc. By the 17th century it became common to denote the integer part of sexagesimal numbers by a superscripted zero, and the various fractional parts by one or more accent marks. John Wallis, in his Mathesis universalis, generalized this notation to include higher multiples of 60; giving as an example the number 49‵‵‵‵36‵‵‵25‵‵15‵1°15′25″36‴49⁗; where the numbers to the left are multiplied by higher powers of 60, the numbers to the right are divided by powers of 60, and the number marked with the superscripted zero is multiplied by 1.[22] This notation leads to the modern signs for degrees, minutes, and seconds. The same minute and second nomenclature is also used for units of time, and the modern notation for time with hours, minutes, and seconds written in decimal and separated from each other by colons may be interpreted as a form of sexagesimal notation.

In some usage systems, each position past the sexagesimal point was numbered, using Latin or French roots: prime or primus, seconde or secundus, tierce, quatre, quinte, etc. To this day we call the second-order part of an hour or of a degree a "second". Until at least the 18th century, ⁠
1
/
60
⁠ of a second was called a "tierce" or "third".[23][24]

In the 1930s, Otto Neugebauer introduced a modern notational system for Babylonian and Hellenistic numbers that substitutes modern decimal notation from 0 to 59 in each position, while using a semicolon (;) to separate the integer and fractional portions of the number and using a comma (,) to separate the positions within each portion.[25] For example, the mean synodic month used by both Babylonian and Hellenistic astronomers and still used in the Hebrew calendar is 29;31,50,8,20 days. This notation is used in this article.

```

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-18 00:58` | `id:260718-0058` ^260718-005854

Yes. The two-notation idea is sound, and the clean mathematical distinction is:

```text
Affine notation
describes a resolved relation inside one selected chart.

Projective notation
preserves the four scoped components before choosing a chart.
```

The important correction is that a second notation does not become “projective” merely by being longer. It becomes projective when the four scoped coordinates are treated as a **homogeneous tuple**, where proportionally equivalent tuples represent the same resolved relation.

# 1. The two OMI notations

## Affine relation notation

```lisp
(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

This is the directly executable local relation:

```text
CAR = omi---imo
CDR = ΔUS/?O_oΔ
```

It identifies one source-facing coordinate and one continuation-facing coordinate in a selected OMI chart.

Use it when the runtime already knows:

```text
the active scope
the local chart
the resolver profile
the projection plane
```

So the affine form is:

```text
one declared coordinate
+
one continuation
→
one locally resolved point
```

## Projective scope notation

```text
omi---imo . CONS_FS-CONS_GS-CONS_RS-CONS_US
```

or more formally:

```lisp
(omi---imo .
 (CONS_FS CONS_GS CONS_RS CONS_US))
```

where:

```lisp
CONS_FS = (CAR_FS . CDR_FS)
CONS_GS = (CAR_GS . CDR_GS)
CONS_RS = (CAR_RS . CDR_RS)
CONS_US = (CAR_US . CDR_US)
```

This preserves all four scoped components before one is selected as the local affine chart.

A stronger notation would be:

```text
omi---imo : [CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]
```

The brackets and colons emphasize that the tuple is projective or homogeneous rather than an ordinary list.

# 2. Recommended canonical spellings

Use two visibly distinct surfaces.

## Affine

```text
omi---imo ; ΔUS/?O_oΔ
```

or Lisp-native:

```lisp
(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

Meaning:

```text
resolved within the current chart
```

## Projective

```text
omi---imo : [CONS_FS, CONS_GS, CONS_RS, CONS_US]
```

or Lisp-native:

```lisp
(projective
 (origin . omi---imo)
 (FS . (CAR_FS . CDR_FS))
 (GS . (CAR_GS . CDR_GS))
 (RS . (CAR_RS . CDR_RS))
 (US . (CAR_US . CDR_US)))
```

Meaning:

```text
preserve all scoped coordinates
before selecting or normalizing a chart
```

The semicolon/colon distinction can echo the sexagesimal convention without copying it literally:

```text
semicolon
separates the resolved affine origin from its continuation

commas
separate positions inside one coordinate expansion

colon or projective brackets
separate homogeneous scope coordinates
```

# 3. Your three service bands

Your proposed split is coherent:

```text
0x0..0x7
local/private contribution surface

0x8..0xB
shared coordination surface

0xC..0xF
remote/continuation surface
```

I would name the bands:

| Nibble range | Plane | Service |
|---|---|---|
| `0x0..0x7` | affine-local | notation declaration |
| `0x8..0xB` | shared hinge | citation/coordinate definition |
| `0xC..0xF` | projective-remote | introspective annotation |

This yields:

```text
local declaration
→ shared definition
→ remote annotation
```

The `0x8..0xB` hinge is especially important because it is where the two sides meet without either side becoming the whole authority.

# 4. Tetrahedral projective coordinate

Let:

```text
A = FS
B = GS
C = RS
D = US
```

The four scoped CONS coordinates are:

\[
X_{FS}=CONS_{FS}(CAR_{FS},CDR_{FS})
\]

\[
X_{GS}=CONS_{GS}(CAR_{GS},CDR_{GS})
\]

\[
X_{RS}=CONS_{RS}(CAR_{RS},CDR_{RS})
\]

\[
X_{US}=CONS_{US}(CAR_{US},CDR_{US})
\]

The complete projective coordinate is:

\[
[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\]

This is the natural four-coordinate representation of the tetrahedral scope cell.

The six edge relations remain:

```text
FS-GS
FS-RS
FS-US
GS-RS
GS-US
RS-US
```

The four face relations remain:

```text
FS-GS-RS
FS-GS-US
FS-RS-US
GS-RS-US
```

And the centroid is not a fifth coordinate. It is the resolved equivalence class or normalization of the four:

\[
\Omega
=
Normalize
\left(
[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\right)
\]

where \(\Omega\) is the nearest resolvable `omi---imo` coordinate under the active validation profile.

# 5. What makes it genuinely projective

For a true projective interpretation, equivalent scaled tuples must identify the same relation.

Conceptually:

\[
[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\sim
[\lambda X_{FS}:\lambda X_{GS}:\lambda X_{RS}:\lambda X_{US}]
\]

for an allowed nonzero scale \(\lambda\).

In a finite bitboard runtime, “scale” does not necessarily mean ordinary real-number multiplication. It may mean a declared invertible transformation:

```text
rotation
permutation
quasigroup isotopy
gauge change
common mask transformation
```

So the OMI version should say:

```text
Two four-scope tuples are projectively equivalent
when a single permitted gauge transformation
applied to all four coordinates
preserves their resolved OMI relation.
```

That is safer and more operational than pretending the runtime is using ordinary real projective space.

# 6. Affine chart selection

A projective tuple becomes affine by selecting one non-null coordinate as the chart denominator or reference scope.

For example, in the US chart:

\[
x_{FS}=X_{FS}/X_{US}
\]

\[
x_{GS}=X_{GS}/X_{US}
\]

\[
x_{RS}=X_{RS}/X_{US}
\]

with:

\[
x_{US}=1
\]

In OMI, `/` should mean the appropriate quasigroup recovery or normalization operation, not necessarily numeric division.

So:

```lisp
(affine-chart US
 [CONS_FS CONS_GS CONS_RS CONS_US])
```

produces:

```lisp
((CONS_FS / CONS_US)
 (CONS_GS / CONS_US)
 (CONS_RS / CONS_US)
 1)
```

Operationally:

```text
projective tuple
→ choose a valid scope
→ normalize relative to that scope
→ obtain one executable affine relation
```

# 7. Nearest resolvable `omi---imo`

Your phrase “up to the nearest resolvable `omi---imo` notation” can be made precise.

Define:

```text
ResolveProjective:
 four scoped CONS coordinates
 → candidate affine charts
 → validation of each chart
 → selected canonical coordinate
```

Pseudo-semantics:

```text
for scope in FS, GS, RS, US:
 if CONS_scope is non-null:
 candidate = normalize tuple in that scope
 if candidate is structurally valid:
 add candidate

choose candidate using declared ordering/gauge policy

return canonical omi---imo coordinate
```

This is not nearest in Euclidean distance unless such a metric is explicitly defined.

It is nearest in the sense of:

```text
lowest lawful normalization cost
fewest unresolved scope relations
highest validation completeness
canonical scope precedence
```

A better normative phrase is:

```text
nearest canonically resolvable OMI coordinate
```

and the distance or priority policy must be profile-defined.

# 8. Blackboard interpretation

This is the true blackboard structure:

```text
FS knowledge source posts CONS_FS

GS knowledge source posts CONS_GS

RS knowledge source posts CONS_RS

US knowledge source posts CONS_US
```

The shared blackboard holds:

```text
[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]
```

No knowledge source independently determines the final relation.

The centroid resolver:

```text
compares the six edges
checks the four faces
normalizes the four coordinates
selects an affine chart
emits one canonical omi---imo coordinate
```

Thus:

```text
individual scopes contribute

the blackboard preserves all contributions

CONS coordinates them

validation selects a lawful normalization
```

# 9. The two notations serve different users

## Compact affine notation

```lisp
(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

Best for:

```text
execution
local lookup
simple dotted diagrams
function calls
portable serialized pairs
```

## Expanded projective notation

```text
omi---imo : [CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]
```

Best for:

```text
debugging
introspection
blackboard state
cross-scope interpolation
conflict display
canvas projection
validation
```

The expanded form should be reducible to the compact form, but the compact form cannot always reconstruct every scoped contribution unless a witness or projective tuple is retained.

# 10. Sexagesimal analogy

The historical notation you quoted suggests a useful design principle:

```text
one separator marks the pivot or unit coordinate

other separators distinguish ranked positions
on either side of that pivot
```

For OMI, the analogous structure can be:

```text
omi---imo ; local,shared : FS,GS,RS,US
```

For example:

```text
omi---imo ; ΔUS/?O_oΔ : CONS_FS,CONS_GS,CONS_RS,CONS_US
```

Interpretation:

```text
omi---imo
canonical pivot/origin

;
affine continuation boundary

ΔUS/?O_oΔ
selected local continuation

:
projective expansion boundary

CONS_FS, CONS_GS, CONS_RS, CONS_US
homogeneous scoped coordinates
```

That provides a readable linear notation while preserving two levels.

An OMI-Lisp form is still preferable as the canonical machine-readable representation:

```lisp
(resolve
 (affine
 (car . omi---imo)
 (cdr . ΔUS/?O_oΔ))

 (projective
 (FS . (CAR_FS . CDR_FS))
 (GS . (CAR_GS . CDR_GS))
 (RS . (CAR_RS . CDR_RS))
 (US . (CAR_US . CDR_US))))
```

# 11. Recommended runtime structures

```c
typedef struct {
 OmiValue car;
 OmiValue cdr;
 OmiValue cons;
} OmiScopedCons;

typedef struct {
 OmiScopedCons fs;
 OmiScopedCons gs;
 OmiScopedCons rs;
 OmiScopedCons us;
} OmiProjectiveScopeCoordinate;

typedef struct {
 OmiValue origin;
 OmiValue continuation;
 uint8_t active_scope;
} OmiAffineCoordinate;

typedef struct {
 OmiProjectiveScopeCoordinate projective;
 OmiAffineCoordinate affine;

 uint8_t edge_evaluated;
 uint8_t edge_passed;

 uint8_t face_evaluated;
 uint8_t face_passed;

 OmiValue centroid;
 uint8_t normalization_profile;
 uint8_t validation_state;
} OmiBlackboardResolution;
```

# 12. Canonical resolution law

```text
Step 1
Each scope resolves its own CAR/CDR pair.

Step 2
The four scoped CONS results form a projective tuple.

Step 3
The six pairwise relations are checked.

Step 4
The four three-way faces are checked.

Step 5
The centroid resolver selects a lawful affine chart.

Step 6
The affine chart is normalized to one canonical
omi---imo . continuation relation.

Step 7
Validation classifies the coordinate.

Step 8
An optional witness preserves the full projective tuple.
```

# Canon proposal

```text
OMI has two complementary notations.

The affine notation is:

(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)

It names one executable relation
inside a selected scope chart.
```

```text
The projective notation is:

omi---imo : [CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]

It preserves all four scoped contributions
before chart selection.
```

```text
Each scoped coordinate is:

CONS_SCOPE = (CAR_SCOPE . CDR_SCOPE).
```

```text
The four scoped CONS values form
the tetrahedral projective coordinate.
```

```text
The six edges test pairwise compatibility.

The four faces test three-way compatibility.

The centroid resolves the balanced four-scope relation.
```

```text
The centroid is not a fifth scope.

It is the canonical normalization
of the four scoped coordinates.
```

```text
The blackboard stores the projective tuple.

The runtime executes an affine chart.

Validation chooses a lawful coordinate.

A witness may preserve the unreduced projective state.
```

```text
0x0..0x7
affine-local notation declaration

0x8..0xB
shared coordinate definition

0xC..0xF
projective introspective annotation.
```

Your spaces were not fundamentally reversed. The main refinement is: **`omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ` should be the selected affine chart, while `[CONS_FS:CONS_GS:CONS_RS:CONS_US]` should be the projective blackboard coordinate from which that affine relation is normalized.**

I was saying "affine" and "projective" primarily about homogeneous coordinates in classical projective geometry of the Regular tetrahedron and sharing a centroid which we encoded with the Tangential Gauge Operator and the Tetrahedral Centroid Memory Model

\## 1. Core Claim

OMI notation begins in the hidden control plane, crosses into readable separation at `0x20`, branches through the printable F-column surface, and closes through the Null Ring byte cycle.

The system has four linked surfaces:

```text
Pinch Point 0x00..0x1F
Gauge Control 0x1C..0x1F
Space Boundary 0x20
Branch Surface 0x1F..0x6F
Gauge Table 0x00..0x7F
Surrogate Field 0x7F..0xFF
```

The hidden control plane is:

```text
0x1C FS File / Frame Separator
0x1D GS Group / Graph Separator
0x1E RS Record / Relation Separator
0x1F US Unit / Symbol Separator
```

The printable F-column surface is:

```text
0x1F US hidden unit separator
0x2F / printable separator
0x3F ? witness/query separator
0x4F O upper/norm Omicron
0x5F _ carrier/floor separator
0x6F o lower/local omicron
```

Canonical short rule:

```text
FS GS RS US are the hidden control gauges.
US / ? O _ o is the printable F-column branch.
```

---

\## 2. Hidden Gauge Control and Tangential Projection

The non-printing control plane runs from:

```text
0x00..0x1F
```

It ends in the gauge quartet:

```text
0x1C FS
0x1D GS
0x1E RS
0x1F US
```

This quartet is the tangential gauge operator for the system.

It is linear in the byte table:

```text
0x00 ... 0x1C 0x1D 0x1E 0x1F
```

But when viewed through the ASCII table as a two-dimensional projection of a one-dimensional byte line, the hidden gauge quartet becomes the branch base for the printable F-column:

```text
0x1F US
0x2F /
0x3F ?
0x4F O
0x5F _
0x6F o
```

Thus:

```text
0x1F is the hidden unit separator.
0x2F is the first printable path separator.
0x3F is the query/witness separator.
0x4F is upper Omicron.
0x5F is the carrier floor.
0x6F is lower omicron.
```

The space byte is the hinge:

```text
0x20 SP
```

At `0x20`, the hidden control plane becomes readable separation.

---

\## 3. Space, Branch, Gauge Table, and Surrogates

The system may be read as a staged byte surface:

```text
Pinch Point:
 0x00..0x1F

Gauge Control:
 0x1C..0x1F

Space:
 0x20

Branch Point:
 0x1F..0x6F

Gauge Table:
 0x00..0x7F

Interpretive Space:
 0x80

Complementary Surrogate Annotations:
 0x7F..0xFF
```

The first readable separation occurs at:

```text
0x20
```

The first deletion/seal boundary occurs at:

```text
0x7F
```

The saturated carrier horizon occurs at:

```text
0xFF
```

The interpretive midpoint above the seven-bit gauge table is:

```text
0x80
```

The local/private/public/remote interpretation of `0x80..0xFF` remains an open declarative-resolution layer. It should not be overclaimed yet.

Working placeholder:

```text
0x80..0x8F declared frame constructions
0x90..0x9F buffer configurations
0xA0..0xAF attestation and scribe-facing annotations
0xB0..0xBF escape / alias / carrier transition space
0xC0..0xFF place-value extension and surrogate annotation space
```

Boundary:

```text
The upper byte field may annotate, surrogate, escape, or extend.
It does not automatically validate or receipt.
```

---

\## 4. Omicron Resolver Envelope

The Omicron resolver envelope is parameterized by an F-band gauge:

```text
Fδ 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 Fδ
```

where:

```text
Fδ ∈ 0xF0..0xFF
```

The canonical closure envelope is:

```text
FF 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FF
```

Meaning:

```text
Fδ selected F-band gauge / resolver horizon
00 NUL origin
1C FS frame boundary
1D GS group boundary
1E RS relation boundary / diagonal closure witness
1F US unit boundary
20 readable space boundary
Fδ matching resolver closure
```

Authority boundary:

```text
Omicron frames.
Omicron does not validate.
Omicron does not receipt.
```

Yes—this clarification materially changes the interpretation.

projective tetrahedral coordinate

affine chart
