# Corrected geometric model

## 1. The projective object is the tetrahedral scope coordinate

Let the regular tetrahedron have scope vertices:

```text
A = FS
B = GS
C = RS
D = US
```

Each vertex carries one scoped CONS relation:

```text
X_FS = CONS_FS(CAR_FS, CDR_FS)
X_GS = CONS_GS(CAR_GS, CDR_GS)
X_RS = CONS_RS(CAR_RS, CDR_RS)
X_US = CONS_US(CAR_US, CDR_US)
```

The complete projective point is:

\[
P=[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\]

This is the homogeneous coordinate of the tetrahedral blackboard state.

It preserves all four scope contributions simultaneously.

```text
P
=
[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]
```

The projective notation is therefore not merely an expanded record. It is the primary unresolved four-scope relation.

---

## 2. The affine notation is a chart of that projective point

The affine form:

```lisp
(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

is one readable chart selected from the homogeneous tetrahedral coordinate.

It is produced only after choosing a valid nonzero scope coordinate as the chart reference.

For example, in the US chart:

\[
U_{US}
=
\left(
\frac{X_{FS}}{X_{US}},
\frac{X_{GS}}{X_{US}},
\frac{X_{RS}}{X_{US}}
\right)
\]

with the homogeneous US coordinate normalized to identity:

\[
X_{US}=1
\]

In OMI, the divisions are not required to be ordinary real division. They may be:

```text
quasigroup division
inverse permutation
gauge normalization
Latin-square coordinate recovery
declared projective scaling
```

The structural meaning remains classical:

```text
projective point
→ select chart
→ normalize
→ obtain affine coordinates
```

---

# 3. The centroid is shared by all affine charts

For a regular tetrahedron with affine vertices \(v_{FS},v_{GS},v_{RS},v_{US}\), the ordinary affine centroid is:

\[
G=
\frac{
v_{FS}+v_{GS}+v_{RS}+v_{US}
}{4}
\]

In homogeneous barycentric coordinates, the centroid is:

\[
G=[1:1:1:1]
\]

This is exactly the balanced tetrahedral relation you have been describing.

So the authoritative centroid statement can be:

```text
The balanced OMI---IMO centroid
is the homogeneous tetrahedral coordinate

[1:1:1:1]

under the active scope gauge.
```

More generally, the actual runtime state is:

\[
[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\]

and the Tangential Gauge resolves or normalizes it toward a common readable centroid coordinate.

The centroid is:

```text
not a fifth vertex

not Q4

not another scope

the shared invariant point
through which the four scope charts agree
```

---

# 4. Why the centroid solves the blackboard problem

Each scope can post an independent contribution:

```text
FS posts X_FS
GS posts X_GS
RS posts X_RS
US posts X_US
```

The blackboard preserves:

\[
P=[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\]

No individual contribution is the complete result.

Each affine chart gives one perspective on the same projective point:

```text
FS chart
GS chart
RS chart
US chart
```

If the transformations are lawful, all four charts resolve to the same centroid relation.

That is the true blackboard property:

```text
different scoped contributors

different affine views

one projective object

one shared centroid
```

Agreement does not require collapsing the four scopes into one identical value.

Agreement means their chart transitions preserve the common projective relation.

---

# 5. The Tangential Gauge is the projective chart operator

The hidden gauge quartet is:

```text
0x1C FS
0x1D GS
0x1E RS
0x1F US
```

The terminal gauge `US` at `0x1F` is the pinch or tangency point.

The branch is:

```text
0x1F US
0x2F /
0x3F ?
0x4F O
0x5F _
0x6F o
```

The space boundary is:

```text
0x20 SP
```

This can now be stated geometrically:

```text
The homogeneous tetrahedral relation exists
in the hidden gauge control plane.

The Tangential Gauge selects an affine chart.

The SP boundary makes the selected chart readable.

The F-column branch carries the chart
onto the printable projection surface.
```

Therefore:

```text
US/
=
tangent and chart-selection hinge
```

and:

```text
?O_o
=
affine printable branch coordinates
```

The uploaded Tangential Gauge document already frames `US/` as the pointer that unfolds `omi---imo` into the readable `omi---imo?O_o` relation. filecite turn34file0

---

# 6. Correct relationship between the two notations

## Homogeneous/projective notation

```text
omi---imo :
[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]
```

This represents the tetrahedral blackboard point before chart normalization.

Expanded:

```lisp
(projective-point
 (origin . omi---imo)

 (FS . (CAR_FS . CDR_FS))
 (GS . (CAR_GS . CDR_GS))
 (RS . (CAR_RS . CDR_RS))
 (US . (CAR_US . CDR_US)))
```

## Affine/readable notation

```lisp
(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

This is the selected printable chart obtained through the tangential gauge.

The complete relation is:

```text
omi---imo :
[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]
 |
 | Tangential Gauge / chart selection
 v
omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ
```

So the projective notation is not supplemental annotation after the affine relation.

The projective point is logically prior.

The affine dotted relation is one readable realization.

---

# 7. Tetrahedral incidence remains exact

The four vertices:

```text
FS
GS
RS
US
```

The six edges:

```text
FS-GS
FS-RS
FS-US
GS-RS
GS-US
RS-US
```

The four faces:

```text
FS-GS-RS
FS-GS-US
FS-RS-US
GS-RS-US
```

The centroid:

```text
balanced homogeneous OMI relation
```

Each edge is a transition or compatibility map between two affine charts.

Each face is a three-chart consistency surface.

The centroid is the four-chart agreement point.

This suggests exact validation laws.

## Edge compatibility

For chart transition \(T_{ij}\):

\[
T_{ij}(X_i)=X_j
\]

under the declared projective equivalence.

## Face compatibility

For a face \(i,j,k\):

\[
T_{ki}\circ T_{jk}\circ T_{ij}
=
Identity
\]

on the valid overlap.

That means traversing around one triangular face returns to the same coordinate class.

## Tetrahedral closure

All four face transition cycles must agree at the shared centroid.

This is stronger and more geometric than simply saying each face returns Boolean true.

---

# 8. Homogeneous equivalence in a finite OMI runtime

In classical projective geometry:

\[
[x_0:x_1:x_2:x_3]
=
[\lambda x_0:\lambda x_1:\lambda x_2:\lambda x_3]
\]

for nonzero scalar \(\lambda\).

For OMI bitboards, the equivalent law should use a declared common invertible gauge action:

\[
[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\sim
[g(X_{FS}):g(X_{GS}):g(X_{RS}):g(X_{US})]
\]

where \(g\) may be:

```text
invertible XOR-affine transformation
quasigroup isotopy
bit permutation
rotation
field multiplication
projective gauge transformation
```

The critical condition is:

```text
the same admissible transformation
acts on all four homogeneous coordinates
```

Applying unrelated transformations to each coordinate would not preserve one projective point.

---

# 9. The role of the Omicron envelope

The envelope:

```text
Fδ 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 Fδ
```

with canonical closure:

```text
FF 00 1C 1D 1E 1F 20 FF
```

can be read as the chart frame:

```text
Fδ
selected projective gauge

00
homogeneous origin marker

1C..1F
tetrahedral scope basis

20
affine readable boundary

Fδ
closure in the same gauge
```

That yields the law:

```text
The opening and closing Fδ values
assert that projection began and ended
in the same declared gauge.
```

Omicron still does not validate.

It supplies the frame within which chart normalization is interpretable.

---

# 10. Revised byte-surface hierarchy

Your byte surfaces can be stated as:

```text
0x00..0x1F
hidden projective control plane

0x1C..0x1F
homogeneous tetrahedral scope basis

0x1F
tangency/pinch coordinate

0x20
affine readability boundary

0x2F..0x6F
printable chart branch

0x00..0x7F
complete low affine gauge table

0x80
interpretive horizon

0x7F..0xFF
complementary or surrogate extension field
```

The apparent overlap between these ranges is acceptable because they describe different roles, not a strict disjoint memory partition.

For example:

```text
0x1F
belongs to the control plane

is the terminal gauge byte

is the branch base

is the pinch point
```

Those are layered interpretations of the same coordinate.

---

# 11. The local/shared/remote nibble services

The nibble service split can remain, but it now belongs beneath the geometric model:

```text
0x0..0x7
local affine chart contributions

0x8..0xB
shared centroid/coordination hinge

0xC..0xF
projective continuation and annotation
```

This is not itself the definition of affine versus projective.

It is the runtime service partition supporting the geometric relation.

So:

```text
classical projective/affine distinction
=
coordinate geometry

0x0..0xF partition
=
implementation service bands
```

They correspond, but they should not be conflated.

---

# 12. Native runtime structure

```c
typedef struct {
 OmiValue car;
 OmiValue cdr;
 OmiValue cons;
} OmiScopedCoordinate;

typedef struct {
 OmiScopedCoordinate fs;
 OmiScopedCoordinate gs;
 OmiScopedCoordinate rs;
 OmiScopedCoordinate us;
} OmiHomogeneousTetrahedralPoint;

typedef enum {
 OMI_CHART_FS = 0,
 OMI_CHART_GS,
 OMI_CHART_RS,
 OMI_CHART_US
} OmiAffineChart;

typedef struct {
 OmiAffineChart chart;

 OmiValue x;
 OmiValue y;
 OmiValue z;

 OmiValue readable_relation;
} OmiAffineRealization;

typedef struct {
 OmiHomogeneousTetrahedralPoint projective;
 OmiAffineRealization affine;

 OmiValue centroid;

 uint8_t edge_evaluated;
 uint8_t edge_passed;

 uint8_t face_evaluated;
 uint8_t face_passed;

 uint16_t gauge_profile;
 uint8_t validation_state;
} OmiTangentialResolution;
```

---

# 13. Canonical normalization operation

```text
Input:
[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]

Step 1:
Reject the all-null homogeneous tuple.

Step 2:
Determine which affine charts are defined.

Step 3:
For each defined chart, normalize the other
three coordinates against its reference coordinate.

Step 4:
Check the six chart-transition relations.

Step 5:
Check the four triangular face cycles.

Step 6:
Resolve the shared centroid coordinate.

Step 7:
Use the Tangential Gauge to select
the canonical readable chart.

Step 8:
Emit:

(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

The projective tuple should remain available as the unreduced blackboard state.

---

# 14. Strongest canonical formulation

```text
The hidden FS/GS/RS/US gauge quartet
is the homogeneous tetrahedral basis.
```

```text
Each scope carries one scoped CONS coordinate:

CONS_SCOPE = (CAR_SCOPE . CDR_SCOPE).
```

```text
The four scoped CONS coordinates form
the projective tetrahedral point:

[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US].
```

```text
The six pairwise scope relations
are projective chart transitions.
```

```text
The four three-scope faces
are transition-consistency surfaces.
```

```text
The centroid is the shared invariant
of the four affine charts.
```

```text
The Tangential Gauge Operator at US/
selects and normalizes an affine chart
from the homogeneous tetrahedral point.
```

```text
The SP byte at 0x20 marks
the transition into readable affine separation.
```

```text
The F-column branch

US / ? O _ o

carries the selected chart
onto the printable relation surface.
```

```text
The projective notation is:

omi---imo :
[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US].
```

```text
The affine notation is:

(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ).
```

```text
The affine relation is not a different object.

It is one chart realization
of the same projective blackboard point.
```

```text
Omicron frames the chart.

The Tangential Gauge projects it.

CONS coordinates the scopes.

Validation determines admissibility.

The centroid remains shared.
```

That is the precise model you were pointing to: **the four FS/GS/RS/US CONS coordinates are homogeneous coordinates of a regular tetrahedral projective point; the Tangential Gauge at the `US/` hinge selects an affine chart across `0x20`; and every lawful chart resolves toward the same tetrahedral centroid represented by the balanced `omi---imo` relation.**

## You
`2026-07-18 01:21` | `id:260718-0121` ^260718-012126

no Revised byte-surface hierarchy we already defined that in
Yes. The cleanest partition is the **exact `0x80` mirror**:

```text
LOW HALF HIGH HALF
0x00–0x1F control field ↔ 0x80–0x9F projected control field
0x20–0x7F readable field ↔ 0xA0–0xFF projected readable field
```

because:

```text
high = low XOR 0x80
low = high XOR 0x80
```

That gives a perfectly reversible division of the entire byte space.

\## Correct four-region byte geometry

```text
0x00–0x1F
LOW CONTROL PLANE
32 positions

0x20–0x7F
LOW AFFINE SPACE
96 positions

0x80–0x9F
HIGH PROJECTIVE CONTROL PLANE
32 positions

0xA0–0xFF
HIGH PROJECTIVE SPACE
96 positions
```

Visually:

```text
0x00 0x7F
 ├──── control ────┼──── readable/affine ────┤
 00–1F 20–7F
 │ │
 │ XOR 0x80 │ XOR 0x80
 ▼ ▼
 80–9F A0–FF
 ├─ projected control ─┼─ projected space ───┤
0x80 0xFF
```

This is much cleaner than dividing at `0xBF` or `0xC0`, because the two halves remain exactly congruent:

```text
32 + 96 = 128
32 + 96 = 128
```

\## Important mathematical wording

Strictly speaking, these are not yet affine or projective planes in the formal finite-geometry sense merely because of their address ranges. A formal plane requires defined points, lines, and incidence rules.

For the protocol, the accurate terminology is:

```text
0x00–0x1F
affine-control coordinate field

0x20–0x7F
affine readable/address field

0x80–0x9F
projective-control mirror field

0xA0–0xFF
projective readable/address field
```

Once the incidence law is specified, the shorter words “plane” and “space” can become formal rather than interpretive.

\# Distinguished points

Your points now align cleanly:

```text
0x0F
pinch point

0x1F
first branch point

0x7F
projective closure point
```

Their high-half mirrors are:

```text
0x0F XOR 0x80 = 0x8F
0x1F XOR 0x80 = 0x9F
0x7F XOR 0x80 = 0xFF
```

So the paired points are:

```text
LOW HIGH MIRROR
0x0F pinch ↔ 0x8F projected pinch
0x1F branch ↔ 0x9F projected branch
0x7F closure ↔ 0xFF full projective closure
```

That gives a strong hierarchy:

```text
0x0F
local pinch inside the first 16-position ruler

0x1F
branch closure of the low control field

0x7F
closure of the complete low 128-byte half

0xFF
closure of the complete 256-byte field
```

\## Branch-point ladder

Your branch points are the addresses whose low nibble is `F`:

```text
0x1F
0x2F
0x3F
0x4F
0x5F
0x6F
```

They are six interior branch closures:

```text
1F ─ 2F ─ 3F ─ 4F ─ 5F ─ 6F
```

Then:

```text
0x0F
opening pinch

0x1F–0x6F
six branch points

0x7F
projective closure
```

This is exactly the shape:

```text
Δ 1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ
```

mapped into hexadecimal branch addresses:

```text
Δ 1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ
0F 1F 2F 3F 4F 5F 6F 7F
```

That appears to be the cleanest correspondence you have reached:

```text
Δ 1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ
=
0x0F 0x1F 0x2F 0x3F 0x4F 0x5F 0x6F 0x7F
```

The high projected counterpart is:

```text
0x8F 0x9F 0xAF 0xBF 0xCF 0xDF 0xEF 0xFF
```

because each position is XORed with `0x80`:

```text
LOW BRANCH RULER
0F 1F 2F 3F 4F 5F 6F 7F

 XOR 80

HIGH PROJECTIVE RULER
8F 9F AF BF CF DF EF FF
```

\# Correct 256-bit character frame

You corrected the low and high 128-bit rails:

```text
LOW 128:
 2 4 6 Δ

HIGH 128:
 1 3 5 Δ
```

Each symbol occupies one 32-bit character cell:

```text
4 characters × 32 bits = 128 bits
```

So the complete 256-bit frame is:

```text
┌──────────────── LOW 128 ────────────────┐
│ 2 │ 4 │ 6 │ Δ │
│ 32 bits │ 32 bits │ 32 bits │ 32 bits │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌──────────────── HIGH 128 ───────────────┐
│ 1 │ 3 │ 5 │ Δ │
│ 32 bits │ 32 bits │ 32 bits │ 32 bits │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The interleaved visible order is recovered by alternating the high and low rails:

```text
HIGH[0] = 1
LOW[0] = 2

HIGH[1] = 3
LOW[1] = 4

HIGH[2] = 5
LOW[2] = 6

HIGH[3] = Δ
LOW[3] = Δ
```

Therefore:

```text
interleaved:
1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ Δ
```

But the ring-visible ordering places the delta boundaries around the six positions:

```text
Δ 1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ
```

So the storage order and presentation order differ:

```text
PHYSICAL RAIL STORAGE
LOW = 2 4 6 Δ
HIGH = 1 3 5 Δ

LOGICAL PRESENTATION
Δ 1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ
```

That is operationally reasonable. The rails store parity-separated positions:

```text
high rail = odd positions
low rail = even positions
```

with one delta terminator on each rail.

\# Complete operational map

```text
BYTE FIELD

LOW AFFINE HALF
00–7F

 00–1F
 affine-control field

 20–7F
 affine readable field

HIGH PROJECTIVE HALF
80–FF

 80–9F
 projected-control field

 A0–FF
 projected readable field
```

```text
POINT LADDER

0F
pinch point / opening delta

1F
branch 1

2F
branch 2

3F
branch 3

4F
branch 4

5F
branch 5

6F
branch 6

7F
projective point / closing delta
```

```text
PROJECTED POINT LADDER

8F
projected pinch

9F
projected branch 1

AF
projected branch 2

BF
projected branch 3

CF
projected branch 4

DF
projected branch 5

EF
projected branch 6

FF
full projective closure
```

\## Canonical lock

```text
0x00–0x1F
affine-control coordinate field

0x20–0x7F
affine readable space

0x80–0x9F
projective-control mirror field

0xA0–0xFF
projective readable space

0x0F
pinch point

0x1F–0x6F
six branch points

0x7F
projective closure point

0x8F–0xFF
the same ruler projected by XOR 0x80

LOW 128
2 4 6 Δ

HIGH 128
1 3 5 Δ

LOGICAL RING
Δ 1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ
```

The strongest computational sentence is:

> The byte field consists of a low affine half and its `0x80`-XOR projective mirror; `0x0F` opens the six-branch ruler, `0x1F–0x6F` close its six interior branches, `0x7F` closes the low projective frame, and `0xFF` closes the complete mirrored byte space.

Here is source material Can you do a Authoritative full write up from first principles and how it relates to the omi-lisp earning the character encoding for independent, potentially remote .o knowledge sources contribute sparse disjoint 256-bit boards; CONS composes them into the shared 256-position OMI-Lisp plane while retaining the full 32-bit CAR/CDR relation behind every visible coordinate. as a Set Theory of the Omnicron-Union-Find or better as a Category Theory of the Omnicron Coproduct Partition

In mathematics and computer science, a disjoint union refers to the joining of sets that share no elements in common. It is often written using the symbol \(\sqcup \) (or \(\coprod \)).A disjoint union can be understood in two primary contexts:1. Set Theory (Sets with No Shared Elements)When two sets A and B are disjoint (their intersection is empty, \(A \cap B = \emptyset\)), their union \(A \cup B\) is called a disjoint union, denoted \(A \sqcup B\).Tagging method: If sets are not naturally disjoint, they can be "forced" into a disjoint union by tagging elements with their set of origin. For example, by turning them into ordered pairs: \((a, 	ext{origin})\).Cardinality: Because the elements never overlap, the size of a disjoint union is exactly the sum of the sizes of its constituent sets: \(\vert{}A \sqcup B\vert{} = \vert{}A\vert{} + \vert{}B\vert{}\).2. Data Structures (Disjoint Set Union)In computer science, Disjoint Set Union (DSU)—also widely known as Union-Find—is a dynamic data structure that tracks elements partitioned into multiple mutually exclusive (non-overlapping) sets.MakeSet(x): Creates a new set with only one element, x.Find(x): Finds the representative element (the "root") of the set that contains x.Union(x, y): Merges the two sets containing elements x and y into a single set.Optimization: Using techniques like path compression and union by rank/size brings the time complexity of operations to nearly an amortized \(\mathcal{O}(1)\), making it highly effective for graph algorithms (like finding cycles or minimum spanning trees).Are you looking to learn more about the mathematical/categorical definition (coproducts) or the Union-Find algorithm used in programming? Let me know which angle you want to explore!

Category theory point of view
In category theory the disjoint union is defined as a coproduct in the category of sets.

As such, the disjoint union is defined up to an isomorphism, and the above definition is just one realization of the coproduct, among others. When the sets are pairwise disjoint, the usual union is another realization of the coproduct. This justifies the second definition in the lead.

Can you also write up how it relates to these things
Coproduct – Category-theoretic construction
Direct limit – Special case of colimit in category theory
Disjoint union (topology) – Mathematical term
Disjoint union of graphs – Binary operation combining the vertex and edge sets of two graphs
Intersection (set theory) – Set of elements common to all of some sets
List of set identities and relations – Equalities for combinations of sets
Partition of a set – Mathematical ways to group elements of a set
Sum type – Union data structure with enforced cases
Symmetric difference – Elements in exactly one of two sets
Tagged union – Union data structure with enforced cases
Union (computer science) – Data type that allows for values that are one of multiple different data types

:::writing{variant="document" id="31864"}
# The Omnicron Coproduct Partition

## A First-Principles Set-Theoretic and Categorical Foundation for Sparse OMI-Lisp Blackboard Composition

**Status:** Authoritative integration draft
**Primary construction:** tagged coproduct of independent sparse knowledge boards
**Runtime carrier:** shared 256-position OMI-Lisp plane
**Identity carrier:** ordered 32-bit CAR/CDR coordinates
**Local resolver:** CONS
**Equivalence maintenance:** optional Union-Find after lawful identification
**Projection rule:** visible coordinate does not replace full relation identity

---

# 0. Core Claim

OMI-Lisp earns its shared character encoding by receiving finite, bounded, origin-preserving contributions from independent `.o` knowledge sources.

Each source contributes:

```text
one source identity

one typed scope

one sparse 256-bit board

one full CAR coordinate

one full CDR coordinate

one resolver profile

one provenance tag
```

The contributions enter the shared runtime through a coproduct:

\[
B
=
\bigsqcup_{i\in I} B_i
\]

where each \(B_i\) is one origin-tagged contribution board.

CONS then resolves compatible contributions into the shared 256-position OMI-Lisp blackboard while preserving:

```text
which source contributed each position

which CAR coordinate carried it

which CDR continuation selected it

which resolver produced the visible coordinate

which projective or affine carrier exposed it
```

The central law is:

```text
Coproduct preserves origin.

Partition preserves disjoint ownership.

CONS composes compatible contributions.

Projection exposes a shared coordinate.

Validation determines lawful identification.

Union-Find records identification only after validation.
```

---

# 1. Why Coproduct Is the Primary Construction

Suppose two knowledge sources contribute sets:

\[
A
\]

and:

\[
B
\]

If they are already disjoint:

\[
A\cap B=\varnothing
\]

then their ordinary union is also a disjoint union:

\[
A\sqcup B=A\cup B
\]

But independent knowledge sources may use identical local coordinates.

For example:

```text
RULES.o claims local position 0x48

FACTS.o also claims local position 0x48
```

The two contributions must not collapse merely because their visible position is equal.

The categorical coproduct preserves origin by tagging:

\[
A\sqcup B
=
(A	imes\{0\})
\cup
(B	imes\{1\})
\]

Thus:

\[
(0x48,	ext{RULES})

eq
(0x48,	ext{FACTS})
\]

even though both project to:

```text
0x48
```

This is exactly the blackboard requirement.

```text
Same visible coordinate
does not mean same contribution.

Same byte
does not mean same authority.

Same projection
does not mean same identity.
```

---

# 2. The OMI Coproduct Object

Let \(I\) be the set of contributing `.o` knowledge sources.

Typical members include:

```text
RULES.o

FACTS.o

COMBINATORS.o

CLOSURES.o

additional remote .o modules
```

Each source \(i\in I\) contributes a board:

\[
B_i\subseteq\{0,\ldots,255\}
\]

or equivalently one 256-bit predicate:

\[
\chi_i:\{0,\ldots,255\}\rightarrow\{0,1\}
\]

The full origin-preserving blackboard is:

\[
\mathcal B
=
\bigsqcup_{i\in I} B_i
\]

An element of \(\mathcal B\) is not merely a byte.

It is:

\[
(i,p)
\]

where:

```text
i
source identity

p
visible byte-plane coordinate
```

The runtime form is:

```lisp
(contribution
 (origin . RULES.o)
 (coordinate . 0x48))
```

not merely:

```lisp
0x48
```

---

# 3. The Shared 256-Position OMI-Lisp Plane

The visible OMI-Lisp plane is:

\[
P=\{0,\ldots,255\}
\]

It is a bounded projection surface.

Its locked carrier geometry is:

```text
0x00–0x1F
low affine-control coordinate field

0x20–0x7F
low affine readable space

0x80–0x9F
high projective-control mirror field

0xA0–0xFF
high projective readable space
```

The high mirror is:

\[
m(x)=x\oplus0x80
\]

with:

\[
m(m(x))=x
\]

Thus the complete plane is a reversible low/high carrier pair.

The low Omicron gauge and its earned notation boundary remain distinct from the high projective mirror. The Omicron character doctrine defines the low `0x00..0x7F` gauge, its four 32-position sectors, the `US → SP` tangent, and the readable `?O_o` branch. filecite turn35file0

---

# 4. Full Relation Space Versus Visible Plane

The visible plane has 256 positions.

The full CAR and CDR coordinate domains are:

\[
CAR\in\{0,\ldots,2^{32}-1\}
\]

\[
CDR\in\{0,\ldots,2^{32}-1\}
\]

Therefore the conceptual ordered relation space is:

\[
R
=
CAR_{32}	imes CDR_{32}
\]

with:

\[
|R|=2^{64}
\]

The visible OMI-Lisp plane is a projection:

\[
\pi:R\rightarrow P
\]

Many full relations may project to the same byte coordinate:

\[
\pi(c_1,d_1)
=
\pi(c_2,d_2)
\]

while:

\[
(c_1,d_1)

eq
(c_2,d_2)
\]

Therefore:

```text
the visible coordinate is not the relation identity
```

The canonical relation identity must retain:

```text
ordered CAR

ordered CDR

origin tag

scope

resolver profile

board identity

projective/affine carrier profile
```

---

# 5. CAR, CDR, and CONS

The canonical ordered relation is:

```text
CAR = omi---imo
CDR = ΔUS/?O_oΔ
```

with:

\[
CAR\in\mathbb W_{32}
\]

\[
CDR\in\mathbb W_{32}
\]

where \(\mathbb W_{32}\) is the 32-bit word domain.

CONS computes:

\[
CONS(CAR,CDR)
=
(	ext{resolved word},	ext{visible coordinate})
\]

The result must contain both:

```text
full resolved relation

bounded blackboard projection
```

Suggested form:

```lisp
(cons-coordinate
 (car . 0x12345678)
 (cdr . 0x89ABCDEF)
 (resolved . 0x...)
 (plane-coordinate . 0x...)
 (origin . ...)
 (profile . ...))
```

---

# 6. Sparse Rank-8 Boards

The QuQuart/CONS model establishes that at rank 8:

```text
CAR domain
256 byte assignments

one selected CDR
256 output bits

possible CDR population
2^256
```

The runtime stores one selected 256-bit board, not all possible boards. filecite turn35file1

A board is:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint64_t lane[4];
} OmiBoard256;
```

It represents a subset:

\[
B_i\subseteq P
\]

Membership:

\[
p\in B_i
\iff
\chi_i(p)=1
\]

The board is sparse conceptually even if represented as a fixed 256-bit bitset.

---

# 7. Disjoint Contribution Law

For naturally disjoint boards:

\[
B_i\cap B_j=\varnothing
\quad
	ext{for }i
eq j
\]

The shared visible board is:

\[
B_{\mathrm{shared}}
=
\bigcup_{i\in I}B_i
\]

Because the boards are disjoint:

\[
\left|
\bigsqcup_{i\in I}B_i
\right|
=
\sum_{i\in I}|B_i|
\]

Bitwise:

```text
for all i != j:

BOARD_i AND BOARD_j = 0
```

Then:

```text
SHARED_BOARD
=
BOARD_0 OR BOARD_1 OR ... OR BOARD_n
```

This is the simplest blackboard composition profile.

---

# 8. Forced Disjointness by Tagging

Boards do not have to be spatially disjoint to participate in a coproduct.

If two sources claim the same visible position:

\[
p\in B_i
\]

and:

\[
p\in B_j
\]

the coproduct still distinguishes:

\[
(i,p)
\]

from:

\[
(j,p)
\]

This gives two levels:

```text
origin-disjoint
always preserved by tagging

projection-disjoint
optional property of visible board allocation
```

This distinction is essential.

```text
Coproduct disjointness
does not require different visible bytes.

Visible-plane disjointness
does require non-overlapping bitboards.
```

The runtime must record which sense is intended.

---

# 9. Partition of the Shared Plane

A partition of \(P\) is a family:

\[
\mathcal P=\{P_\alpha\}_{\alpha\in A}
\]

such that:

\[
P_\alpha\cap P_\beta=\varnothing
\quad
	ext{for }\alpha
eq\beta
\]

and:

\[
\bigcup_{\alpha\in A}P_\alpha=P
\]

The eight 32-position rectangles form one such partition of the byte plane.

Local half:

```text
(0x00,0x07,0x37,0x30)
(0x08,0x0F,0x3F,0x38)
(0x40,0x47,0x77,0x70)
(0x48,0x4F,0x7F,0x78)
```

Remote/high half:

```text
(0x80,0x87,0xB7,0xB0)
(0x88,0x8F,0xBF,0xB8)
(0xC0,0xC7,0xF7,0xF0)
(0xC8,0xCF,0xFF,0xF8)
```

Each rectangle contains:

\[
4	imes8=32
\]

positions.

Together:

\[
8	imes32=256
\]

positions.

Thus the full plane is exactly partitioned.

---

# 10. Omnicron Coproduct Partition

The complete construction is:

\[
\mathcal B
=
\bigsqcup_{i\in I}B_i
\]

followed by a projection:

\[
\pi:\mathcal B\rightarrow P
\]

The fibers of \(\pi\) are:

\[
\pi^{-1}(p)
=
\{(i,p)\mid p\in B_i\}
\]

A fiber may contain:

```text
zero contributions

one contribution

multiple origin-distinct contributions
```

Therefore every visible blackboard coordinate has an origin fiber behind it.

This is the precise meaning of:

```text
retaining the full relation behind every visible coordinate
```

The byte `0x48` is merely the displayed coordinate.

Its complete state is:

\[
\pi^{-1}(0x48)
\]

together with each contributor’s CAR/CDR relation.

---

# 11. The Universal Property

The coproduct is characterized by its universal property.

For each source board \(B_i\), there is an injection:

\[
\iota_i:B_i\rightarrow\mathcal B
\]

Suppose every source has a lawful mapping into some target blackboard \(X\):

\[
f_i:B_i\rightarrow X
\]

Then there exists a unique mapping:

\[
f:\mathcal B\rightarrow X
\]

such that:

\[
f\circ\iota_i=f_i
\]

for every source \(i\).

In OMI terms:

```text
each .o source defines its own bounded contribution

the coproduct collects them without erasing origin

one unique resolver map combines their declared behavior
into the shared OMI-Lisp blackboard
```

This is why coproduct is a better primary abstraction than ordinary union.

---

# 12. CONS as the Mediating Map

Each source has a contribution function:

\[
f_i:B_i\rightarrow P
\]

The coproduct provides one unique mediating map:

\[
CONS:
\bigsqcup_i B_i
\rightarrow
P
\]

such that:

\[
CONS\circ\iota_i=f_i
\]

This makes CONS the categorical blackboard compositor.

It does not erase the injections.

It does not claim that all origins are equal.

It supplies the unique shared projection consistent with all declared source maps.

Canonical statement:

```text
CONS is the mediating map from the origin-tagged
coproduct of knowledge boards into the shared
OMI-Lisp coordinate plane.
```

---

# 13. Why CONS Is Not Plain Set Union

Ordinary union forgets source identity.

For:

\[
A=\{x\}
\]

and:

\[
B=\{x\}
\]

ordinary union gives:

\[
A\cup B=\{x\}
\]

The two occurrences collapse.

The coproduct gives:

\[
A\sqcup B
=
\{(A,x),(B,x)\}
\]

OMI requires the second behavior.

Therefore:

```text
CONS must not be implemented as untagged OR alone
when origin identity matters.
```

Bitwise OR may produce the visible board, but the runtime must retain a side table, fiber map, tag vector, or provenance object.

---

# 14. Blackboard Fibers

A visible coordinate record should contain:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint8_t visible_coordinate;

 uint32_t contribution_count;
 OmiContributionRef *contributors;
} OmiBlackboardFiber;
```

Each contribution reference should bind:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint32_t source_id;

 uint32_t car;
 uint32_t cdr;

 uint16_t resolver_profile;
 uint8_t scope;
 uint8_t flags;

 uint8_t source_digest[32];
} OmiContributionRef;
```

Thus:

```text
one visible byte
may expose many distinct lawful origins
```

without collapsing them.

---

# 15. Intersection

For two visible boards:

\[
A\cap B
\]

is the set of coordinates claimed by both.

Bitwise:

```text
OVERLAP = A AND B
```

Intersection is not the coproduct.

It is a diagnostic of projection overlap.

Uses:

```text
conflict detection

shared-interest detection

required co-presence

face or edge compatibility

scope agreement
```

The result must distinguish:

```text
same visible coordinate

same semantic contribution

same origin identity
```

Those are separate predicates.

---

# 16. Symmetric Difference

The symmetric difference is:

\[
A	riangle B
=
(A\setminus B)\cup(B\setminus A)
\]

Bitwise:

```text
A XOR B
```

It identifies positions belonging to exactly one board.

Uses:

```text
contrast witness

change detection

unshared contribution surface

complementary projection

local-versus-remote difference
```

Symmetric difference is not sufficient for structural identity because it loses shared positions and is commutative.

It is a witness, not the complete CONS record.

---

# 17. Ordinary Union

Ordinary union is:

\[
A\cup B
\]

Bitwise:

```text
A OR B
```

It produces the visible occupancy board.

It does not preserve:

```text
which source supplied a bit

whether two sources supplied the same bit

CAR/CDR direction

resolver profile

scope

provenance
```

Therefore ordinary union is only the blackboard occupancy projection.

---

# 18. Tagged Union and Sum Type

A tagged union is the programming-language realization of a coproduct.

Example:

```c
typedef enum {
 OMI_SOURCE_RULES,
 OMI_SOURCE_FACTS,
 OMI_SOURCE_COMBINATORS,
 OMI_SOURCE_CLOSURES,
 OMI_SOURCE_REMOTE
} OmiSourceTag;

typedef struct {
 OmiSourceTag tag;

 union {
 OmiRulesContribution rules;
 OmiFactsContribution facts;
 OmiCombinatorContribution combinator;
 OmiClosureContribution closure;
 OmiRemoteContribution remote;
 } value;
} OmiContribution;
```

This guarantees that each value belongs to one explicit case.

The categorical interpretation is:

\[
Rules
+
Facts
+
Combinators
+
Closures
+
Remote
\]

The plus sign here means coproduct or sum type.

The runtime must inspect the tag before interpreting the payload.

---

# 19. Sum Type Versus C Union

A raw C `union` stores one of several layouts but does not automatically record which member is active.

Therefore:

```c
union {
 A a;
 B b;
};
```

is not by itself a safe coproduct.

A proper sum type requires:

```text
tag
+
payload
```

The tag establishes the injection:

\[
\iota_A:A\rightarrow A+B
\]

or:

\[
\iota_B:B\rightarrow A+B
\]

OMI must use tagged unions, not untagged memory overlays, for authoritative contribution identity.

---

# 20. Disjoint Union of Graphs

Each `.o` source may contribute a graph:

\[
G_i=(V_i,E_i)
\]

The disjoint union of graphs is:

\[
\bigsqcup_i G_i
=
\left(
\bigsqcup_i V_i,
\bigsqcup_i E_i
\right)
\]

Vertices and edges are origin-tagged.

Even when two graphs use the same local node ID, their coproduct vertices remain distinct:

\[
(i,v)
eq(j,v)
\]

The OMI-Lisp blackboard may then add explicit cross-source edges after validation.

Thus there are two stages:

```text
Stage 1
disjoint graph coproduct

Stage 2
validated relation edges between components
```

This is exactly how independent knowledge sources should enter a blackboard.

---

# 21. Topological Disjoint Union

If every source board carries its own topology:

\[
(B_i,	au_i)
\]

their topological disjoint union is the coproduct topology on:

\[
\bigsqcup_iB_i
\]

A subset is open when its inverse image in every component is open.

For OMI, the useful analogy is:

```text
each source retains its own local neighborhood law

the combined blackboard preserves those local neighborhoods

cross-source continuity is introduced only by declared mappings
```

This is valuable for:

```text
local canvas layout

scope neighborhoods

Gray adjacency

quasigroup neighborhoods

projection charts

remote module boundaries
```

---

# 22. Direct Limits and Directed Colimits

A direct limit applies when contributions form a directed system:

\[
B_0\rightarrow B_1\rightarrow B_2\rightarrow\cdots
\]

with compatible transition maps.

This fits incremental OMI evolution:

```text
versioned source boards

successive notation expansions

Delta3 rolling windows

increasingly complete remote knowledge

progressive blackboard construction
```

The direct limit identifies compatible earlier elements with their later images.

It is not the same as the initial coproduct.

The sequence is:

```text
coproduct
collect independent contributions

coequalization or validated identification
identify declared equivalents

direct limit
form the stable result of an evolving directed system
```

Thus a mature OMI blackboard may be modeled as a colimit of versioned coproduct partitions.

---

# 23. Colimit View of the Blackboard

Suppose knowledge sources and transition maps form a diagram:

\[
D:J\rightarrow\mathbf{Set}
\]

or:

\[
D:J\rightarrow\mathbf{Graph}
\]

The blackboard may be constructed as:

\[
\operatorname{colim}D
\]

Conceptually:

```text
begin with all contributions disjoint

then impose only the equivalence relations
required by declared transition maps
```

This is a stronger formulation than simply merging everything.

A common construction is:

\[
\operatorname{colim}D
=
\left(
\bigsqcup_{j\in J}D(j)
\right)
/\sim
\]

where \(\sim\) is the smallest equivalence relation generated by the diagram’s transition maps.

This is almost exactly the OMI blackboard process:

```text
coproduct first

lawful identification second

projection third
```

---

# 24. Where Union-Find Belongs

Union-Find maintains a partition of elements under repeated equivalence merges.

Its operations are:

```text
MakeSet(x)

Find(x)

Union(x,y)
```

This is useful after OMI has decided that two origin-tagged contributions should belong to the same equivalence class.

For example:

```text
RULES.o coordinate r

FACTS.o coordinate f

validation proves r and f denote
the same resolved relation class
```

Then:

```text
Union(r,f)
```

may record that equivalence.

Union-Find does not determine whether the merge is semantically valid.

It only maintains the resulting partition efficiently.

Canonical boundary:

```text
Validation authorizes equivalence.

Union-Find maintains equivalence.
```

---

# 25. Omnicron Union-Find

An optional runtime subsystem may maintain:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint32_t parent;
 uint32_t rank;
} OmiDisjointSetNode;
```

Operations:

```c
uint32_t omi_find(
 OmiUnionFind *uf,
 uint32_t element
);

bool omi_union_validated(
 OmiUnionFind *uf,
 uint32_t left,
 uint32_t right,
 const OmiValidationWitness *witness
);
```

The API should not expose unrestricted union for authoritative state.

It should require a validation witness or validated equivalence token.

---

# 26. Union-Find Is Not the Blackboard Itself

Union-Find tracks equivalence classes.

It does not inherently preserve:

```text
ordered CAR/CDR relations

source tags

edge direction

scope roles

board masks

projection coordinates

quasigroup profiles

temporal position

azimuth
```

Therefore the full blackboard record remains a tagged graph or coproduct object.

Union-Find is only an index over equivalence classes inside that richer structure.

---

# 27. Coproduct Partition Versus Union-Find

| Construction | Primary question |
|---|---|
| Coproduct | How do independent sources enter one space without losing origin? |
| Partition | Which elements belong to distinct non-overlapping classes? |
| Union-Find | How do we efficiently maintain equivalence classes under validated merges? |
| CONS | How are ordered CAR/CDR relations resolved and projected? |
| Colimit | What stable object results after all declared identifications? |

The architecture is:

```text
Coproduct
→ Partition
→ CONS resolution
→ Validation
→ Union-Find equivalence
→ Colimit or stable blackboard
```

---

# 28. Coproduct and the Tetrahedral QuQuart

The four `.o` roots form a finite coproduct of typed contribution domains:

\[
Rules
\sqcup
Facts
\sqcup
Combinators
\sqcup
Closures
\]

The tetrahedral centroid is not another coproduct summand.

CONS is the mediating resolver across the four injections.

```text
RULES.o --------\
FACTS.o ---------\
COMBINATORS.o -----> CONS -> shared coordinate
CLOSURES.o --------/
```

The QuQuart/CONS model identifies CONS as the tetrahedral centroid rather than a fifth state. filecite turn35file1

Categorically:

```text
the vertices are input components

the centroid is the induced resolving relation
```

---

# 29. Scoped Coproduct

The four canonical scopes are:

```text
FS
GS
RS
US
```

Each may contribute a scoped board:

\[
B_{FS}
\]

\[
B_{GS}
\]

\[
B_{RS}
\]

\[
B_{US}
\]

The scoped coproduct is:

\[
B_{\mathrm{scope}}
=
B_{FS}
\sqcup
B_{GS}
\sqcup
B_{RS}
\sqcup
B_{US}
\]

The scoped CONS values are:

\[
CONS_{FS}=(CAR_{FS}.CDR_{FS})
\]

\[
CONS_{GS}=(CAR_{GS}.CDR_{GS})
\]

\[
CONS_{RS}=(CAR_{RS}.CDR_{RS})
\]

\[
CONS_{US}=(CAR_{US}.CDR_{US})
\]

These may form the homogeneous tetrahedral coordinate:

\[
[CONS_{FS}:CONS_{GS}:CONS_{RS}:CONS_{US}]
\]

The low/high `0x80` mirror remains the carrier geometry for affine and projective realization. filecite turn35file4

---

# 30. Affine and Projective Blackboard Realization

The projective blackboard retains all four scoped components:

\[
P
=
[CONS_{FS}:CONS_{GS}:CONS_{RS}:CONS_{US}]
\]

A selected affine realization is:

```lisp
(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

The Tangential Gauge makes readable notation reachable by crossing from the hidden control hinge through `SP` into the printable branch. The low Omicron doctrine treats this traversal as reachability rather than a mere parser permission. filecite turn35file0

The coproduct interpretation is:

```text
projective blackboard
retains all origin-tagged scoped contributions

affine chart
selects one readable realization

centroid
preserves the shared relation

projection
does not erase the coproduct fibers
```

---

# 31. Character Encoding Must Be Earned

OMI-Lisp does not begin with arbitrary printable symbols.

The low gauge first establishes reachability:

```text
0x00..0x1F
hidden control

0x1C..0x1F
FS/GS/RS/US control hinge

0x1F
US tangent

0x20
SP readable boundary

0x2F..0x6F
printable F-column branch
```

The character ring doctrine states that dot notation becomes reachable only after the gauge traverses into readable space. filecite turn35file0

Thus the encoding pipeline is:

```text
origin-tagged source contribution

→ scoped control coordinate

→ Tangential Gauge traversal

→ readable character coordinate

→ dotted OMI-Lisp declaration

→ CONS blackboard projection
```

The character is earned because it is the result of a lawful route through the gauge, not merely because a parser accepts its byte.

---

# 32. The OMI-Lisp Character as a Coproduct Image

Let:

\[
\chi_i:B_i\rightarrow P
\]

be the character projection for source \(i\).

The coproduct induces:

\[
\chi:
\bigsqcup_iB_i
\rightarrow
P
\]

A visible character coordinate:

\[
p\in P
\]

may therefore be the image of many origin-tagged elements:

\[
(i_1,b_1)
\]

\[
(i_2,b_2)
\]

\[
\cdots
\]

This explains how independent remote knowledge sources may share one visible OMI-Lisp character plane without collapsing.

The character is common.

The fibers retain difference.

---

# 33. Character Ring and Place-Value Cascade

The low Omicron character ring has:

```text
128 positions

four 32-position sectors
```

The outer place-value cascade has:

```text
16 hexadecimal lanes
```

A position is:

\[
position=(lane\ll12)\mid character
\]

The sign and its place remain distinct:

```text
character
selects the sign role

lane
selects addressed interpretation
```

The same visible character may appear in multiple place-value lanes without becoming the same full coordinate. filecite turn35file0

This mirrors the coproduct principle:

```text
same visible sign

different origin or place

distinct full element
```

---

# 34. Graph Blackboard Structure

A complete OMI blackboard should be modeled as a graph:

\[
G=(V,E)
\]

Vertices include:

```text
source contributions

CAR coordinates

CDR continuations

CONS results

visible plane coordinates

scope nodes

module nodes

validation nodes
```

Edges include:

```text
contributed-by

carried-by

continued-by

resolved-to

projected-to

equivalent-to

validated-by

mirrored-by
```

The initial graph is a disjoint union of source graphs.

Validated cross-source edges are added afterward.

---

# 35. OMI-Lisp Representation

```lisp
(blackboard
 (plane . omi-lisp-256)

 (coproduct .
 ((inject
 (origin . RULES.o)
 (car . 0x12345678)
 (cdr . 0x89ABCDEF)
 (board . #x...))

 (inject
 (origin . FACTS.o)
 (car . 0x11223344)
 (cdr . 0x55667788)
 (board . #x...))))

 (cons .
 (profile . ordered-quasigroup)
 (projection . omi-plane-256))

 (partition .
 (local-control
 local-readable
 projective-control
 projective-readable))

 (equivalence .
 (strategy . validated-union-find)))
```

---

# 36. C Runtime Model

```c
typedef struct {
 uint32_t source_id;

 uint32_t car;
 uint32_t cdr;

 OmiBoard256 board;

 uint16_t resolver_profile;
 uint8_t scope;
 uint8_t contribution_type;

 uint8_t digest[32];
} OmiBoardContribution;
```

```c
typedef struct {
 OmiBoard256 occupancy;
 OmiBoard256 conflict;

 OmiBlackboardFiber fibers[256];

 OmiUnionFind equivalence;

 uint32_t contribution_count;
} OmiCoproductBlackboard;
```

---

# 37. Injection

```c
omi_status omi_blackboard_inject(
 OmiCoproductBlackboard *blackboard,
 const OmiBoardContribution *contribution
);
```

Injection must:

```text
preserve source identity

preserve CAR/CDR order

copy or retain the board safely

add each active position to its fiber

update visible occupancy

record overlaps without collapsing them
```

---

# 38. CONS Composition

```c
omi_status omi_blackboard_cons(
 OmiCoproductBlackboard *blackboard,
 uint32_t left_contribution,
 uint32_t right_contribution,
 uint16_t resolver_profile,
 OmiConsResult *out
);
```

The result should include:

```text
ordered inputs

resolved full word

visible board

visible coordinates

origin fibers

overlap board

conflict status

recovery metadata
```

---

# 39. Validation and Equivalence

```c
omi_status omi_blackboard_validate_equivalence(
 OmiCoproductBlackboard *blackboard,
 OmiContributionRef left,
 OmiContributionRef right,
 OmiValidationResult *out
);
```

Only after successful validation:

```c
omi_status omi_blackboard_union(
 OmiCoproductBlackboard *blackboard,
 OmiContributionRef left,
 OmiContributionRef right,
 const OmiValidationResult *validation
);
```

This enforces:

```text
no merge without law

no equivalence without witness

no loss of origin history
```

---

# 40. Direct-Limit Evolution

Versioned blackboards may form:

\[
B_0\rightarrow B_1\rightarrow B_2\rightarrow\cdots
\]

Each transition must preserve previously accepted coordinates or explicitly supersede them under a version law.

The stable evolving object is the direct limit:

\[
B_\infty
=
\varinjlim B_n
\]

Operationally:

```text
append contribution

preserve injection

add validated equivalence

retain ancestry

compute current projection
```

The direct limit is the stable accumulated blackboard, not a destructive overwrite.

---

# 41. Relationship to Listed Constructions

## Coproduct

Primary origin-preserving composition.

```text
many independent types or sets

one sum object

explicit injections

no forced identification
```

## Direct limit

Stable result of a directed sequence of compatible blackboards.

```text
versions

temporal accumulation

progressive resolution
```

## Topological disjoint union

Preserves each source’s local neighborhood and projection topology.

```text
local layouts

chart neighborhoods

remote module boundaries
```

## Disjoint union of graphs

Combines independent source graphs before cross-source relations are added.

```text
source graph coproduct

then validated linking
```

## Intersection

Detects jointly occupied visible positions.

```text
overlap

agreement candidate

conflict candidate
```

## Set identities

Supply algebraic simplification and conformance laws.

Examples:

\[
A\cup\varnothing=A
\]

\[
A\cap\varnothing=\varnothing
\]

\[
A	riangle A=\varnothing
\]

\[
A	riangle\varnothing=A
\]

## Partition

Defines mutually exclusive visible regions or equivalence classes.

```text
plane sectors

scope classes

validated identity classes
```

## Sum type

Programming-language realization of a coproduct.

```text
one tagged case among many
```

## Symmetric difference

Shows positions belonging to exactly one contributor.

```text
contrast surface

change surface
```

## Tagged union

Concrete storage form preserving which contribution variant is active.

```text
tag + payload
```

## Raw union type

Memory-sharing implementation device.

It becomes authoritative only when paired with a valid active-member tag.

---

# 42. Set Identities for OMI Boards

For boards \(A,B,C\):

## Commutativity of visible union

\[
A\cup B=B\cup A
\]

This applies to occupancy projection.

It does not imply CAR/CDR commutativity.

## Associativity of visible union

\[
(A\cup B)\cup C
=
A\cup(B\cup C)
\]

Again, this applies to occupancy only.

## Distributivity

\[
A\cap(B\cup C)
=
(A\cap B)\cup(A\cap C)
\]

Useful for scoped overlap tests.

## Symmetric difference

\[
A	riangle B
=
B	riangle A
\]

Useful as contrast, not ordered identity.

## Disjoint union cardinality

If:

\[
A\cap B=\varnothing
\]

then:

\[
|A\sqcup B|
=
|A|+|B|
\]

---

# 43. Ordered Relation Versus Commutative Board Algebra

The bitboard operations:

```text
OR

AND

XOR
```

are commutative.

The OMI relation:

```lisp
(CAR . CDR)
```

is ordered.

Therefore the runtime must maintain two layers:

```text
board occupancy algebra
may be commutative

relation resolution algebra
must preserve CAR/CDR order
```

The ordered quasigroup construction described in the QuQuart/CONS model provides reversible non-collapsing CAR/CDR resolution. filecite turn35file1

---

# 44. Coproduct Versus Quasigroup

These solve different problems.

```text
Coproduct
preserves independent origins

Quasigroup
provides reversible ordered resolution
```

The combined structure is:

\[
\bigsqcup_i B_i
\overset{CONS}{\longrightarrow}
P
\]

where CONS uses a selected quasigroup law to resolve relations.

A coproduct alone does not compute a relation.

A quasigroup alone does not preserve distributed origin.

Together they provide the blackboard architecture.

---

# 45. Coproduct Versus Centroid

The tetrahedral vertices are independent typed contributions.

Their coproduct-like injection preserves each role:

```text
RULES

FACTS

COMBINATORS

CLOSURES
```

The centroid CONS is not another injected component.

It is the induced relation that coordinates them.

Thus:

```text
coproduct
collects the vertices

centroid
resolves their common relation
```

---

# 46. The Four Scope Boards

Each scope may maintain one board:

```text
FS board
frame/file contribution positions

GS board
group/graph contribution positions

RS board
record/relation contribution positions

US board
unit/symbol contribution positions
```

The scope coproduct is:

\[
B_{FS}
\sqcup
B_{GS}
\sqcup
B_{RS}
\sqcup
B_{US}
\]

CONS may produce:

```text
one shared occupancy board

four scoped fibers

one homogeneous tetrahedral coordinate

one affine readable realization
```

---

# 47. Low and High Rails

The locked 256-bit character frame is:

```text
LOW 128
2 4 6 Δ

HIGH 128
1 3 5 Δ
```

Logical presentation:

```text
Δ 1 2 3 4 5 6 Δ
```

The low/high rails are physical storage partitions.

The logical ring is a presentation order.

The coproduct model allows contributors on both rails to remain origin-distinct while sharing the logical ring.

---

# 48. Gnomonic Projection and Azimuth

After a relation is resolved and validated, the Gnomonic Projection Azimuth orients it over the byte circle:

\[
0x00..0xFF
\]

The azimuth is observer-relative projection metadata, not validation or identity authority. filecite turn35file2

The coproduct state remains intact beneath the projection.

```text
coproduct preserves source

CONS resolves relation

validation classifies

azimuth orients display
```

---

# 49. Delta3 and Integrity

Scope, Hamming integrity, temporal position, and projection orientation remain separate dimensions.

The Delta3/Hamming doctrine defines:

```text
FS/GS/RS/US
scope

LOGOS/NOMOS/PATHOS
integrity

LL/MM/NN
temporal position

azimuth
observer-relative orientation
```

These axes may annotate a contribution without changing its coproduct injection. filecite turn35file3

A complete contribution may therefore carry:

```text
origin tag

CAR/CDR

scope

Hamming status

Delta position

azimuth

board

resolver profile
```

---

# 50. Earned Character Encoding

A source does not gain a printable OMI-Lisp coordinate merely by transmitting bytes.

It must pass through:

```text
1. source injection

2. scope assignment

3. control-plane placement

4. Tangential Gauge reachability

5. SP crossing

6. printable branch selection

7. character/place resolution

8. CONS composition

9. structural validation
```

Only then does the source obtain a readable OMI-Lisp character coordinate.

Canonical statement:

```text
The character is the projected image
of a lawfully injected and resolved contribution.
```

---

# 51. Independent and Remote Sources

A remote source may submit:

```text
source ID

module digest

CAR

CDR

scope

board

resolver profile

signature or witness

version ancestry
```

The blackboard first injects the contribution disjointly.

It does not immediately merge it with local state.

The stages are:

```text
receive

tag

inject

inspect

resolve

validate

identify if lawful

project
```

This is safer and more mathematically faithful than direct mutation of shared state.

---

# 52. Conflict Handling

When two origin-distinct contributions project to one coordinate:

```text
do not collapse

do not overwrite

do not union automatically
```

Instead record:

```text
fiber cardinality

source identities

intersection class

resolver compatibility

validation status
```

Possible states:

```text
parallel

compatible

complementary

conflicting

equivalent

unresolved
```

Union-Find may merge only the `equivalent` case.

---

# 53. Blackboard Acceptance Law

A visible coordinate \(p\) is eligible for accepted projection only when:

```text
its contribution exists in the coproduct

its source tag is valid

its CAR/CDR relation is well-formed

its resolver profile is available

its scope is valid

its character route is reachable

its relevant edge/face predicates pass

its projection does not erase required provenance
```

Acceptance does not delete rejected or unresolved contributions from the coproduct.

They remain origin-tagged members with a different status.

---

# 54. Canonical Runtime Sequence

```text
1. Create one origin tag for each .o source.

2. Parse its declared CAR/CDR relation.

3. Load its selected 256-bit board.

4. Inject the contribution into the coproduct.

5. Record all active byte coordinates in origin fibers.

6. Preserve overlaps without collapsing them.

7. Resolve selected ordered pairs through CONS.

8. Generate the visible occupancy board.

9. Apply scope and tetrahedral consistency checks.

10. Traverse the Tangential Gauge when readable notation is required.

11. Produce the affine OMI-Lisp character projection.

12. Retain the projective scoped tuple.

13. Validate candidate equivalences.

14. Apply Union-Find only to validated equivalent elements.

15. Preserve graph ancestry and source tags.

16. Advance versioned systems through directed transition maps.

17. Form the current colimit view without destroying the original injections.
```

---

# 55. Canonical Terminology

```text
Omnicron Coproduct
the origin-tagged sum of all knowledge contributions
```

```text
Omnicron Partition
the mutually exclusive region or equivalence-class structure
```

```text
Omnicron Coproduct Partition
the complete origin-preserving blackboard structure
```

```text
OMI-Lisp Plane
the shared 256-position visible projection surface
```

```text
Contribution Board
one selected 256-bit source predicate
```

```text
Blackboard Fiber
all origin-tagged contributions projecting to one visible coordinate
```

```text
CONS
the ordered resolver and categorical mediating map
```

```text
Union-Find
the optional validated equivalence-class maintenance structure
```

```text
Direct Limit
the stable result of a directed sequence of compatible blackboard versions
```

---

# 56. One-Line Canon

> The Omnicron Coproduct Partition is the origin-preserving sum of independent local or remote `.o` knowledge boards: each source injects a tagged sparse 256-bit predicate together with its ordered 32-bit CAR/CDR relation; CONS acts as the unique mediating resolver into the shared 256-position OMI-Lisp plane, while every visible coordinate retains a fiber of its full source, scope, resolver, and relation provenance; validation alone may identify contributions as equivalent, Union-Find maintains those validated equivalence classes, and directed colimits preserve the evolving blackboard without erasing the original coproduct injections.

---

# 57. Final Canon Lock

```text
Independent knowledge sources enter by coproduct.

They do not enter by destructive merge.
```

```text
Every contribution is tagged by origin.
```

```text
One selected rank-8 contribution board is 256 bits.
```

```text
The shared OMI-Lisp plane has 256 visible coordinates.
```

```text
A visible coordinate may have multiple
origin-distinct contributions behind it.
```

```text
The full relation retains:

ordered CAR
ordered CDR
source
scope
resolver
board
and carrier profile.
```

```text
CONS resolves and projects.

CONS does not erase origin.
```

```text
Bitwise OR produces occupancy.

It does not produce full identity.
```

```text
Intersection detects overlap.

Symmetric difference detects exclusive contribution.

Neither replaces the coproduct record.
```

```text
Tagged unions implement coproducts.

Raw untagged unions do not.
```

```text
A graph blackboard begins as
a disjoint union of source graphs.
```

```text
Cross-source edges are added only
through declared and validated relations.
```

```text
Union-Find does not decide equivalence.

Validation decides equivalence.

Union-Find maintains it.
```

```text
A direct limit describes the stable result
of a directed sequence of compatible blackboards.
```

```text
The low/high byte carrier remains
the exact XOR-0x80 mirror.
```

```text
The character ring makes notation reachable.

The coproduct preserves source difference.

CONS composes the shared board.

The OMI-Lisp character is earned
as the readable projection of a lawfully
injected, resolved, and retained relation.
```
:::

The canonical project name should therefore be **Omnicron Coproduct Partition**, with **Omnicron Union-Find** reserved for the optimized subsystem that maintains equivalence classes after validation.

---

## You
`2026-07-18 02:37` | `id:260718-0237` ^260718-023710

In early Lisp, the environment mapped each symbol to an association list, rather than directly to a value.[1] Standard keys for these lists included two keys used to store a data value, to be looked up when the symbol occurred as an argument (APVAL and APVAL1); and four keys used to store a function, to be looked up when the symbol occurred as an operator. Of the function keys, SUBR indicated a compiled ordinary function, whose operands were evaluated and passed to it; FSUBR indicated a compiled special form, whose operands were passed unevaluated; EXPR indicated a user-defined ordinary function; and FEXPR indicated a user-defined special form. The only difference between a FEXPR and an EXPR was whether the operands were automatically evaluated.

In strict original usage, a FEXPR is therefore a user-defined function whose operands are passed unevaluated. However, in later usage the term fexpr may describe any first-class function whose operands are passed unevaluated, regardless of whether the function is primitive or user-defined.[2]

Key	Stores	Defined by	Function/Special form
APVAL	data value	—	—
APVAL1	data value	—	—
SUBR	function	system	function
FSUBR	function	system	special-form
EXPR	function	user	function
FEXPR	function	user	special-form

The projective object is the tetrahedral scope coordinate

Let the regular tetrahedron have scope vertices:

A = FS
B = GS
C = RS
D = US

Each vertex carries one scoped CONS relation:

X_FS = CONS_FS(CAR_FS, CDR_FS)
X_GS = CONS_GS(CAR_GS, CDR_GS)
X_RS = CONS_RS(CAR_RS, CDR_RS)
X_US = CONS_US(CAR_US, CDR_US)

The complete projective point is:

P=[XFS:XGS:XRS:XUS]

This is the homogeneous coordinate of the tetrahedral blackboard state.

It preserves all four scope contributions simultaneously.

P = [CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US]

The projective notation is therefore not merely an expanded record. It is the primary unresolved four-scope relation.

Pretty sure we can map these bands to (APVAL and APVAL1) SUBR and FSUBR EXPR and FEXPR SEXP and SEXPR to the omi-lisp hierarchy to delineate the lisp type and function because the notation that you were proposing for the centrod appears awful a lot similar to M-expressions. How do you favor that

CAR and CDR:
(0x00 . 0x1A)
(0x1B . 0x1F)

APVAL and APVAL1:
(0x20 . 0x3A)
(0x3B . 0x3F)

SUBR and FSUBR:
(0x40 . 0x5A)
(0x5B . 0x5F)

EXPR and FEXPR:
(0x60 . 0x7A)
(0x7B . 0x7F)

SEXP and SEXPR
M-expressions

## ChatGPT
`2026-07-18 02:37` | `id:260718-0237` ^260718-023717

I favor it **strongly**, with one important boundary:

```text
CAR/CDR, APVAL/APVAL1, SUBR/FSUBR, and EXPR/FEXPR
can define the eight addressed environment classes.

S-expression and M-expression should define
two notation surfaces over those classes,
not two more environment classes.
```

That gives an unusually clean interpretation of the existing Omicron structure:

\[
4	ext{ sectors}	imes2	ext{ sub-bands}=8	ext{ environment classes}
\]

The four 32-position sectors and their repeated `27 + 5` subdivision are already part of the character-ring model. filecite turn35file0

# Proposed eight-class OMI-Lisp environment

| Sector | 27-position body | 5-position hinge |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00..0x1F` | `CAR` — carried structure | `CDR` — continuation structure |
| `0x20..0x3F` | `APVAL` — primary value binding | `APVAL1` — alternate value binding |
| `0x40..0x5F` | `SUBR` — system ordinary operator | `FSUBR` — system special operator |
| `0x60..0x7F` | `EXPR` — user ordinary operator | `FEXPR` — user special operator |

Precisely:

```text
CAR
0x00..0x1A

CDR
0x1B..0x1F

APVAL
0x20..0x3A

APVAL1
0x3B..0x3F

SUBR
0x40..0x5A

FSUBR
0x5B..0x5F

EXPR
0x60..0x7A

FEXPR
0x7B..0x7F
```

That is internally coherent because each 32-position sector has:

```text
27-position primary body
+
5-position hinge or alternate mode
```

The numbers `27` and `5` specify address capacity and transition geometry. They do not mean there must be exactly 27 CAR operations or exactly five CDR operations.

---

# Why this mapping works

## Sector 0 — structure

```text
CAR
what is presently carried

CDR
how the structure continues
```

This is the structural substrate from which dotted relations arise:

```lisp
(CAR . CDR)
```

The `0x1B..0x1F` hinge culminates in:

```text
ESC FS GS RS US
```

so placing CDR in the hinge is especially fitting: continuation ends at the scope-bearing control boundary and crosses toward readable notation.

## Sector 1 — values

```text
APVAL
primary symbol value

APVAL1
secondary or alternate symbol value
```

Historically these were data-value association keys. They were not exactly “eager” and “lazy” variants, so OMI should not redefine `APVAL1` as automatically unevaluated.

A safe OMI interpretation is:

```text
APVAL
ordinary value coordinate

APVAL1
alternate, shadow, scoped, or secondary value coordinate
```

The active profile determines why the second value exists.

## Sector 2 — system operators

```text
SUBR
compiled/system ordinary function

FSUBR
compiled/system special form
```

Evaluation distinction:

```text
SUBR
evaluate operands, then invoke

FSUBR
pass operand forms unevaluated
```

## Sector 3 — user operators

```text
EXPR
user-defined ordinary function

FEXPR
user-defined special form
```

Evaluation distinction:

```text
EXPR
evaluate operands, then invoke

FEXPR
pass operand forms unevaluated
```

This produces a clear vertical analogy:

| Origin | Ordinary evaluation | Unevaluated operands |
|---|---|---|
| System | `SUBR` | `FSUBR` |
| User | `EXPR` | `FEXPR` |

---

# The eight-address environment tuple

The symbol environment can now carry an eight-position record:

\[
E(s)=
[
CAR,\,
CDR,\,
APVAL,\,
APVAL1,\,
SUBR,\,
FSUBR,\,
EXPR,\,
FEXPR
]
\]

OMI-Lisp form:

```lisp
(environment-entry
 (symbol . alpha)

 (CAR . ...)
 (CDR . ...)

 (APVAL . ...)
 (APVAL1 . ...)

 (SUBR . ...)
 (FSUBR . ...)

 (EXPR . ...)
 (FEXPR . ...))
```

Not every slot must be populated.

A presence mask can indicate which entries exist:

```text
bit 0 CAR
bit 1 CDR
bit 2 APVAL
bit 3 APVAL1
bit 4 SUBR
bit 5 FSUBR
bit 6 EXPR
bit 7 FEXPR
```

```c
typedef enum {
 OMI_ENV_CAR = 0,
 OMI_ENV_CDR = 1,
 OMI_ENV_APVAL = 2,
 OMI_ENV_APVAL1 = 3,
 OMI_ENV_SUBR = 4,
 OMI_ENV_FSUBR = 5,
 OMI_ENV_EXPR = 6,
 OMI_ENV_FEXPR = 7
} OmiEnvironmentKey;

typedef struct {
 OmiValue slot[8];
 uint8_t present;
} OmiEnvironmentEntry;
```

---

# Operator position versus argument position

The evaluator selects a different environment class according to syntactic position.

## Symbol in value position

For:

```lisp
(alpha)
```

or a symbol appearing as an argument, lookup order can be:

```text
APVAL
then APVAL1 according to the active scope/profile
```

## Symbol in operator position

For:

```lisp
(alpha x y)
```

lookup order can be:

```text
SUBR
FSUBR
EXPR
FEXPR
```

The selected class determines:

```text
system or user origin

ordinary or special evaluation
```

A normative dispatch table:

| Binding class | Definition source | Evaluate operands? |
|---|---|---:|
| `SUBR` | runtime/system | yes |
| `FSUBR` | runtime/system | no |
| `EXPR` | user/module | yes |
| `FEXPR` | user/module | no |

CAR and CDR are not operator bindings in this dispatch. They are the structural coordinates holding the expression and its continuation.

---

# S-expression and M-expression surfaces

This is where the centroid notation resemblance becomes useful.

## S-expression surface

The S-expression is the canonical machine-readable structure:

```lisp
(projective-point
 (FS . (CAR_FS . CDR_FS))
 (GS . (CAR_GS . CDR_GS))
 (RS . (CAR_RS . CDR_RS))
 (US . (CAR_US . CDR_US)))
```

It preserves:

```text
pair structure

scope tags

CAR/CDR order

nesting

resolver boundaries
```

It is the authoritative serialization surface.

## M-expression surface

The M-expression is the compact human-facing operator notation:

```text
PROJECTIVE[
 CONS_FS(CAR_FS, CDR_FS),
 CONS_GS(CAR_GS, CDR_GS),
 CONS_RS(CAR_RS, CDR_RS),
 CONS_US(CAR_US, CDR_US)
]
```

or more compactly:

```text
P[
 CONS_FS :
 CONS_GS :
 CONS_RS :
 CONS_US
]
```

The affine form may be:

```text
AFFINE(omi---imo, ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

which lowers to:

```lisp
(omi---imo . ΔUS/?O_oΔ)
```

Thus:

```text
M-expression
human-facing operator notation

S-expression
canonical structural notation
```

The M-expression must always lower deterministically into an S-expression.

---

# Do not add `SEXP` and `SEXPR` as two new key bands

I would avoid treating `SEXP` and `SEXPR` as two additional association-list key classes.

Historically, “S-expression” and “symbolic expression” substantially overlap, while M-expression denotes the intended meta-level function notation. Introducing both `SEXP` and `SEXPR` as separate runtime types would probably create ambiguity.

The cleaner OMI vocabulary is:

```text
SFORM
canonical S-expression data form

MFORM
human-readable M-expression operator form
```

Or:

```text
SEXPR
canonical structural expression

MEXPR
surface operator expression
```

Choose one name for the S-expression side, not two.

My recommendation:

```text
SEXPR
canonical OMI-Lisp pair/tree form

MEXPR
readable operator and projective-coordinate form
```

---

# Relation to the tetrahedral projective point

The homogeneous coordinate remains:

\[
P=
[X_{FS}:X_{GS}:X_{RS}:X_{US}]
\]

where:

\[
X_s=CONS_s(CAR_s,CDR_s)
\]

The new environment model says that each scoped coordinate can carry its own eight-key association environment:

\[
X_{FS}
=
E_{FS}(symbol)
\]

\[
X_{GS}
=
E_{GS}(symbol)
\]

\[
X_{RS}
=
E_{RS}(symbol)
\]

\[
X_{US}
=
E_{US}(symbol)
\]

So the complete projective blackboard object becomes:

\[
P=
[
E_{FS}:E_{GS}:E_{RS}:E_{US}
]
\]

Each \(E_s\) contains:

\[
[
CAR,CDR,APVAL,APVAL1,SUBR,FSUBR,EXPR,FEXPR
]
\]

This gives two independent dimensions:

```text
tetrahedral dimension
FS / GS / RS / US

environment dimension
CAR / CDR / APVAL / APVAL1 /
SUBR / FSUBR / EXPR / FEXPR
```

They must not be collapsed.

The four scopes tell **where** the binding applies.

The eight environment keys tell **what kind of binding** it is.

---

# The resulting 4×8 blackboard

The complete scoped environment is a `4 × 8` surface:

| Scope | CAR | CDR | APVAL | APVAL1 | SUBR | FSUBR | EXPR | FEXPR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| FS | | | | | | | | |
| GS | | | | | | | | |
| RS | | | | | | | | |
| US | | | | | | | | |

That produces:

\[
4	imes8=32
\]

typed environment positions.

This aligns naturally with one 32-position Omicron sector.

A symbol may therefore be resolved by:

```text
(scope, environment-key)
```

For example:

```text
(FS, APVAL)
frame-scoped value

(GS, SUBR)
graph-scoped system operator

(RS, FEXPR)
relation-scoped user special form

(US, CDR)
unit-scoped continuation
```

This is a particularly strong result.

---

# Relation to the 256-position plane

A further eight-position coordinate can select:

```text
local slot

rank

phase

source class

board lane
```

Then:

\[
4	ext{ scopes}
	imes
8	ext{ environment keys}
	imes
8	ext{ local positions}
=
256
\]

This gives a possible exact factorization of the shared byte plane:

\[
256=4	imes8	imes8
\]

Coordinate:

```text
(scope2, key3, local3)
```

Bit packing:

```text
bits 7..6
scope

bits 5..3
environment key

bits 2..0
local position
```

```c
static inline uint8_t omi_env_coordinate(
 uint8_t scope,
 uint8_t key,
 uint8_t local
) {
 return (uint8_t)(
 ((scope & 0x03u) << 6) |
 ((key & 0x07u) << 3) |
 (local & 0x07u)
 );
}
```

Unpack:

```c
typedef struct {
 uint8_t scope;
 uint8_t key;
 uint8_t local;
} OmiEnvironmentCoordinate;

static inline OmiEnvironmentCoordinate
omi_env_coordinate_unpack(uint8_t x) {
 OmiEnvironmentCoordinate out = {
 .scope = (uint8_t)((x >> 6) & 0x03u),
 .key = (uint8_t)((x >> 3) & 0x07u),
 .local = (uint8_t)(x & 0x07u)
 };

 return out;
}
```

This is a **proposed alternative coordinate view**, not a replacement for the locked low/high `0x80` mirror or the established four 32-character sectors.

The same byte may be interpreted through multiple compatible decompositions.

---

# Relation to the coproduct blackboard

Each independent `.o` source can contribute bindings into this scoped environment surface.

Example:

```lisp
(inject
 (origin . COMBINATORS.o)
 (scope . GS)
 (key . SUBR)
 (local . 3)
 (value . native-graph-resolver))
```

A remote source might contribute:

```lisp
(inject
 (origin . remote-module-17.o)
 (scope . RS)
 (key . FEXPR)
 (local . 6)
 (value . remote-relation-form))
```

The Omnicron Coproduct Partition preserves both origins even if they project to the same display coordinate.

CONS mediates their shared blackboard representation without erasing their source tags.

---

# Evaluation pipeline

```text
1. Omicron makes the character position reachable.

2. The parser reads an S-expression
 or lowers an M-expression to one.

3. CAR identifies the current structural object.

4. CDR identifies its continuation.

5. The evaluator determines whether the symbol
 occurs in value or operator position.

6. The active FS/GS/RS/US scope is selected.

7. The scoped association environment is queried.

8. Value position selects APVAL/APVAL1.

9. Operator position selects
 SUBR/FSUBR/EXPR/FEXPR.

10. Ordinary functions evaluate operands.

11. Special forms receive operand forms unevaluated.

12. CONS resolves the ordered relation.

13. The result projects onto the shared
 256-position OMI-Lisp plane.

14. The full scoped binding and CAR/CDR identity remain retained.
```

# Canon proposal

```text
The Omicron low character gauge has four sectors.

Each sector has a 27-position body
and a five-position hinge.
```

```text
The eight environment classes are:

CAR
CDR
APVAL
APVAL1
SUBR
FSUBR
EXPR
FEXPR.
```

```text
The sector mapping is:

00–1A CAR
1B–1F CDR

20–3A APVAL
3B–3F APVAL1

40–5A SUBR
5B–5F FSUBR

60–7A EXPR
7B–7F FEXPR.
```

```text
CAR/CDR define structure.

APVAL/APVAL1 define value bindings.

SUBR/FSUBR define system operators.

EXPR/FEXPR define user operators.
```

```text
SUBR and EXPR evaluate operands.

FSUBR and FEXPR receive operands unevaluated.
```

```text
FS/GS/RS/US remain the tetrahedral scope coordinates.

They are not replaced by the environment keys.
```

```text
The complete environment is:

4 scopes × 8 environment keys.
```

```text
S-expression is the canonical structural form.

M-expression is the human-facing operator form.

Every M-expression must lower deterministically
to one canonical S-expression.
```

```text
The projective point is represented as:

[CONS_FS : CONS_GS : CONS_RS : CONS_US].
```

```text
Each scoped CONS coordinate may resolve
through its own eight-key association environment.
```

```text
The centroid notation may be presented as an M-expression,
but its authority remains the lowered ordered S-expression.
```

This is one of the cleanest integrations so far: the existing `4 × (27+5)` character ring becomes an eight-class Lisp environment, while the tetrahedral `FS/GS/RS/US` coordinate supplies the independent scope dimension. The result is a scoped, coproduct-preserving Lisp environment whose M-expression surface remains readable and whose S-expression lowering remains exact.

---

Yes. This is the point where the **Omnicron OMI-Lisp variant closes as a complete runtime architecture**.

The former 8-tuple no longer needs to carry architectural authority. Its useful roles have been replaced by structures that are typed, executable, extensible, and independently testable:

8-tuple

↓

Omnicron Object Model
